The session's only clean parlay HIT (Volume Game Triple: Kawhi REB, SGA PTS, Mobley REB) succeeded by combining two different REB floor props with one iron-floor PTS prop across three independent games — this structure avoided 3PM legs entirely and avoided any player with a lineup risk flag, which was the decisive structural advantage over the four parlays that went PARTIAL or MISS.
parlay_reinforcements: The Volume Game Triple's REB + PTS structure across three independent games with no teammate overlap and no high-lineup-risk players was the cleanest combination on the card — pairing two iron-floor REB props (Kawhi T4, Mobley T2) with one iron-floor PTS prop (SGA T25) across different games and teams produced a comfortable clean sweep; this REB-REB-PTS trileg structure on independent games with 78%+ confidence floors should be a go-to parlay template.

The feeder-target correlation logic (SGA AST → Holmgren PTS within the same OKC-DEN game) was directionally sound — SGA did deliver 15 assists confirming the feeder role — but the correlated leg structure was rendered moot by Holmgren's DNP; when building intra-game correlated legs, both players must have confirmed active status at parlay lock time, not just at the pre-game injury check window, making same-game correlated parlays higher operational risk than cross-game independent structures.

reinforcements: Low-threshold PTS floors (T10 and T15) on high-usage primaries with 9/10 or 10/10 hit rates proved near-automatic: Randle T10 (hit 14), RJ Barrett T10 (hit 25), Duren T15 (hit 26), Brandon Miller T15 (hit 23) — these tiers are the system's most reliable prop class.

Multi-stat coverage on elite scorers paid off cleanly: Durant went 3/3 (PTS/REB/AST), Doncic went 3/3 (PTS/REB/3PM), and Derrick White went 3/3 (PTS/REB/AST) — stacking confirmed players at their floor tiers across prop types is high-value.

The 3PM T1 floor (OVER 1) strategy on high-volume shooters was 5/5 when restricted to consistent-tagged players with 9/10 or 10/10 rates (Doncic, Booker, Reaves, Cunningham, Knueppel) — this tier is a systematic edge.
REB picks at T4 and T6 with confirmed L10 floor-gate passage went 8/8 across all players — the 25th-percentile 3rd-lowest gate is functioning correctly as a filter against volatility.

The blowout-resilient PTS classification correctly protected Duren (+14.5 underdog, hit 26) and Cunningham (+14.5 underdog, hit 15 AST) — the primary scorer exemption from blowout secondary-skip is validated.
lessons: Jaylen Brown's iron-floor PTS tag failed badly (8 actual vs 20 pick) in just 15 minutes — the post-game context shows only 15 minutes played, suggesting an early exit or coach decision. The iron-floor tag and 8/10 hit rate did not protect against a minutes collapse. When min_floor is derived from full-game samples, a 15-minute game is structurally unforeseeable, but the miss reveals that iron-floor confidence capping at 80% may be appropriate even for consistent-tagged scorers when no minute-protection signal exists.

Brandon Ingram's PTS miss (9 actual vs 15 pick, VOLATILE tag, 7/10 hit rate) in 36 minutes confirms that the VOLATILE penalty at 70-72% confidence is the correct zone for him — the reasoning cited vs_soft=85% as a supportive signal, but 7/10 at T15 for a volatile SF is a marginal pick even in favorable matchups. The Analyst should treat 7/10 VOLATILE PTS picks at T15+ as borderline-skip candidates regardless of DvP rating.

RJ Barrett's AST miss (1 actual vs 2 pick, iron-floor tag, 8/10 hit rate) in 32 minutes with a down trend is a low-tier variance miss — the player logged 32 minutes and 25 points but only 1 assist. The down trend was correctly noted but the iron-floor tag suppressed the VOLATILE penalty. Iron-floor classification should not override a down-trend + declining AST pattern for wing scorers; the trend signal deserved more weight.

Julius Randle's AST miss (2 actual vs 4 pick, 7/10 hit rate, VOLATILE) confirms that T4 AST picks for non-primary PGs remain risky at 70% confidence — the reasoning correctly flagged VOLATILE -5% bringing confidence to 70%, and the pick still missed. The system is right to be cautious here; this classification validates the 70% floor threshold as a borderline zone.

Cooper Flagg's PTS miss (14 actual vs 15 pick, iron-floor tag, 7/10 rate) with only 1 point below threshold illustrates that T15 iron-floor picks at exactly 70% confidence with FG_COLD:-18% present are genuinely marginal. The FG_COLD signal was logged as 'informational only' but a -18% shooting suppression on a 15-point threshold is a material risk that should trigger a tier step-down to T10.

LaMelo Ball's PTS miss (14 actual vs 15 pick, 9/10 rate, 22 minutes played) — the post-game context shows only 22 minutes, well below his typical load. A min_floor=22.9 below 24 triggered a confidence cap but did not trigger a tier step-down. The minutes-floor cap rule should be evaluated for whether it's doing enough: a min_floor below 24 should perhaps force a mandatory tier step-down to T10 rather than just capping confidence.
recommendations: Institute a mandatory tier step-down for PTS picks where min_floor < 24 AND the pick tier is T15 or higher: LaMelo Ball (min_floor=22.9, T15 missed by 1) and Flagg (FG_COLD -18%, T15 missed by 1) both fell just below threshold — dropping to T10 in these cases costs little upside and eliminates the near-miss exposure. Apply this as a rule: min_floor < 24 on a T15 PTS pick → step to T10.

Treat FG_COLD >= -15% as a hard tier-reduction signal, not informational: Flagg's FG_COLD was -18% and his PTS pick was T15 at 70% confidence. The informational-only designation allowed an analytically weak pick to proceed. Recommendation: FG_COLD <= -15% should force a one-tier step-down on PTS picks, matching the existing FG_MARGIN_THIN rule logic.

For wing scorers (SG/SF) with a down trend on AST, do not apply iron-floor classification to suppress the VOLATILE penalty: Barrett's AST miss (1 actual, down trend, 32 minutes, 25 points scored) shows that high scoring output does not guarantee assist accumulation. The down trend on AST for wings should trigger a VOLATILE deduction even when the iron-floor tag is present — iron-floor should protect the tier pick, not the confidence level.

Add a minutes-exit risk modifier for players whose L10 log contains any game with fewer than 20 minutes played in a non-DNP context: Brown played only 15 minutes despite no pre-game flag. If any L10 game shows <20 active minutes (not a DNP), apply a -5% confidence penalty and note as MINUTES_RISK. This is a distinct signal from min_floor and would have reduced Brown's PTS confidence below the 80% assigned.

For 3PM picks classified as VOLATILE with a tough DvP, maintain the existing approach of not applying additional tough-DvP penalties per the 3PM noise rule — the Edwards 3PM miss (1 actual, 7/10 rate, tough DvP) is a clean variance outcome on a 70% confidence pick. The rule is correct; the Edwards miss is within expected 30% failure rate and does not warrant rule changes.

miss_details.root_cause: 7/10 hit rate with VOLATILE -5% landing at 70% confidence — this is the system's defined margin zone. The analyst correctly applied the T3+ ceiling rule (7/10 AND up trend), noted tough DvP with no additional penalty per 3PM noise rule, and landed at 70%. Edwards logged 31 minutes and had an off shooting night (1 3PM). At 70% confidence, a 30% miss rate is expected; this is a clean variance outcome within the model's stated uncertainty. The tier walk correctly identified T4 as blocked and stepped to T3, and the pick was appropriately marginal. No rule or signal failure.

Brown logged only 15 minutes per post-game context — an early exit or coach-managed minutes restriction that was not predictable from any pre-game signal (injury_status_at_check = NOT_LISTED, no voiding). The iron-floor tag and 8/10 hit rate (with min_floor=26.1 >= 24) all indicated a sound pick. The reasoning cited 'mid SA defense won't suppress Brown's volume' which was correct in isolation, but the miss was entirely driven by a minutes collapse below any floor the model tracks. This is a model_gap_signal: NBAgent has no mechanism to detect in-game minutes management decisions by coaching staff that occur without a pre-game injury flag. The post-game context confirms 15 minutes played with no injury confirmation, suggesting a load-management or game-script decision. Note: this was the parlay-breaking leg in 'Iron Floor Quad.'

Barrett logged 32 minutes and scored 25 points — fully active with substantial usage — but accumulated only 1 assist against a 2-pick threshold. The iron-floor tag and 8/10 hit rate at T2 supported the pick, and the down trend was correctly acknowledged with a conservative 78% confidence cap. The analyst's reasoning ('reliable secondary playmaker accumulating assists at T2') was reasonable for a wing scorer. However, in games where Barrett concentrates on scoring (25 PTS), assist accumulation can collapse to near-zero — this is within the 22% miss rate implied by 78% confidence. Classified as variance. Note: the down trend on AST was a legitimate caution signal; the iron-floor tag appropriately prevented a VOLATILE deduction but the down trend deserved slightly more weight in the confidence calculation.

Randle logged 26 minutes and scored 14 points but recorded only 2 assists vs the 4-pick threshold. The analyst correctly applied VOLATILE -5% to reach 70% confidence and noted the borderline hard-gate condition (PF position, AST avg ~4.7, soft DvP). The 7/10 hit rate in a VOLATILE context at 70% confidence is the system's defined edge zone — a 30% miss rate is expected. The reasoning ('playmaking role confirmed with primary ball-handlers available') is correct in principle but the soft DvP context did not translate to assist volume in this specific game. Clean variance at the model's stated 70% confidence floor.

Ball logged only 22 minutes — well below his typical load — and scored 14, missing by exactly 1 point. The pick had min_floor=22.9 < 24, which correctly triggered a confidence cap, but the rule only capped confidence rather than forcing a tier step-down. With min_floor below 24 and a T15 PTS pick, the existing rule structure was insufficient: Ball's reduced minutes (22 logged) were the proximate cause of missing the threshold. A min_floor < 24 should mandate a tier step-down to T10 on PTS picks of T15 or higher — the signal existed in the quant data at pick time (min_floor=22.9 was computed and present) but the rule only adjusted confidence, not the tier. This is a model_gap_rule miss: the signal was available; the rule didn't fully act on it.

Flagg logged 32 minutes and scored 14 — missing by exactly 1 point with a FG_COLD:-18% signal logged as 'informational only.' The iron-floor tag and 7/10 hit rate placed this at the system's minimum viable threshold (70%), but the FG_COLD -18% signal was explicitly present in the quant data at pick time and not acted upon as a tier modifier. The analyst's reasoning correctly identified Flagg as DAL's primary scorer with boosted usage, but the FG_COLD signal was material: a shooter 18% below baseline shooting efficiency on a T15 PTS pick is a meaningful suppression risk. The rule treating FG_COLD as informational-only allowed this marginal pick to proceed at T15 when T10 would have been the appropriate tier. Model_gap_rule: the FG_COLD signal existed, the rule did not use it to step down the tier.

Ingram logged 36 minutes — fully active — but scored only 9 points, well below the 15-pick threshold. The VOLATILE tag and 7/10 hit rate correctly placed this at 72% confidence, the system's acknowledged margin zone for volatile scorers. The analyst's reasoning cited vs_soft=85% as supportive, which is a real signal, but the 7/10 rate combined with VOLATILE is the system's weakest qualifying combination for a T15 PTS pick. At 72% confidence, a 28% miss rate is expected, and Ingram's volatile shooting profile means misses can be significant in magnitude (not near-misses). The pick was technically sound per system rules but borderline — Ingram's VOLATILE + 7/10 + T15 profile is a candidate for future skip-list consideration without additional confluence signals.

no_data_details.no_data_explanation: LeBron James was confirmed OUT pre-game (injury_status_at_check = OUT at pick time) and logged 0 minutes per post-game context; the pick carried voided=true with void_reason='player OUT per injury report,' indicating the void was correctly recorded but the pick still appears in the graded set — standard workflow gap where OUT status was confirmed and voiding was applied.

Same as PTS pick: LeBron was OUT pre-game (injury_status_at_check = OUT), logged 0 minutes, voided=true — both LeBron picks were correctly voided but surface as NO_DATA due to the DNP, consistent with the workflow gap classification.

Payton Pritchard was confirmed OUT pre-game (injury_status_at_check = OUT), logged 0 minutes per post-game context, and the pick carries voided=true with void_reason='player OUT per injury report' — correctly voided; no analytical failure.

Same as REB pick: Pritchard was OUT pre-game (injury_status_at_check = OUT), logged 0 minutes, voided=true — both Pritchard picks were correctly voided and are classified as workflow gaps consistent with the pre-game OUT designation.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 1/2 parlay session — 'Scorers Sweep' hit cleanly on all four legs while 'Iron Floor Quad' was broken by Jaylen Brown's unforeseeable 15-minute exit, making Brown the single point of failure on an otherwise 3/4 card.
parlay_lessons: The 'Iron Floor Quad' miss exposes a structural concentration risk in parlays that include any player with min_floor < 24 or an undocumented minutes-management history: Brown's 15-minute exit was not predictable from any pre-game signal, but parlays cannot absorb even one minutes-collapse leg — future parlays should explicitly screen out any leg where min_floor < 24 or where the player has shown any in-season load-management pattern, regardless of iron-floor classification.

Including a 20-point PTS threshold leg (Brown OVER 20) in a 4-leg parlay alongside T10 floor legs (Barrett OVER 10, Randle OVER 10) creates asymmetric risk: if the high-threshold leg is the anchor of the parlay narrative ('Brown's up-trend vs soft SA'), the parlay's structural integrity depends on a leg that is materially harder to hit than its partners. The parlay agent should cap the highest single-leg threshold in any multi-leg card to no more than 1.5x the median threshold of all legs (here, median was 12.5, so max should be ~19 — Brown at 20 was marginally above this).
parlay_reinforcements: The 'Scorers Sweep' structure — stacking primary scorer PTS legs from independent games where all players have soft or mid DvP and 8/10+ hit rates at their selected tier — produced a clean 4/4 sweep (Miller 23, Durant 29, Giannis 22, Randle 14 all cleared). This multi-game independent scorer stack with matched floor thresholds (T15/T20/T20/T10) and no cross-game correlations is the system's most validated parlay template.

Low-threshold anchor legs (T10 PTS at 10/10 hit rate) serve as structural insurance in multi-leg parlays: Randle OVER 10 (hit 14) and Barrett OVER 10 (hit 25) both cleared comfortably and appeared in both parlays. These legs should always be included when available as a near-certain base in any parlay construction, as they reduce the probability of a full miss even when a higher-threshold leg underperforms.

reinforcements: Low-threshold PTS floors (T10-T15) on volume scorers with 9-10/10 hit rates proved nearly automatic: Harden 30, Edwards 36, Kawhi 45, Banchero 25, LaMelo 30, Knueppel 24 all cleared comfortably — the tier-walk step-down discipline is working precisely as designed.

AST props at conservative thresholds (T2-T6) went a perfect 10/10, confirming that pairing a soft-to-mid AST DvP gate with a player's documented iron floor or 10/10 hit rate is a high-confidence structure regardless of trend direction.

3PM props at T1 with 9-10/10 hit rates swept 6/6 even through a trend=down case (Bane) and a trend=down with iron_floor override (Mitchell) — the step-down-to-T1 rule on down-trending shooters is well-calibrated.

The BLOWOUT_RISK -10% confidence cap (Knueppel, LaMelo, both at 74%) correctly restrained overconfidence on a 12.5-point spread game; both players still hit comfortably, validating that the blowout_games resilience counter (93-95%) can support including these picks at reduced confidence rather than skipping them.

The tough DvP hard gate on AST (Randle, Kawhi) correctly stepped down from T4 to T2 in both cases, producing clean 10/10 floors — the hard gate rule on position-specific tough DvP AST is preventing overreach and should be maintained.
lessons: Blowout game script is a scoring suppressor even for elite centers: Jokic missed PTS 20 with 16 actual in a 36-point DEN blowout win — his triple-double (13 AST, 12 REB) confirms he played but scored less as the game became garbage time. The reasoning noted 'spread=6.5 competitive enough to protect minutes' but a 36-point final margin was not captured pre-game; the system needs a real-time blowout game script adjustment, not just a pre-game spread gate.

Kevin Durant's PTS miss (11 actual vs. 20 threshold, 26 minutes played) on a 9/10 hit rate is the most concerning miss: the reasoning cited '8-game hit streak' and 'min_floor=32.5 above 24-min threshold' but he logged only 26 minutes — suggesting either a load management decision or an early exit that was not injury-classified. With no post-game news confirming injury and no pre-game OUT flag, the 6-minute shortfall from the stated min_floor is unexplained and represents a model gap around minutes floor reliability in away games against specific opponents.

Sengun REB missed badly (2 actual vs. 6 threshold) despite a 10/10 hit rate, 80% confidence, and a tough DvP. With 24 minutes played, he was active and contributing. A single catastrophic REB game (2) against a tough opponent on a B2B road game suggests that the 10/10 hit rate at T6 was masking a tail risk: the L10 3rd-lowest REB=6 floor cited in reasoning was exactly at the threshold, leaving zero cushion. When the 3rd-lowest L10 value equals the pick threshold exactly, there is no protective buffer — one bad game breaks the streak.
recommendations: Implement a live game script adjustment for PTS props on centers and high-usage forwards: when the in-game spread expands beyond 20 points, apply a scoring suppression flag. The Jokic miss (16 PTS in a 36-point blowout) and the reasoning's reliance on pre-game spread (6.5) illustrate that pre-game spread alone is insufficient — build a second-half load management discount into the model for marquee big men in non-competitive games.

Add a minutes floor reliability check for away games: Durant's stated min_floor of 32.5 was not met (26 min actual). When a player's away B2B minutes history shows variance >5 min from the stated floor, downgrade confidence by 5% or flag the pick for manual review. The 'min_floor' field should be sourced from away-game-only splits, not overall averages, to avoid overestimating floor reliability on road legs.

Tighten the REB floor gate: when the L10 3rd-lowest value equals the pick threshold exactly (as with Sengun REB=6 gating at T6), require a 1-unit cushion — i.e., the 3rd-lowest L10 value must be strictly greater than the pick threshold, not equal to it. An exact match provides no variance buffer and a single outlier game causes a miss. Apply this rule to all prop types.

For Jokic PTS specifically: given his triple-double profile and Denver's tendency to run up scores against weak opponents, consider a conditional rule that steps down Jokic PTS by one tier (T25→T20→skip) when the opponent is ranked bottom-5 defensively AND DEN is a home favorite by 6+. The blowout risk is structurally higher for Jokic than for guards because his scoring is more discretionary in garbage time.

Validate Kevin Durant's miss classification next session with additional data: his 26-minute appearance against a stated min_floor of 32.5 is either a load management decision (model_gap_signal — no quant field captures coach rest decisions mid-game) or an undisclosed minor injury exit. If confirmed as load management, add a 'star load management away B2B' penalty flag for veterans on road second nights — even those with 100% B2B resilience ratings.

miss_details.root_cause: Sengun played 24 minutes and recorded stats (PTS=10 confirmed HIT), so this is not a DNP or injury exit — he was active. The tier_walk shows REB: 8→5/10 | 6→10/10✓, with the 10/10 hit rate at T6 fully supporting the pick. The reasoning cited 'L10 3rd-lowest REB=6 passes 25th-pct gate at T6' — but this is precisely the vulnerability: the floor was set exactly at the threshold with zero cushion. A 2-rebound game on 24 minutes in a road B2B against a tough DEN opponent represents a tail-end outlier event on an otherwise near-perfect recent track record. No pre-game signal existed to flag this; the pick was structurally sound. However, the exact-threshold floor (3rd-lowest L10 = pick value) is a pattern worth tightening in the selection rule. Classified as variance: the 10/10 hit rate fully supported the pick; one catastrophic outlier game in 10 is within the expected variance envelope for this threshold.

Durant played 26 minutes and recorded supporting stats (REB=4 HIT, AST=5 HIT), confirming he was active and not injured. The tier_walk shows PTS: 25→4/10 | 20→9/10✓, and the reasoning cited a 9/10 hit rate, an 8-game streak, and a min_floor of 32.5 above the 24-minute threshold. The 6-minute shortfall from the stated min_floor (26 actual vs. 32.5 projected) is the proximate cause of the scoring miss — 11 points in 26 minutes is plausible production at that minutes level. No pre-game OUT or DOUBTFUL flag existed; injury_status_at_check = NOT_LISTED. The system had no mechanism to anticipate a mid-game minutes restriction that was not injury-driven. This is a model_gap_signal: NBAgent does not currently compute a 'coach-imposed minutes cap on away B2B for veteran stars' signal, and no quant field in the pick object would have flagged this. The stated min_floor reliability in away games and the absence of a load management probability field are the structural gaps.

Jokic played 30 minutes and posted a triple-double (13 AST HIT, 12 REB implied by the AST HIT and web recap), confirming full activity. The post-game recap confirms: 'Denver's 129-93 blowout win over Houston... lopsided nature of the game likely limited his scoring opportunities in the second half.' The tier_walk shows PTS: 25→5/10 | 20→10/10✓, and the reasoning cited 'spread=6.5 competitive enough to protect minutes.' However, the pre-game spread was 6.5 in DEN's favor — a meaningful home favorite projection — and the final margin was 36 points. The reasoning's reliance on a 6.5-point spread as a 'competitive' threshold was too permissive for a center whose scoring is discretionary in blowout conditions. A signal existed in the pick object (spread=6.5, DEN home) that a tighter rule could have caught: centers and primary big men on home favorites by 6+ against bottom-tier offenses (HOU mid DvP) carry elevated blowout scoring suppression risk. The model has a BLOWOUT_RISK penalty for large spreads (≥12) but not for moderate spreads that still produce lopsided games. This is a model_gap_rule: the signal (moderate spread on a dominant home center) existed in context but the prompt rule's blowout penalty threshold was not tuned to catch this scenario.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 2/2 sweep — both multi-game parlays anchored by low-threshold PTS iron floors across independent games, with every leg clearing by a wide margin.
parlay_lessons: Both parlays shared the same two anchor legs (RJ Barrett PTS 10, Alperen Sengun PTS 10), creating hidden single-game concentration risk: if Sengun had missed his PTS prop (he hit exactly at threshold with 10.0), both parlays would have busted simultaneously. The Parlay Agent should track anchor leg overlap across same-session parlays and flag when the same leg appears in multiple cards — even on a winning day, this structural concentration means a single player's bad game wipes out the entire parlay card, not just one bet.

The Sengun PTS leg hit exactly at threshold (actual=10.0, pick=10) — a razor-thin margin that anchored both parlays. Given Sengun's same-game REB miss (2 actual) and reduced minutes (24), his overall output was suppressed in this game. Parlays using a player as an anchor should require their floor prop to have at least a 1-unit buffer above threshold in the L10 3rd-lowest, not just an exact-match floor, to reduce the risk of a parlay-busting near-miss.
parlay_reinforcements: The multi-game independent-scorer structure (three players from three different games, each with 9-10/10 hit rates at conservative PTS thresholds) is the optimal parlay architecture: no shared game script risk, no correlated blowout exposure, and each leg independently justified. This structure produced 6/6 legs hit across both parlays and should be the primary template for future multi-game cards.

Stacking a trending scorer (Banchero, up trend, 10/10, soft DvP) with two iron-floor anchors (Barrett T10, Sengun T10) is a high-EV parlay structure: the trending scorer provides the upside asymmetry (Banchero hit 25 vs. 15 threshold) while the anchors provide near-certain floor coverage. This 'upside leg + two floors' construction is worth formalizing as a named parlay template.

reinforcements: Low-threshold AST props (T2/T4) on primary ball-handlers delivered a 92.9% hit rate — the single best prop type of the session. Picks like LeBron AST T4 (7 actual), SGA AST T4 (9 actual), and Reaves AST T2 (7 actual) all cleared by wide margins. The conservative threshold strategy on AST is working precisely as designed.

3PM T1 props on volume shooters in favorable matchups hit 7 of 8 qualifying picks (excluding voided White). Doncic T3 (9 actual), Booker T1 (4 actual), Murray T1 (2 actual), and Reaves T1 (4 actual) all cleared. The T1 floor combined with high pace/soft DvP matchups provides a robust structural edge.

The BLOWOUT_RISK + spread≥12 confidence cap (hard cap at 74%) correctly constrained picks in LAL vs CHI (spread ~12) without blocking valid selections. Players like Luka (51 PTS), Reaves (30 PTS), and LeBron (18 PTS) all hit comfortably, validating that the cap managed overconfidence without generating false negatives.

Iron_floor picks in competitive games hit 100% of the time this session — Chet Holmgren PTS T10 (14 actual), Cooper Flagg REB T6 (6 actual, exact threshold = HIT), Jaylen Brown AST T2 (7 actual). The iron_floor tag is providing reliable signal when game script is not adversarial.

Applying mandatory tier step-downs triggered by min_floor < 24 (e.g. Jaylen Brown PTS T20→T15, Jalen Duren PTS T15→T10) produced hits in both cases, confirming the rule's conservative function is correct — the step-down protected against marginal tiers without sacrificing the pick entirely.
lessons: Bam Adebayo 3PM T1 missed (0 actual vs. pick 1): The 'up trend' and 9/10 hit rate created overconfidence in a low-frequency event prop for a center. At T1 3PM for a non-perimeter player, a single 0-for-3PM game is always in-range variance, but the signal that Adebayo is a C with relatively low 3PM volume makes this structurally fragile regardless of recent trend. The trend=up tag on 3PM for big men needs additional scrutiny.

Cade Cunningham 3PM T2 missed (0 actual vs. pick 2): The tier_walk explicitly noted VOLATILE-5% would drop confidence to 65% and flagged a SKIP — yet the pick appeared in the graded set at 70% confidence. This is a possible workflow enforcement gap: if VOLATILE-5% is correctly reducing T2 3PM to 65% below the 70% floor, the pick should have been suppressed entirely before publication.

Desmond Bane REB T2 missed (1 actual vs. pick 2): The 25th-pct gate showed 3rd-lowest L10 REB = 3 > 2, which appeared safe. However, in a game that finished ORL +5 (CLOSE), Bane played 42 minutes — a usage pattern suggesting heavy offensive load but limited rebounding opportunity at the wing position. The REB miss here reflects that wing/guard REB T2 props are vulnerable to game-flow effects that push the player toward ball-handling rather than crashing the glass. The 9/10 overall rate may overstate reliability in close games where wings operate in transition-heavy modes.

Jalen Duren PTS T15 missed at 14 actual (one point below threshold): After the mandatory min_floor step T15→T10 was applied in the tier_walk, the pick was published at T10 per the reasoning — yet the graded pick_value shows 15, not 10. This is a pick recording inconsistency: the tier_walk logic stepped down to T10 but the published pick_value remained T15. The actual result of 14 would have been a HIT at T10. This is a model_gap_rule failure in pick publication — the stepped-down tier must be reflected in the final pick_value field.
recommendations: Enforce a categorical rule: for C and PF players (Adebayo, Jokic, Duren, Holmgren, etc.), 3PM T1 picks require a minimum of 7/10 hit rate AND the player's 3PM average across L10 must exceed 0.8 per game. A trend=up tag alone is insufficient to qualify a low-volume 3PM prop for a big man — add an explicit L10 3PM average floor gate before publishing these picks.

Fix the tier step-down publication pipeline: when a mandatory step-down is applied (e.g. min_floor < 24 triggers T15→T10), the final pick_value field in the published pick must reflect the stepped-down tier (T10 = pick_value: 10), not the original evaluated tier. The Duren PTS case (tier_walk said T10, pick_value published as 15, actual was 14) would have been a HIT at T10. Implement a post-walk pick_value reconciliation check before publication.

Add a volatility flag specifically for wing/guard REB T2 props in CLOSE games (spread_abs < 6): when a wing player (SG/SF/PF hybrid) has a REB T2 pick and the pre-game spread is under 6 points, apply an additional -5% confidence deduction. Close games push wings into playmaking roles rather than rebounding, and the Bane REB miss (1 actual, ORL +5 final) illustrates this dynamic. The 9/10 overall rate masks game-script fragility at this tier.

Establish a VOLATILE + SKIP enforcement audit: any pick where the tier_walk explicitly concludes 'SKIP' due to VOLATILE-5% dropping below 70% must not appear in the published graded picks above 70% confidence. The Cunningham 3PM T2 pick showed 70% confidence in the graded record despite the tier_walk documenting a SKIP decision. Add a post-processing validation step that cross-checks tier_walk SKIP conclusions against published confidence values and suppresses any pick where SKIP was reached.

For 3PM props on players with trend=down, apply the existing hard skip rule consistently even at T1 — the Payton Pritchard 3PM T1 pick was internally flagged as hard-skip (trend=down + tough DvP) but published at 74% and hit. While this was a beneficial outcome, the inconsistency between the tier_walk logic and the published pick creates calibration noise. Either enforce the hard skip universally or document explicitly when iron_floor overrides the skip, so the rule is deterministic rather than ad hoc.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0 — Game context: MIA vs MIL, MIA won by 7 (CLOSE). No blowout effect. STEP 1 — Adebayo played 35 minutes with non-zero output in other stats (REB hit 8.0 same game), confirming active participation. STEP 2 — injury_status NOT_LISTED, voided=false; no injury event. Classification: variance. STEP 3 — Original reasoning cited 85% overall at T1, up trend, MIL ranked 19th defensively. This was sound for a floor prop, but 3PM T1 for a center is inherently low-frequency — a single 0-for-3 shooting night is a natural occurrence even at 85% historical rate. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 9/10 (90%) at pick time with trend=up; this is a legitimate variance miss on a high-confidence pick, not a systemic error. STEP 5 — Tier walk showed T1 was the only qualifying tier after T2 fell below 70%; the pick was at the lowest viable tier. STEP 6 — No amendment object present. The structural concern is that 3PM props for C-position players have inherently higher single-game variance than PTS/REB props — the overall 85% rate may overstate game-to-game reliability at T1 for big men.

STEP 0 — Game context: DET vs PHI, DET won by 22 (BLOWOUT). DET was the winning side; in a blowout win, stars typically accumulate volume stats in the first three quarters and may have reduced 4th quarter minutes. However, Cunningham played only 28 minutes — consistent with a blowout where starters rest late. STEP 1 — Cunningham recorded 13 AST in this game (same session), confirming he played. 0 3PM in 28 minutes is a legitimate zero. STEP 2 — injury_status NOT_LISTED, voided=false. STEP 3 — Original reasoning explicitly documented: 'VOLATILE-5% drops below 70%; skipping — see skip record.' The tier_walk concluded SKIP at 65% confidence. Yet this pick appears in the graded set at 70% confidence and recorded as a MISS. This is a model_gap_rule failure: the analyst correctly identified the SKIP condition internally but the pick was published anyway. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 7/10 at T2 3PM; VOLATILE-5% should have hard-capped this below the 70% publication floor. STEP 5 — Tier walk confirms only T2 qualified at 7/10 before VOLATILE deduction, which then dropped it to 65%. STEP 6 — No amendment present. Root cause: the VOLATILE-5% rule reduced this below the 70% publication floor but the enforcement mechanism failed to suppress publication. The blowout game script (28 minutes, starters rested in Q4) further compressed the opportunity for 3PM accumulation, amplifying what should already have been a skipped pick.

Workflow gap: Derrick White was confirmed OUT pre-game (injury_status_at_check = OUT, voided = true per the pick object). The pick was correctly voided by the system. This is not an analytical error — the pick was properly flagged and marked voided=true. The 0.0 actual value reflects a DNP. No lesson for the Analyst's selection logic applies here.
Workflow gap: Derrick White was confirmed OUT pre-game (injury_status_at_check = OUT, voided = true). All White picks were correctly voided. No analytical error applies.
Workflow gap: Derrick White was confirmed OUT pre-game (injury_status_at_check = OUT, voided = true). All White picks were correctly voided. No analytical error applies.
Workflow gap: Derrick White was confirmed OUT pre-game (injury_status_at_check = OUT, voided = true). All White picks were correctly voided. No analytical error applies.

STEP 0 — Game context: ORL vs WSH, ORL won by 5 (CLOSE). No blowout effect. CLOSE game script is neutral context. STEP 1 — Bane played 42 minutes with 6 AST (same session hit) and other stats, confirming full active participation. 1 REB in 42 minutes is the miss. STEP 2 — injury_status NOT_LISTED, voided=false. No injury. Classification: variance. STEP 3 — Original reasoning cited 85% overall T2 REB, B2B=89%, and 3rd-lowest L10 REB=3>2 clearing the 25th-pct gate. This was a structurally sound pick. The reasoning correctly noted WSH's 32nd-ranked defense as favorable context. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 9/10 (90% at T2); miss margin was -1 (1 actual vs. 2 needed). This is a near-miss on a high-confidence prop. STEP 5 — Tier walk confirms T2 was appropriately selected after T4 failed at 5/10; the 25th-pct gate showed 3rd-lowest=3>2 providing a cushion that was not realized tonight. STEP 6 — No amendment present. At 42 minutes in a close game, Bane operated primarily as a playmaker/scorer (6 AST, meaningful scoring), leaving minimal rebounding opportunity for a wing player. This is within normal variance for a guard/wing REB T2 prop even with strong historical hit rates.

STEP 0 — Game context: DET vs PHI, DET won by 22 (BLOWOUT). DET was the winning side; Duren played only 15 minutes per post-game context, indicating he was pulled early in a blowout — a common outcome when the winning team rests starters/rotation players in Q4 of a dominant victory. STEP 1 — Duren played 15 minutes and scored 14 PTS, confirming participation but compressed minutes. STEP 2 — injury_status NOT_LISTED, voided=false. No injury. STEP 3 — Original reasoning documented that min_floor=21.9<24 triggers mandatory step T15→T10, meaning the published pick should have been T10 (pick_value=10), not T15. At T10, Duren's 14 PTS would be a HIT. The tier_walk explicitly concluded 'Apply step to T10' but the published pick_value remained 15. This is a pick recording/publication error — the stepped-down tier was not reflected in the final pick_value. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 7/10 at T15; after the mandatory step the relevant tier was T10 which would have a higher hit rate. STEP 5 — Tier_walk shows the correct step-down logic was computed (T15→T10) but the pick_value field was not updated to match. This is a tier_skip_error in the publication pipeline — the analyst identified the correct step but the output was not reconciled. STEP 6 — No amendment present. Additionally, the BLOWOUT game (22-point DET win) compressing Duren's minutes to 15 is consistent with starters being rested in Q4 of a blowout, further supporting that the T15 threshold was too aggressive for this game script even before the min_floor rule. At pick_value=10 the result would have been a HIT; the miss is entirely attributable to a publication reconciliation failure.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 2/4 parlay session — both misses were caused by the single Derrick White DNP, which torpedoed two parlays simultaneously, while the two hits were driven by independent multi-game low-threshold legs that had no shared game-script exposure.
parlay_lessons: A single player appearing as an anchor leg in multiple parlays creates correlated parlay failure risk even when the games are nominally independent: Derrick White's DNP killed both 'Thursday Floor Sweep' and 'Bam-White Cross-Game Lock' simultaneously, turning one availability event into a 2-parlay loss. Cap any single player to one parlay leg per session to prevent this concentration failure.

Parlays that include a player with QUESTIONABLE or elevated injury_status_at_check should apply a structural downgrade — even if the pick itself clears confidence thresholds, the parlay as a unit becomes fragile. The pre-game OUT status on White was known at pick time (injury_status_at_check = OUT), meaning both parlays containing him should have been voided or reconstructed before lock. Implement a parlay-level sweep that voids any parlay containing a leg where the underlying pick was subsequently voided.

The 'Bam-White Cross-Game Lock' parlay (parlay_04) lost 2 of 3 legs — White DNP and Bane REB miss — leaving only the Adebayo REB hit. The label 'Cross-Game Lock' implied high confidence across independent games, but combining an injury-risk scorer with a wing REB low-threshold prop created two independent fragility points. Parlay construction should favor legs from the same prop-type strength category (AST and 3PM T1 are performing above 85%+) rather than mixing PTS anchor with REB floor props that have different variance profiles.
parlay_reinforcements: The 'Multi-Game Volume Triple' and 'Independent Scorers Sweeper' both hit cleanly by anchoring on ultra-low AST thresholds (T2 at 2 assists) combined with a high-confidence PTS or scoring prop. The AST T2 leg type — characterized by 9/10 hit rate, iron_floor tags, and soft DvP — is the most structurally reliable parlay building block in the current system, providing near-certain floor coverage while the anchor scorer leg provides upside.

Cross-game parlays with zero lineup overlap and no shared game-script exposure (Reaves/LAL, Giannis/MIL, SGA/OKC — three entirely different games) demonstrated clean independence: when one leg outperforms dramatically (Giannis 31 vs. 20 needed, SGA 35 vs. 25 needed), the cushion absorbs any single-leg near-miss. Prioritize parlays where at least two legs are from games with spread_abs < 10 to minimize blowout game-script interference across the entire ticket.

reinforcements: Iron-floor AST T2 legs (pick_value=2) across independent games posted a near-perfect rate: Kawhi Leonard, Brandon Ingram, Anthony Edwards, Amen Thompson, and Donovan Mitchell all cleared 2 AST — these ultra-conservative thresholds continue to be the system's most reliable single-leg building block.

The CLE blowout (won by 33) did not compromise Harden's PTS, REB, AST, or 3PM floors — the blowout cap mechanic at 74% correctly priced in uncertainty while still qualifying all four legs, all of which hit. Star usage on the winning side in blowouts is durable at conservative tiers.

RJ Barrett's T10 PTS pick (95% consistent tag, up trend, mid DvP) produced 22 points — the combination of consistent tag + up trend + conservative tier proved accurate even against a non-soft defensive opponent, validating that the consistent tag can override mid-DvP concerns at deeply conservative thresholds.

Cade Cunningham's AST T8 pick, taken with VOLATILE -5% and BLOWOUT_RISK cap to 74%, still hit with 15 assists against MEM (DET won by 16). The risk-adjusted confidence was appropriate — the pick qualified on adjusted merit and delivered well above threshold.

Amen Thompson's VOLATILE -5% adjustment landing the PTS pick at exactly 70% (the floor) still hit with 23 points, confirming that the volatility penalty is a calibration tool, not a disqualifier — picks that survive the VOLATILE deduction to reach exactly 70% remain viable when soft DvP and up trend both confirm.
lessons: Alperen Sengun's DNP (0 min, 0 PTS, 0 AST) was not flagged pre-game despite being NOT_LISTED at injury check — both picks (PTS and AST) missed with actual_value=0.0. The workflow failed to void these picks when the player did not play. The injury_status_at_check of NOT_LISTED masked a real availability risk. A same-day afternoon lineup re-check closer to tip-off should be mandatory for any player with a recent game-log gap or load management history; DNP inference at 0 minutes is too late.

Donovan Mitchell's 3PM miss (actual=1, pick=2) occurred in a 33-point CLE blowout where Mitchell played only 29 minutes — the step-down from T2 to T1 due to trend=down and BLOWOUT -10% still produced a 75% confidence pick that missed. In blowout scenarios on the winning side, shooting volume for secondary prop types (3PM) is particularly vulnerable to early benching even at conservative tiers; the BLOWOUT_RISK deduction may need a stricter threshold for 3PM specifically on the winning team when spread_abs ≥ 12.

Julius Randle's AST T2 miss (actual=1, pick=2) against GS's tough AST DvP is partially explained by the MIN AST suppressor watch item (Chris Finch's switching scheme) interacting with GS's own defensive structure — Randle played only 27 minutes and recorded just 1 assist. The system correctly blocked T4 due to tough AST DvP and the PF position gate, but still ran T2 at 80%. The MIN AST suppressor watch item should reduce T2 AST confidence by at least 5pp when both a tough AST DvP and the MIN suppressor flag co-occur on a non-primary ball-handler.
recommendations: Implement a mandatory same-day final lineup confirmation step (within 60-90 minutes of tip-off) for any player whose injury_status_at_check is NOT_LISTED but who has missed or been limited in any of the prior 3 games. Sengun's dual-miss DNP (0 PTS, 0 AST, 0 min) was entirely preventable with a later availability check.

For 3PM picks on the winning side of games with spread_abs ≥ 12, apply an additional -5pp deduction beyond the standard BLOWOUT -10% (total -15pp from base), or hard-cap 3PM confidence at 70% in these scenarios. Mitchell's 3PM miss shows that shooting volume compresses faster than PTS/AST in blowout wins due to early benching and reduced fourth-quarter minutes.

When the MIN AST suppressor watch item is active AND the opponent also carries a tough AST DvP rating, apply a minimum -10pp adjustment to ALL AST tiers (including T2) for non-primary ball-handlers. Randle's T2 AST miss (1 actual vs. 2 pick) was the product of two compounding suppressor signals that the system treated as independent — they are not.

Alperen Sengun's two picks (PTS and AST) were included in two separate parlays ('Iron Floor Trio' and 'Scottie-Mitchell Cross-Game Lock'), causing both parlays to miss on a single DNP event. Enforce a rule: no single player may appear in more than one parlay on the same day. Player concentration across parlays converts a single availability event into a multi-parlay loss.

The 3PM step-down rule (trend=down → mandatory T2→T1 step) combined with iron_floor did not prevent Mitchell's 3PM miss. Consider adding a hard skip rule for 3PM when both trend=down AND blowout_risk=True are simultaneously present, regardless of iron_floor status — the iron_floor tag on 3PM appears to provide less protection than on PTS/AST in compressed blowout minutes.

miss_details.root_cause: Workflow gap: Sengun did not play (0 minutes, 0 PTS per DNP inferred from game log). The pick was not voided pre-game despite the player ultimately not playing. injury_status_at_check was NOT_LISTED at the time of the evening check (21:53 PT), meaning no pre-game OUT flag existed to trigger a void — but the player still did not dress. This is a workflow timing failure: the final lineup confirmation did not occur close enough to tip-off to catch the late DNP. The original reasoning (90% at T10, 12-game hit streak, soft DvP, home rest) was otherwise sound based on available data; the miss is entirely attributable to availability, not analytical error. No amendment was present on this pick.

Workflow gap: same DNP event as Sengun PTS — 0 minutes played, 0 AST recorded. injury_status_at_check was NOT_LISTED, no pre-game OUT flag triggered a void, and the pick ran despite the player not dressing. The tier_walk correctly noted trend=down limiting confidence to floor (70%), and the analytical reasoning was appropriate given available data. The miss is entirely a workflow timing failure — the afternoon lineup agent did not catch a late DNP. No amendment was present. Per audit policy, no analytical lessons are generated from this miss.

STEP 0 — Game context: CLE @ DAL final 138-105, CLE won by 33 — BLOWOUT. Mitchell played only 29 minutes, consistent with early benching on the winning side once the lead was secure. STEP 1 — Activity confirmed (29 min played, 1 3PM made, 8 AST per other picks hitting). STEP 2 — Classification: model_gap_rule. The signal (spread=13.5, BLOWOUT_RISK) was present in the pick object and the system applied BLOWOUT -10% and the trend=down step-down from T2 to T1, landing at 75% confidence. However, the miss reveals that 3PM props for the winning team in a 33-point blowout are more vulnerable than PTS or AST at the same tier — Mitchell's minutes were compressed and shooting opportunities declined in garbage time. The rule correctly flagged blowout risk but did not go far enough: a hard skip or deeper cap specifically for 3PM on winning-side players when spread_abs ≥ 12 would have caught this. STEP 3 — Original reasoning cited iron_floor and step-down logic correctly; the blind spot was 3PM-specific volume compression under blowout conditions. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 8/10 (85% overall) at T2 before adjustments; the 1-for-3 result on 3PM in a blowout is within the tail risk of an 85% base rate but is directionally consistent with blowout suppression. STEP 5 — Tier walk correctly executed step-down T2→T1; no skip error. The issue is the rule not hard-capping 3PM in blowout scenarios. STEP 6 — No amendment present.

STEP 0 — Game context: MIN @ GS final 127-117, MIN won by 10 — COMPETITIVE. Game script is not a causal factor; this was a close-to-normal game environment. STEP 1 — Activity confirmed (27 min played, per post-game news; Randle recorded 1 AST). STEP 2 — Classification: model_gap_rule. The system correctly blocked T4 AST (tough DvP + PF position gate) and fell back to T2 at 95% (consistent tag), conservatively capped to 80% citing the MIN AST suppressor watch item and down trend. Despite this, Randle recorded only 1 assist — below the T2 threshold of 2. The miss reveals that the MIN AST suppressor watch item (-26pp below baseline, Chris Finch's switching scheme) was identified as a risk but not sufficiently weighted: the system applied conservative confidence (80%) but still ran the pick. When the MIN AST suppressor watch item co-occurs with a tough opponent AST DvP, the two signals compound in a way the current rules do not fully capture. STEP 3 — Original reasoning noted 'T2 is a very low bar for Randle' and cited the consistent tag — this was a reasonable thesis, but the reasoning underweighted the dual-suppressor interaction (MIN scheme + GS tough DvP + trend=down + only 27 min). STEP 4 — Hit rate was 10/10 (95% overall) at T2 on the consistent tag; a miss here is statistically unusual but not impossible (5% expected miss rate), though the compounding suppressor context elevated actual miss probability above model expectation. STEP 5 — Tier walk correctly blocked T4 and fell to T2; no walk error. The gap is in the confidence adjustment, not the tier selection. STEP 6 — No amendment present.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 1/4 parlay session — Sengun's DNP (0 min) was the single failure point that killed two parlays ('Iron Floor Trio' and 'Scottie-Mitchell Cross-Game Lock'), while Randle's 1-assist miss ended the 'Multi-Game AST Sweep'; only 'Scorers Plus Floor' swept cleanly.
parlay_lessons: Alperen Sengun appeared as the anchor scorer leg in two separate parlays on the same day ('Iron Floor Trio' PTS and 'Scottie-Mitchell Cross-Game Lock' PTS) — his single DNP event caused both to miss simultaneously. Enforce a strict one-player-per-parlay-card rule: no player may appear in more than one parlay on the same date, regardless of how strong their individual pick looks. Concentration risk across parlays is as dangerous as within a single parlay.

The 'Multi-Game AST Sweep' used three T2 AST legs (all pick_value=2) across independent games — a structurally sound design — but the Randle leg carried a known MIN AST suppressor risk and a tough opponent DvP that together elevated miss probability above the advertised 95% hit rate. When building an AST sweep parlay, exclude any leg where two compounding AST suppressor signals (scheme + opponent DvP) co-exist, even at T2; replace with a leg from a game without suppressor flags.

The one winning parlay ('Scorers Plus Floor') succeeded because all three legs were fully independent games (TOR vs PHX, MIN vs GS, CLE vs DAL) with no shared players, no shared game, and mixed prop types (PTS + AST + REB) — this cross-prop, cross-game independence structure is the template for durable parlay construction and should be the default architecture.
parlay_reinforcements: The 'Scorers Plus Floor' structure — one up-trending scorer PTS leg (Barrett T10, 95% consistent) anchoring two separate ultra-conservative floor legs (Edwards AST T2, Mitchell REB T2) across three fully independent games — is the most resilient parlay architecture the system has produced this session. Replicate this pattern: one 'proven scorer' PTS anchor + two non-correlated iron-floor legs in different games and different prop types.

Cross-prop diversity within a parlay (mixing PTS + AST + REB across different players and games) provides structural insurance that a single game script or scheme matchup cannot collapse the entire ticket — the 'Scorers Plus Floor' hit precisely because no two legs shared a game or prop type, making each leg's success condition fully independent of the others.

T2 AST legs (pick_value=2) remain the single most reliable parlay building block when the player is a consistent primary creator with soft or mid DvP and no suppressor flags — Kawhi Leonard (3 actual), Brandon Ingram (3 actual), Anthony Edwards (5 actual), and Amen Thompson (8 actual) all cleared T2 AST comfortably, confirming this leg type as a high-confidence anchor for future parlays.

reinforcements: Blowout-resilient PTS floors held perfectly: Derrick White (15 actual vs T10), Jalen Johnson (23 actual vs T15), and Payton Pritchard REB (4 actual vs T2) all hit in the BOS blowout with the spread≥12 cap correctly reducing confidence without eliminating picks — the tiered cap approach preserved value while managing risk.

Soft DEN defense paid off across the LAL-DEN slate: Reaves (32 PTS), LeBron (17 PTS, 5 AST), Luka (30 PTS, 4 3PM), and Jokic (24 PTS, 14 AST) all cleared their tiers decisively — stacking multiple props against a confirmed soft defense in a competitive, close game (LAL won by 2) maximized yield with minimal game-script suppression risk.

The mandatory min_floor step-down rule functioned correctly: both Jamal Murray (T20→T15 due to min_floor=17.0) and LaMelo Ball (T15→T10 due to min_floor=22.0) were stepped down before the session ran, and both were correctly graded — Murray missed PTS (correctly stepped down but still missed, suggesting the min_floor signal warranted a skip rather than a step), while LaMelo hit PTS at T10, validating the step-down as a protective mechanism.

3PM props were the strongest category (6/6, 100%) — the consistent-tag + T1 floor structure with trend-based step-down produced a clean sweep. Bane's T1 3PM holding at exactly 1.0 (exact threshold = HIT per grading rule) and Kawhi's T1 3PM (2 actual) demonstrate that the minimum-viable-tier approach for trend=down shooters is correctly calibrated.

Iron-floor tags proved reliable on multi-prop stacks: Brandon Miller's AST (3 actual) and 3PM (2 actual) both hit despite a poor scoring night, confirming that iron-floor secondary props can serve as insurance legs even when the primary PTS prop collapses — validating the structural case for cross-prop diversification within a player stack.
lessons: Kawhi Leonard AST T2 missed at 0.0 actual despite a 10/10 hit rate — this was the model_gap_signal miss of the session. The LAC-SAC game ended LAC losing by 9 (SAC won 118-109), a close game with no blowout suppression excuse. Kawhi played only 26 minutes, suggesting load management or an in-game restriction that the system had no signal for. The 'B2B b2b=89%' note in tier_walk indicates back-to-back data was used but the actual game was not a B2B — the system did not have a mechanism to detect minutes restriction outside the B2B context for a player of Kawhi's age and management profile. A load-management/minutes-cap signal for veteran players who are known to be minutes-managed should be added.

LaMelo Ball AST T4 missed badly (2 actual vs pick_value 4) in a competitive CHA-SA game (SA won by 13, COMPETITIVE) where LaMelo played only 22 minutes — a significant minute restriction that the system did not anticipate despite the SAS floor-compression watch item being flagged. The miss margin of −2 is consistent with the SAS floor-compression pattern (mean miss margin −6.0 on that watch item) and the 22-minute game further compressed counting stats. The AST T4+ pick should have been avoided given both the SAS scheme flag and LaMelo's min_floor risk (22.0 min played confirms low-minutes exposure was real). This is a model_gap_rule miss: the SAS watch item existed but was not elevated to a hard gate for high-threshold AST props.

Brandon Miller PTS T15 produced only 6 actual points — a severe miss (−9 below threshold) in a COMPETITIVE CHA-SA game. Miller played 33 minutes, so minutes were not the cause. The iron_floor tag and 9/10 hit rate gave false confidence, and the trend=down signal was present but did not trigger a step-down for PTS props. Combined with the SAS floor-compression pattern already flagged as a watch item, this outcome strengthens the case that SAS scheme structurally suppresses counting stats. The miss is variance in isolation, but the SAS pattern is now at n=4 misses, approaching the threshold where a hard discount rule should be considered.

Jamal Murray PTS T15 (5 actual, miss by 10) in a CLOSE LAL-DEN game (LAL won by 2, 36 minutes played by Murray) cannot be attributed to blowout or minutes restriction — Murray played a full game. The VOLATILE tag and min_floor=17.0 flag were present and the mandatory step had already been applied. This is a variance miss on a correctly-stepped-down, appropriately-hedged pick (70% confidence), but the combination of VOLATILE + min_floor risk + away team in a competitive game suggests the pick should have been skipped entirely rather than stepped down. The min_floor step-down rule may need an additional skip condition: if min_floor < 20 AND VOLATILE AND away game, skip rather than step.

Payton Pritchard PTS T10 correctly identified as a skip by the system (VOLATILE -5% dropped confidence to 65%), and the actual outcome (4 points) validated that skip decision. This is a reinforcement of the VOLATILE skip rule — note that even though the pick was included in the session output (it appears in the graded picks as a MISS), the reasoning field explicitly states it should have been skipped. This represents a workflow execution gap: if a pick is analytically identified as a skip during the tier walk, it must not be submitted. The pick appeared in results as a MISS because it ran despite the skip signal.
recommendations: Add a veteran load-management minutes-cap signal for known minutes-managed players (Kawhi Leonard being the prototype): if a player's season avg_minutes is below 30 and they are age 33+, apply an additional −10% confidence penalty on all props and flag as 'load_mgmt_risk'. Kawhi's 26 minutes played confirms the system had no mechanism to anticipate this beyond the B2B flag, which was inapplicable here.

Elevate the SAS floor-compression watch item to a conditional hard gate for high-threshold props: for PTS T15+ and AST T4+ against San Antonio, require hit_rate ≥ 9/10 AND trend ≠ down to proceed. The pattern is now confirmed across Brandon Miller PTS (−9 miss), LaMelo Ball AST (−2 miss), and prior session misses — the n=4 threshold justifies a provisional hard gate ahead of the ≥400-pick reconfirmation target.

Enforce a skip condition for VOLATILE + min_floor < 20 + away game combinations on PTS props: Jamal Murray's T15 miss (5 actual, 36 min played, no external cause) demonstrates that the step-down rule alone is insufficient when all three risk factors co-occur. The correct action when VOLATILE + min_floor < 20 + away = skip entirely, not step down. Apply retroactively as a rule update for the next session.

Implement a workflow enforcement rule: any pick whose tier_walk reasoning field concludes with a 'skip' determination must be excluded from the submission queue. Payton Pritchard PTS T10 was analytically identified as a skip (VOLATILE -5% → 65%, below threshold) but was submitted and missed (4 actual). The analytical logic was correct; the execution was the failure. Add a post-processing gate that screens reasoning fields for skip language before finalizing the pick list.

For multi-prop stacks on players in CHA/SA games, cap at two legs per player and require at least one leg to be a secondary stat (AST or REB, not PTS) with iron_floor present: Brandon Miller's PTS miss (6 actual) could not be offset because the iron_floor only protected AST and 3PM. When the SAS scheme flag is active, PTS should be the leg most likely to be suppressed — structure stacks to hedge PTS exposure with lower-variance secondary props.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0 — Game: CHA @ SA, SA won 115-102 (margin 13, COMPETITIVE). No blowout suppression, but COMPETITIVE game in San Antonio with a 13-point SA win is consistent with SAS defensive scheme compressing Charlotte's counting stats. STEP 1 — Miller played 33 minutes with 6 PTS, non-zero activity confirmed — no injury or DNP. STEP 2 — model_gap_rule: the SAS floor-compression watch item was explicitly flagged in the scheme notes (mean miss margin −6.0, n=3 prior misses vs SAS), yet the pick proceeded at full confidence because no hard gate existed for PTS props against SAS. The signal existed in quant context but the rule threshold was too permissive. STEP 3 — Analyst reasoning cited iron_floor T15 and 9/10 hit rate but did not apply a discount for the SAS watch item, which was the critical oversight. The trend=down signal also existed but no step-down rule applies to PTS — a second gap. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 9/10, making this a genuine shock miss; the SAS scheme signal is the only available structural explanation. STEP 5 — Tier walk shows T15→9/10 with iron_floor; no higher-confidence tier was skipped, so tier_skip_error does not apply. The issue was proceeding at this tier against SAS at all. STEP 6 — No amendment flagged. With this miss, the SAS floor-compression pattern reaches n=4 (Miller PTS + LaMelo AST this session, plus 3 prior session misses), strengthening the case for a provisional hard gate.

STEP 0 — Game: DEN @ LAL, LAL won 127-125 (margin 2, CLOSE). Competitive game, no blowout suppression. Murray played 36 minutes — full participation confirmed. STEP 1 — Murray played 36 minutes, non-zero activity, 5 PTS scored. Active and participating throughout. STEP 2 — variance: all protective rules were applied correctly. The mandatory step T20→T15 was executed due to min_floor=17.0, VOLATILE tag was noted, and confidence was appropriately reduced to 70%. Despite these safeguards, Murray scored only 5 points in a full 36-minute game in a close contest. No structural explanation (blowout, minutes restriction, injury exit) is available. STEP 3 — Analyst reasoning correctly identified the min_floor risk and applied the mandatory step-down; the original thesis was appropriately hedged. The 70% confidence reflected genuine uncertainty. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 8/10 (prior to the mandatory step that brought the tier to T15); at T15 the implied rate supported the pick. A miss on a correctly-hedged 70% pick is within normal variance. STEP 5 — Tier walk shows T25→3/10, T20→8/10 (original tier), then mandatory step to T15; the step-down was the only viable action given the data. STEP 6 — No amendment flagged. Note: the combination of VOLATILE + min_floor < 20 + away game should be reviewed as a potential skip condition for future sessions — this outcome supports that refinement even though this specific miss is classified as variance.

STEP 0 — Game: SAC @ LAC, SAC won 118-109 (margin 9, CLOSE). LAC lost by 9 — a close game with no blowout excuse for either side. STEP 1 — Kawhi played 26 minutes (confirmed from post-game news context). Non-zero minutes but 0 assists recorded. Fully active, not a DNP or injury exit. STEP 2 — model_gap_signal: Kawhi recorded 0 AST in 26 minutes despite a 10/10 historical hit rate at T2. The 26-minute game suggests a load management decision or minutes cap that reduced his in-game effectiveness and role. The system has no load-management minutes-cap signal for veteran players outside of the B2B framework — the tier_walk references B2B data (b2b=78%/89%) but this was not a B2B game. There is no quant field, annotation, or prompt rule for in-game minutes restriction by load management for age-managed veterans. STEP 3 — Analyst reasoning cited 10/10 hit rate, consistent tag, and B2B data — all valid for a normal-minutes game. The 26-minute game was not predictable from available pick-time data, making this a genuine signal gap, not an analytical error. STEP 4 — 10/10 hit rate is elite; a 0-assist outcome from 26 minutes in a close game is an extreme outlier. STEP 5 — Tier walk shows T4→7/10, T2→10/10; the selected tier was the best available and there is no tier-walk issue. STEP 6 — No amendment flagged. Root signal needed: a minutes-management flag for age/injury-history-managed veterans that can discount prop confidence when role compression is plausible independent of B2B context.

STEP 0 — Game: CHA @ SA, SA won 115-102 (margin 13, COMPETITIVE). LaMelo played only 22 minutes — a significant restriction in a game CHA lost by 13. In a COMPETITIVE loss, starters may have been pulled earlier than usual in the fourth quarter, compressing LaMelo's counting stat opportunities. STEP 1 — LaMelo played 22 minutes (confirmed post-game news), 2 AST recorded. Active but heavily minutes-restricted. STEP 2 — model_gap_rule: two signals existed at pick time that together should have blocked this pick: (a) the SAS floor-compression watch item was flagged in scheme notes, specifically relevant to high-threshold AST props; (b) LaMelo's min_floor was 22.0, which directly predicted the 22-minute actual game. The combination of SAS scheme watch item + low min_floor for AST T4+ should have triggered a skip, but no hard gate existed for this combination. The signal was present; the rule was too permissive. STEP 3 — Analyst reasoning noted the SAS floor-compression watch item explicitly ('SAS floor compression watch item noted but small sample') but proceeded anyway, citing the 100% hit rate as sufficient justification. This was the analytical error — the watch item should have been a hard stop for T4+ AST, not an acknowledged footnote. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 10/10, which created false confidence. The SAS watch item and min_floor signal should have overridden the raw hit rate. STEP 5 — Tier walk shows T6→5/10, T4→10/10; T4 was the only viable tier. No tier_skip_error, but the correct outcome was a full skip, not tier selection. This pick was top_pick=true — a top pick miss amplifies the lesson. STEP 6 — No amendment flagged. The top_pick designation on a pick with two active risk flags (SAS scheme + min_floor) should itself have been a disqualifying condition.

STEP 0 — Game: WSH @ BOS, BOS won 111-100 (margin 11, COMPETITIVE). Not a full blowout, but an 11-point BOS home win where Pritchard played 30 minutes — no blowout suppression applies. STEP 1 — Pritchard played 30 minutes with 4 PTS. Fully active, non-zero across other categories (4 REB, 4 AST both hit). STEP 2 — model_gap_rule: the analyst's own tier_walk reasoning concluded this pick should be skipped ('VOLATILE -5% → 65% → below 70% SKIP') but the pick was submitted anyway. This is a workflow execution failure — the analytical logic correctly identified the skip condition, but the pick was not removed from the submission queue. The rule existed and was applied correctly in reasoning; the gap was in enforcement. STEP 3 — Analyst reasoning explicitly states 'VOLATILE -5% drops 70% to 65%; below threshold — skip' — the reasoning was correct and self-aware. The failure was submitting a pick that the analyst's own logic had already disqualified. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 7/10 at T10; after VOLATILE deduction to 65%, below the 70% minimum threshold. The 4 actual points confirms the VOLATILE classification was accurate. STEP 5 — Tier walk shows T15→4/10, T10→7/10; T10 was the highest viable tier before deductions. The pick would have needed to proceed at T10 or be skipped — the correct answer was skip. STEP 6 — No amendment flagged. Workflow fix required: picks whose reasoning field contains an explicit skip conclusion must be filtered out by the post-processing layer before submission. This miss was analytically predicted by the system itself and should never have appeared in the final pick list.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 2/3 parlay session — Kawhi Leonard AST T2 (0 actual) busted the Saturday Floor Sweeper; the two surviving parlays both went 3/3 anchored by Luka PTS and Banchero PTS floors.
parlay_lessons: The Saturday Floor Sweeper's Kawhi AST leg was the structural weakness of the card: a veteran load-management player on a minutes-cap (26 min played) is a fragile parlay leg regardless of hit rate, because the binary all-or-nothing parlay format magnifies any low-probability outcome. Future parlays should avoid high-threshold AST props for known minutes-managed veterans unless minutes floor is confirmed pre-game — the 10/10 hit rate created false security in a prop type where a single off-night produces a total loss.

When the SAS floor-compression watch item is active, any CHA player prop in a SAS road game should be treated as a high-risk parlay leg — LaMelo Ball's top_pick AST miss (2 actual vs T4) and Brandon Miller's PTS miss (6 actual vs T15) confirm that the watch item is structurally predictive enough to disqualify these legs from parlay inclusion until a hard gate is established. Do not include players from floor-compression scheme matchups as parlay legs, even at 10/10 base rates.
parlay_reinforcements: The cross-game, cross-prop PTS anchor structure worked cleanly in both winning parlays: Luka PTS T25 (30 actual) + Banchero PTS T10 (27 actual) as co-anchors in independent games with soft/mid defense provided a resilient foundation — when two elite-tier PTS props from different games are stacked, a single leg failure by one does not undermine the other, and the third leg (Kawhi 3PM T1, 2 actual) provided low-variance insurance. The multi-game scorer anchor with a low-threshold insurance leg is the highest-performing parlay structure this session.

The Cross-Game Volume Mix parlay succeeded by pairing a high-variance scorer (Reaves, 32 actual) with two low-threshold, high-floor secondary props (Bane AST T2, 4 actual; LaMelo PTS T10, 17 actual) — this structure distributes risk across prop types and game scripts, ensuring that even a moderate outcome from the anchor scorer would have carried the card. Prioritize parlay legs that combine one high-upside PTS anchor with two independent low-threshold (T1-T2) props from separate games.

reinforcements: Ultra-conservative PTS thresholds (T10) with 10/10 or 9/10 hit rates went 5/5 — the system's strongest signal is volume-floor scoring props at deeply conservative tiers. RJ Barrett (27), Julius Randle (32), Scottie Barnes (14), Evan Mobley (18), and Chet Holmgren (21) all cleared T10 comfortably. Continue anchoring PTS selection at T10 with consistent tags.

REB T4 iron-floor or near-iron picks with 3rd-lowest L10 strictly below 4 proved extremely reliable: Randle (7 REB vs T4), Duren (11 REB vs T4), Flagg (6 REB vs T4), Mobley (11 REB vs T4), and Gobert (7 REB vs T6) all hit. The strictly-less-than floor gate correctly filtered out Hartenstein and Thompson (both 3rd-lowest = pick value exactly), of which Thompson missed badly (1 REB). The gate is functioning as designed.

AST T2 picks at 9/10 or 10/10 hit rates were 5/5 on hittable legs (Randle, Edwards, Ingram excluded due to 0 AST miss, Mitchell, SGA). The T2 conservative step-down for AST props on non-primary ball-handlers remains the right structural call — the one AST miss (Ingram) was at T2 with a 10/10 rate, suggesting the threshold itself was sound but the outcome was a genuine outlier.

The lineup amendment system correctly identified upside for absence-beneficiaries — Duren (11 REB, amended up to 85%), Barnes (10 REB, amended up to 85%), Mobley (11 REB, amended up to 80%) all well exceeded their thresholds. When a primary creator goes OUT and a big man inherits rebounding/scoring load against a weakened defense, the upward amendment logic is directionally accurate and should continue to be trusted.

Picks on the winning side of COMPETITIVE games (margin 10-19) showed no systematic compression — OKC's SGA (10 AST, 2 3PM), Holmgren (21 PTS), and TOR's Barrett (27 PTS), Barnes (14 PTS, 10 REB) all hit well above threshold. COMPETITIVE game script is genuinely neutral context and does not require confidence penalties.
lessons: Brandon Ingram's AST miss (0 actual vs T2 pick) with a 10/10 hit rate and 39 minutes played is a true variance outlier — no injury exit, full minutes, game was COMPETITIVE (TOR won by 11). However, the 10/10 AST T2 rate means one zero-assist game in L10 is statistically plausible. Flag: AST props for forwards (SF) who operate as secondary creators are more susceptible to zero-assist outliers than PG/SG primary ball-handlers even at T2; the zero-assist game is the tail risk that T2 still exposes. Consider whether a minimum L10 floor check (e.g., no more than 1 game with 0 AST in L10) should be added as a gate for AST SF/PF picks.

James Harden's 3PM miss (1 actual vs T2 = 2 threshold) occurred in a game CLE won by 10 (COMPETITIVE). The tier_walk correctly flagged a LARGE SPREAD blowout cap was applied (spread_abs=16.5), but the final spread was only 10 points — the blowout did not materialize at the extreme level anticipated. The pick was made with spread-based concern already priced in at 74%. This is a variance miss on a 10/10 T2 rate — Harden hit 1/2 of threshold. However, note that the lineup agent marked this pick as void (Harden OUT) yet actual_value = 1.0 and the pick graded as a MISS, meaning Harden played 38 minutes despite the OUT flag. The workflow flagged him OUT but he played — this is a data-integrity flag: the lineup update's triggered_by field listed 'James Harden OUT' but box score shows 38 minutes. Do not penalize the Analyst for this pick's selection logic; investigate whether the OUT report was retracted pre-tip and the lineup agent did not re-check.

Ausar Thompson's REB miss (1 actual vs T4) was correctly flagged as a SKIP by the floor gate (3rd-lowest L10 = 4, equal to pick value — no strictly-less buffer). The pick ran at 78% despite the analyst's own tier_walk concluding 'SKIP.' This represents a workflow execution gap: the system computed SKIP in the tier_walk but still filed the pick. Reinforce the rule: if tier_walk concludes SKIP at all valid REB tiers, the pick must not be submitted regardless of lineup update direction. The Cunningham OUT amendment pushed confidence to 78% (up direction), but the absence of a strictly-less floor buffer should have been a hard block.
recommendations: Add a hard zero-assist floor check for AST picks on SF/PF positions: if L10 contains any game with 0 AST, require the hit rate at the selected tier to be 10/10 (not 9/10) before filing. Ingram had 10/10 at T2 but still produced 0 AST in a game with 39 minutes — the zero-assist game that is possible even at T2 is the relevant risk for non-PG creators.

Implement a hard block on any pick where the tier_walk field contains the word 'SKIP' or 'floor gate FAILS' — regardless of lineup amendments. Ausar Thompson's miss was predicted by the analyst's own floor gate logic but the pick ran anyway. The lineup agent's upward amendment should never override a SKIP determination from the floor gate; these are independent checks and the floor gate must take precedence.

Investigate the James Harden OUT data-integrity issue: the lineup agent flagged Harden as OUT pre-game (triggered_by: 'James Harden OUT'), yet box score shows 38 minutes played and actual stats were recorded. If the OUT designation was a false positive or was retracted, the void should have been cancelled. Build a pre-tip status re-check into the pipeline: if a player was flagged OUT by the lineup agent but has a valid tip-off window remaining, re-query injury status within 30 minutes of game start and un-void if status changed to active.

For 3PM props on CLE players in large-spread games: the LARGE SPREAD cap (spread ≥ 13, cap at 74-80%) and the actual blowout outcome diverged — the game finished at a 10-point margin, not the anticipated blowout. Harden's 3PM miss was a genuine variance outcome, but the cap logic correctly reduced exposure. No rule change needed; reinforce that the spread-based cap is a prudent constraint even when the blowout doesn't materialize, as it correctly sizes confidence for tail-risk scenarios.

Continue using the T4 REB floor gate with strict inequality (3rd-lowest must be strictly less than pick value) as a hard filter. The two picks that were flagged SKIP by this gate (Hartenstein and Thompson) had actual values of 12 and 1 respectively — one hit the floor easily (Hartenstein, would have been a hit) and one missed badly (Thompson, 1 REB). The gate correctly identified both as lacking a reliable floor buffer. The asymmetric outcome (one would have hit, one badly missed) validates the gate as a conservative but correct risk filter over the long run.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0: TOR vs DET — TOR won by 11, COMPETITIVE game. No blowout script effect. STEP 1: Ingram logged 39 minutes; non-zero output in other categories (full participant). The 0 AST result is a within-game distributional outlier, not a DNP or injury exit. STEP 2: Classified as variance. The 10/10 AST T2 hit rate is the system's highest-confidence AST floor — a 0-assist game against this baseline is a genuine low-probability tail event. No quant signal, injury flag, or scheme mismatch was available at pick time that would have predicted a zero. STEP 3: Original reasoning ('10/10 AST T2 consistent; reliable floor regardless of DvP in competitive game') was structurally sound — the T2 threshold is deliberately conservative for exactly this reason. The reasoning correctly identified the floor but could not price a rare zero-assist game for a forward. STEP 4: 10/10 hit rate at T2 means one miss in L10 is within statistical expectation; this is the tail event that a 90%+ implied rate acknowledges. STEP 5: Tier walk correctly stepped from T4 (borderline gate, skipped) to T2 (10/10, confirmed). No tier_skip_error. STEP 6: Lineup amendment direction = 'unchanged' — no revision triggered by Cunningham OUT for this TOR-side pick. No amendment commentary required. Structural note: SF/PF AST props at T2 carry zero-assist tail risk even at 10/10 — consider adding a L10 zero-count gate.

STEP 0: CLE vs DAL — CLE won by 10, COMPETITIVE game. Pre-game spread was 16.5, but actual margin was 10; the anticipated blowout did not materialize. STEP 1: Harden logged 38 minutes per post-game news context, producing real stats including 1 3PM. STEP 2: Classified as variance. The pick carried a large-spread blowout cap already applied (confidence reduced to 74%), and the actual outcome was a near-miss at 1/2 — within normal shooting variance for a volume perimeter player. Data-integrity flag: lineup agent listed Harden as OUT pre-game (triggered_by), yet box score shows 38 minutes played. This suggests the OUT report was either a false positive or was retracted pre-tip. The pick was not voided despite the agent flagging it; this is a pipeline integrity issue separate from selection quality. STEP 3: Original reasoning ('10/10 3PM T2 consistent; blowout cap applied; DAL defense is soft for perimeter') correctly identified the floor and applied a conservative cap. The cap reduced confidence appropriately. The miss at 1 vs threshold 2 is a single-make shortfall on a high-volume 3PM prop — within variance. STEP 4: 10/10 T2 hit rate supports this as a sound pick; the miss does not indicate a systematic flaw. STEP 5: Tier walk stepped from T3 (pace gate not confirmed, capped at T2) to T2 (10/10, 92% base). Conservative and correct. No tier_skip_error. STEP 6: Amendment direction = 'down' (Harden OUT flag); pick still ran and missed. Amendment correctly flagged OUT risk — revised to 74%, but the pick ran as the player was apparently active. Phrase: 'Amendment correctly flagged downside risk (Harden OUT triggered). Revised to 74% — pick ran and missed by 1 make.' This is a workflow gap on the OUT/active status re-check, not a selection error.

STEP 0: DET @ TOR — TOR won by 11, COMPETITIVE game. No blowout game-script compression applicable. STEP 1: Thompson logged 24 minutes; 1 REB in that time is a legitimate but very low output.
