A tighter rule requiring confirmed OUT for Without-Star PTS baseline activation would have either skipped or reduced this pick below the output threshold.

STEP 0 — Game context: LAC @ DAL final 138-131, margin=7, CLOSE game script. LAC won by 7; not a blowout. Game script is neutral — Kawhi played full minutes (36 min confirmed in POST-GAME NEWS), so minute compression is not a causal factor. STEP 1 — Player active: Kawhi logged 36 minutes with non-zero output (5 AST, 5 3PM confirmed as HITs in same game). The REB miss is a pure counting stat variance outcome, not a minutes or availability issue. STEP 2 — No injury_exit in POST-GAME NEWS. Kawhi was noted as QUES at pick time but played full minutes. STEP 3 — Original reasoning cited iron-floor T4 at 95% hit rate with a T4 exact-match exemption and consistent tag. The reasoning was sound given the data available; the miss is a 1-rebound shortfall (3 actual vs. 4 pick) in a 36-minute game. The Without-Garland T4=55% concern was correctly flagged and the standard T4 baseline was used instead. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 9/10 (95%) at the standard T4. A miss on a 95% historical rate represents the 5% tail — this is within normal variance. STEP 5 — Tier walk notes T4 exact-match exemption applies; Without-Garland T4=55% failed but standard T4 at 95% passed. The tier selected was the minimum valid tier with exemption, which is a thin structural position, but the 9/10 baseline made it procedurally sound. A trend=down −5% penalty was applied, producing the 80% confidence output. STEP 6 — No lineup_update sub-object present. Classification is variance: the pick had an iron-floor T4 with 9/10 historical support, Kawhi played full minutes, the game was not a blowout, and the outcome (3 REB) is a single-rebound shortfall that falls within the normal distribution of a player whose REB floor is at the minimum valid tier. The trend=down signal was appropriately penalized in confidence but not sufficient to warrant a skip given iron-floor status.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 3/3 sweep on the Saturday Floor Lock, anchored by iron-floor AST and a dominant Kawhi 3PM performance (5 made vs. a pick of 1).
parlay_lessons: The Saturday Floor Lock parlay included LeBron PTS as a single-leg pick (which missed) and LeBron AST as a parlay leg (which hit exactly at threshold — 4.0 actual vs. 4 pick). The parlay survived because the AST leg was chosen over the PTS leg for inclusion, which was correct structurally: the AST iron-floor with elite playmaker exemption is a more durable leg than a PTS pick built on a 6-game Without-Star baseline. When a player has two candidate legs with different structural confidence, always prefer the iron-floor AST or counting stat leg over the PTS leg when the PTS baseline depends on a small-sample Without-Star qualifier — the parlay card would have been at serious risk if LeBron PTS had been included instead.
parlay_reinforcements: The opposing-team stack (LeBron AST as feeder + Banchero PTS as scorer in the same LAL-ORL game) demonstrated that positive correlation in a competitive game script lifts both legs simultaneously: LeBron's 4 AST in 34 minutes in a 1-point game supported a sustained offensive rhythm that also enabled Banchero's 16 PTS. Mixed-team same-game stacks with feeder/scorer correlation logic are validated when the game stays competitive throughout — the CLOSE script (margin=1) was the ideal environment for both counting stat floors to be reached without blowout truncation.

Using an independent third leg (Kawhi 3PM vs. DAL) from a different game to anchor the parlay against correlated-leg variance was structurally sound: even if the LAL-ORL game had gone sideways (as it nearly did for LeBron PTS in the single-leg card), the Kawhi leg was fully decoupled. When building 3-leg parlays with a same-game stack, always pair with one independent leg from a high-confidence tier (T1 3PM, 95% historical rate) to provide a non-correlated floor.

reinforcements: Scottie Barnes T10 PTS (10/10 consistent tag, 96% vs tough defenses) delivered 17 vs PHX — the consistent tag plus historically confirmed tough-defense performance is a reliable combination even when trend=down; the 10/10 hit rate made this the cleanest pick on the slate.

Low-threshold AST and 3PM picks on TOR players (Barrett T2 AST, Barrett T1 3PM) both hit in a competitive PHX game — the T2/T1 floor strategy for volatile players with directional risk correctly limits exposure while maintaining a qualifying tier, and the 75-80% confidence band went 2/2.

Jokic's iron-floor T20 PTS (22 actual vs 20 threshold) validated the elite-scorer blowout exemption logic in a DEN win by 16: despite a competitive-to-blowout margin, his 35 minutes of play confirmed that elite scorers are not rested mid-game at spreads in the 8–14 range unless the game is fully decided in the fourth quarter, and his volume held.

Randle REB T4 (9 actual vs 4 threshold) hit emphatically even in a 10-point road loss — the ultra-low REB floor strategy correctly isolated the most game-script-resilient prop type for a player on the losing side of a competitive game, confirming that rebounding volume is far less sensitive to blowout-loss game scripts than scoring or assists.

The 81-85% confidence band went 2/2 (Scottie Barnes PTS at 82%, Barnes REB at 83%), continuing the pattern that higher-confidence picks — when properly gated through the high-confidence gate conditions — outperform the 70-75% band. This reinforces the value of demanding all three gate conditions before assigning 81%+.
lessons: Julius Randle PTS MISS (9 vs threshold 10): The Without-Edwards 10/10 hit rate at T15 (corrected to T10) was genuinely elite historical evidence, but a 10.5-point road underdog situation at BOS creates losing-side game-script suppression that compressed Randle's scoring volume below even the T10 floor. MIN lost by 10 (COMPETITIVE borderline), and with 29 minutes played, Randle's scoring was simply squeezed — he finished at 9, one unit below threshold. This is a model_gap_rule miss: the losing-side blowout PTS penalty exists in the rules but was not applied aggressively enough given a spread_abs of 10.5. The pick's tier_walk noted the Without-Edwards elevation but did not apply a full -10% losing-side PTS penalty, which would have pushed confidence below the selection threshold or triggered a tier step-down. Lesson: when spread_abs ≥ 10 and the player is on the losing-side underdog team, apply a mandatory -10% PTS confidence penalty regardless of Without-star elevation; if the resulting confidence drops below 75%, step down one tier.

RJ Barrett PTS MISS (13 vs threshold 15): The tier_walk correctly identified that VOLATILE + 7/10 at T15 fires a hard skip, and the reasoning explicitly noted 'hard skip fires — volatile_weak_combo skip.' This pick was selected despite the hard skip rule being triggered, which is a selection_error. The human_verdict of 'keep' means the Analyst or human override retained it, but the model's own logic flagged it as a skip. With only 23 minutes played (compressed in a PHX blowout win of 22 points — TOR was the losing side of a BLOWOUT), Randle's 13 PTS fell 2 below threshold. Even absent the blowout context, the VOLATILE + 7/10 combination at T15 was a structural disqualifier. Lesson: VOLATILE + sub-8/10 hit rate at T15+ is a hard skip with no override pathway unless iron_floor is present; human 'keep' overrides on hard-skip picks should be flagged as audit violations. The blowout-loss context (TOR lost by 22) further confirms that retaining this pick was doubly wrong.

Brandon Ingram AST MISS (1 vs threshold 2): Ingram recorded only 1 assist in 27 minutes in a competitive PHX game (PHX won by 22 — actually a BLOWOUT, TOR lost by 22). With trend=down on AST and 27 minutes played in a blowout loss, Ingram's assist opportunities collapsed as TOR's offensive system broke down in the second half. The T2 AST=90% overall baseline is sound, but the blowout-loss game script for TOR — which was losing by a large margin — structurally suppressed half-court creation sequences, reducing assist volume below T2. This is a model_gap_rule miss: the TOR blowout-loss context (spread_abs=2.5 pre-game, but the actual margin was 22) was not predictable from pre-game data alone, but the trend=down signal on AST combined with a road game against a tough defense should have triggered a tier step-down to T1 or a confidence reduction below selection threshold. The pick also included a -5% tough-defense adjustment but did not apply a sufficient conservative discount for a road AST pick with downward trend. Lesson: for road AST picks with trend=down against tough defenses, apply an additional -5% conservative penalty (total -10% for trend+road+tough) before selecting; if the resulting confidence drops below 72%, skip the AST pick entirely.
recommendations: Implement a mandatory losing-side blowout PTS penalty of -10% when spread_abs ≥ 10 and the player is on the underdog team, applied regardless of Without-star elevation. Randle's PTS miss is the direct evidence: a 10/10 Without-Edwards baseline did not protect against a 10.5-point road underdog game script that compressed scoring to exactly one unit below the T10 threshold. If post-penalty confidence falls below 75%, step down one tier rather than proceeding at reduced confidence.

Enforce the VOLATILE + sub-8/10 hard skip at T15+ with zero human override pathway unless iron_floor is explicitly present on the picked stat. Barrett's PTS pick should never have appeared on the final card — the tier_walk correctly identified the hard skip, but it was retained anyway. Add an audit flag: any pick that fires a hard skip rule but appears in the final output without iron_floor override must be voided pre-game.

For road AST picks with trend=down against tough defenses, apply a stacked penalty of -10% total (trend=down: -5%, road+tough: -5%) before finalizing confidence. If the resulting confidence is below 72%, do not select the AST pick. Ingram's T2 AST miss in a TOR blowout loss demonstrates that downward trending assist volume on the road compounds with game-script compression — the pre-game confidence of 75% was already borderline and should have been reduced below selection threshold.

When the actual game margin significantly exceeds the pre-game spread (e.g., pre-game spread of 2.5 but actual margin of 22), this represents an unpredictable game-script outcome. However, for future sessions: when TOR (or any team) is playing a road game against a team with top-10 defensive rating AND the player has trend=down on the prop type, treat the combination as equivalent to a known BLOWOUT_RISK scenario and apply the -10% blowout penalty preemptively. The PHX game (TOR lost by 22) reinforces that tough-defense road games carry structural blowout risk even at small pre-game spreads.

The 70-75% confidence band went 5/8 (62.5%) against an expected 72.5%, underperforming by 10 percentage points. All three misses occurred in this band. Review whether picks in this band with two or more compounding risk factors (VOLATILE tag, trend=down, road underdog, tough defense) should be elevated to 76%+ requirements or skipped entirely. Consider a rule: picks in the 70-75% band require at least one positive signal (iron_floor, consistent tag, or 10/10 hit rate) to qualify; picks with only baseline qualifying hit rates (7/10 at T15, 9/10 at T10) plus two risk factors should be stepped down or skipped.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0: MIN @ BOS — MIN won? No: BOS won 102-92, margin 10, labeled COMPETITIVE. MIN lost by 10 as the road underdog. STEP 1: Randle played 29 minutes, recorded 9 PTS — active, below threshold by 1. STEP 2: No injury event; NOT_LISTED at pick time. Classification: model_gap_rule. STEP 3: The reasoning correctly identified Without-Edwards T15=100% (corrected to T10) and the strong historical baseline, but did not apply a sufficient losing-side game-script penalty. With MIN as a 10.5-point road underdog at BOS (tough defense), the scoring suppression from a competitive loss was enough to push Randle 1 unit below the T10 threshold. The tier_walk applied a -5% losing-side blowout concern but noted uncertainty about whether to apply it at full -10%. STEP 4: Hit rate was 10/10 Without-Edwards — a genuinely elite baseline — but the 1-unit miss margin confirms that a T10 floor on a player facing tough defense as a 10.5-point road underdog has minimal cushion. STEP 5: The tier_walk shows T10 as the post-correction qualifying tier; Without-Edwards T15=100% was the original basis. No tier_skip_error — T10 was the correct application. STEP 6: No lineup_update present. Root cause: The losing-side blowout PTS penalty (-10%) was under-applied in the tier_walk calculation. The signal existed (spread_abs=10.5, road underdog) but the rule threshold was too permissive — the pick proceeded at confidence near 84% when a full -10% penalty should have pushed it toward 74% or prompted a tier step-down to T8/T6 with additional cushion. This is a model_gap_rule miss: tighter application of the losing-side penalty rule would have either reduced confidence below selection threshold or moved to a more conservative tier with sufficient floor.

STEP 0: TOR @ PHX — PHX won 120-98, margin 22, labeled BLOWOUT. TOR was on the losing side of a 22-point blowout. STEP 1: Barrett played 23 minutes, scored 13 PTS — active, below threshold by 2. The compressed minutes (23 in a blowout loss) are consistent with reduced fourth-quarter playing time as TOR trailed significantly. STEP 2: No injury event; NOT_LISTED at pick time. Classification: selection_error. STEP 3: The reasoning explicitly states 'VOLATILE + 7/10 at T15 — volatile_weak_combo skip fires; no iron_floor to invoke exception.' The tier_walk correctly identified the hard skip rule triggering, yet the pick appeared in the final output with human_verdict='keep.' This is a selection_error — the system's own logic disqualified this pick via hard skip, and retaining it constituted an override of an explicit rule without the required exception condition (iron_floor=false). STEP 4: Hit rate was 7/10 — the minimum threshold for the VOLATILE hard skip to fire at T15. Combined with VOLATILE tag, this is exactly the combination the skip rule was designed to prevent. A 7/10 hit rate means 3 failures in the last 10 games; with VOLATILE distribution, the miss margin risk is disproportionately large. STEP 5: The tier_walk documents the hard skip firing clearly. No higher tier was available (T20 not qualifying). The skip was the correct outcome; the error was in overriding it. STEP 6: No lineup_update present. The blowout-loss context (TOR lost by 22, Barrett played only 23 min) further validates that this pick had multiple structural disqualifiers stacked: VOLATILE + 7/10 + road underdog + blowout-loss game script. The hard skip rule exists precisely to prevent selection in these conditions. Root cause: Selection error — VOLATILE + 7/10 hard skip at T15 was correctly identified in the tier_walk but overridden by human_verdict='keep' without the required iron_floor exception condition. The blowout outcome reinforces that the skip rule was protective.

STEP 0: TOR @ PHX — PHX won 120-98, margin 22, labeled BLOWOUT. TOR was on the losing side of a 22-point blowout. This game-script context is shared with Barrett's picks — all TOR players faced blowout-loss compression in the second half. STEP 1: Ingram played 27 minutes, recorded 1 AST — active, below threshold by 1. The 27 minutes in a blowout loss is consistent with meaningful second-half reduction as TOR trailed heavily. STEP 2: No injury event; NOT_LISTED at pick time. Classification: model_gap_rule. STEP 3: The reasoning cited T2 AST=90% overall and 84% vs tough defenses, correctly identified the competitive game script assumption (spread_abs=2.5), and applied a -5% tough-defense penalty. However, the reasoning could not anticipate the actual 22-point blowout outcome from a 2.5-point pre-game spread. The critical gap: trend=down on AST combined with a road game on the losing side of an eventual blowout created a convergent suppression scenario that the pre-game confidence calculation did not fully price. STEP 4: Hit rate was 9/10 — a strong baseline — but T2 AST with trend=down in a blowout-loss game script produced exactly the near-miss pattern described in the MIN AST suppressor notes (miss margin of -1). STEP 5: The tier_walk shows T2 as the qualifying tier after standard overall=90%; T4 was not qualifying. No tier_skip_error. The T2 floor represents minimal cushion — one bad game (fewer half-court creation sequences in a blowout loss) is all that's needed for a miss. STEP 6: No lineup_update present. Root cause: The signal existed that TOR was playing a road game against PHX (tough defense) with Ingram showing trend=down on AST, but the rule for stacking road+tough+trend-down AST penalties was insufficiently aggressive — the -5% tough-defense penalty was applied but the additional -5% for trend=down road AST was not, nor was there a blowout-risk penalty for what turned out to be a 22-point loss. This is model_gap_rule: tighter stacking of road+tough+trend-down penalties on AST props (-10% total) would have pushed confidence below 70% and prevented selection. The pre-game spread of 2.5 did not trigger BLOWOUT_RISK, but the combination of factors warranted extra conservatism on a T2 AST pick with downward trend.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 3/3 sweep anchored by Randle's ultra-low REB floor and Barnes's 10/10 PTS consistency, with Barrett's T1 3PM exact-threshold hit completing the card.
parlay_lessons: The 'Sunday Floor Lock' succeeded, but it contained a structural fragility: the Barrett 3PM leg hit at exactly the threshold (1 actual vs 1 pick_value) — an exact-threshold hit is a HIT per grading rules, but this represents zero margin. In a parlay, any leg hitting at exactly the threshold with a VOLATILE tag and trend=down is a near-miss risk that the parlay survived on the minimum outcome. Future parlay construction should prefer legs where the player's floor is demonstrably above the threshold (e.g., Randle's 9 REB vs 4 threshold) rather than legs where exact-threshold outcomes are the most likely hit scenario. When a leg has VOLATILE tag + trend=down, consider whether the floor provides genuine cushion or merely a coin-flip on the threshold number.

Two of the three parlay legs came from the same TOR @ PHX game (Barnes PTS, Barrett 3PM) — this creates implicit correlation risk from game-script compression. PHX won by 22 (BLOWOUT), and both TOR players hit despite the blowout-loss context, but in a scenario where TOR's offense collapsed earlier in the game, both legs could have missed simultaneously. Multi-leg same-game stacks on the losing side of a potential blowout should be treated as correlated even when the props are ostensibly independent (PTS vs 3PM) — game-script suppression affects all counting stats for the losing team's players.
parlay_reinforcements: The Randle REB T4 anchor leg (pick_value=4, actual=9) demonstrated the correct use of an ultra-low floor prop as a parlay anchor: the rebounding prop was structurally insulated from the losing-side game script that suppressed Randle's PTS, and it hit by a 5-unit margin. Using a player's most game-script-resilient prop type (REB for a forward) as the anchor leg — rather than their highest-volume stat — is a validated parlay construction pattern that should be replicated when a player appears in a high-spread road underdog game.

The multi-game structure (MIN @ BOS + TOR @ PHX) provided genuine independence between the Randle REB leg and the two PHX legs: BOS winning by 10 and PHX winning by 22 were unrelated game outcomes, and Randle's rebounding volume was uncorrelated with either TOR player's performance. When building parlays across multiple games, combining a floor-anchored REB leg from one game with consistent-tag PTS and trend-supported 3PM legs from a separate game is a structurally sound approach that this card validated.

reinforcements: Depleted-opponent matchup stacking continued to deliver: Luka Doncic PTS 25 OVER vs. a DET squad missing Cunningham/Stewart/Sasser produced 32 PTS (39 min), and Banchero PTS 15 OVER vs. IND — the league's softest defense — exploded for 39 PTS in 37 min. When a primary scorer faces a confirmed-depleted opponent in a close game, the conservative low-threshold tier (T25 for Luka, T15 for Banchero) provided enormous cushion while still qualifying at the system's required hit rate.

Ultra-low AST floors (T4 at 4+ for both Luka and SGA) proved resilient across game scripts: Luka hit 6 AST in a close DET game and SGA hit 5 AST despite a confirmed blowout (OKC +20) where minute compression was a documented risk. The 85–90% hit rate at T4 AST for elite playmakers is validated as a durable signal even under blowout conditions, though the blowout cap correctly constrained confidence and kept these picks out of headline roles.

Iron-floor REB anchors at ultra-low thresholds (Barnes T4 REB, Bane T2 AST) provided clean hits on a B2B second night and a blowout spread respectively — both outcomes exceeded threshold by significant margins (Barnes 7 REB vs. 4 floor; Bane 7 AST vs. 2 floor). When iron-floor protection is confirmed at a low absolute threshold against a depleted frontcourt (UTAH missing Kessler/Nurkic), these function as near-locks and are ideal parlay anchors.

The system's human verdict trim/manual-skip decisions correctly identified the structurally marginal picks (SGA 3PM, SGA AST, Holmgren PTS, Banchero AST, Barnes PTS) — all five hit on the day, but the trim flags were process-correct given VOLATILE tags, blowout spreads, and B2B penalties. Do not update selection logic to include these types more aggressively; the hits on a single session do not override the structural reasons for the flags.

Luka's 3PM iron-floor pick (T3, actual = 3 — exact threshold hit) validated the HIT-on-exact-threshold grading rule and confirmed that iron-floor 3PM picks at low tiers can function as parlay anchors when the VOLATILE penalty is the only disqualifying factor. The system correctly classified this as a trim on process grounds (VOLATILE drops confidence below 75% floor), but the result reinforces that iron-floor protection at T3 for elite shooters with trend=up is statistically sound.
lessons: Five picks carried human verdict trim or manual_skip flags — all five hit — but this is a one-session sample and does not invalidate the trim logic. The structural risks flagged (VOLATILE 3PM with blowout spread, B2B second-night PTS suppression at T10, blowout cap on secondary scorer PTS) remain valid bases for downward confidence adjustment. The system should not relax VOLATILE or blowout-cap penalties based on a single clean session; these flags exist to protect against sessions where all five marginal picks miss simultaneously.

The Jalen Johnson DNP (confirmed OUT pre-game) was handled correctly by the voiding workflow for the individual pick, but his inclusion as a parlay leg in 'Soft Slate Scorers' created a structural parlay exposure that was not surfaced in the parlay rationale. The parlay hit because two legs covered and the parlay result was marked HIT despite a null leg — but the Parlay Agent should explicitly flag any leg where the player has a recent OUT history or a same-day injury designation, and require confirmation of availability before finalizing the parlay.

The session was concentrated in the 70–75% confidence band (8 of 11 picks), with zero picks in the 81%+ bands. While 100% hit rate at 70–75% is a strong session result, the calibration data now shows a structural gap: the system is not generating high-confidence picks (81%+) on most slates, likely because blowout caps and VOLATILE penalties are systematically pulling qualifying picks down into the 70–75% band. The Analyst should review whether the blowout spread cap thresholds (≥12 → 74% ceiling) are set too aggressively for primary scorers in matchups where the spread reflects opponent depletion rather than genuine garbage-time risk.
recommendations: For Luka Doncic multi-prop stacking (PTS + AST), continue to use the T25 PTS / T4 AST combination when DET, WAS, or similarly depleted opponents appear — the 87% vs-tough PTS rate and 94% vs-tough AST rate provide structural justification. Do not upgrade to T30 PTS without confirming the T30 tier hit rate independently; the T25 tier produced 32 actual PTS with 7 units of cushion, validating tier selection.

For Scottie Barnes on B2B second nights: the T4 REB iron-floor pick is the correct primary selection (83% B2B hit rate, confirmed vs. depleted frontcourts), but the T10 PTS pick should be systematically deprioritized when the B2B rate falls to 75% and a blowout spread applies simultaneously — the double penalty (B2B suppression + blowout game-script) is not fully offset by the consistent tag alone. If both conditions are present, require B2B rate ≥80% at the selected PTS tier before including.

For SGA and Holmgren in games where OKC is favored by 15+: the blowout cap correctly constrained confidence to 74%, but the trim flags on SGA AST and Holmgren PTS were process-justified despite the hits. Maintain the current blowout cap rules for these player types. Do not add PHI as a 'soft' opponent bonus that overrides the blowout structure — the OKC blowout (won by 20) confirmed the structural risk was real even though stats cleared the thresholds.

For Paolo Banchero AST picks: the VOLATILE tag combined with the T4 floor and a blowout spread (−13.5 as home favorite) created a correctly identified manual-skip. Banchero's 6 actual AST was a hit on the day, but the skip was process-correct given 53% post-miss hit rate on VOLATILE AST. Re-evaluate Banchero AST only when VOLATILE tag is absent, trend is up, and the spread is ≤8 points — do not chase a single-session result here.

For the Parlay Agent: before finalizing any parlay leg, require a confirmed 'NOT_LISTED' injury status check for all players — not just at the time of pick generation, but as a pre-lock verification step. Jalen Johnson's OUT designation was known pre-game and correctly voided the individual pick, but the parlay leg was not removed. Implement a rule: any leg where injury_status_at_check is anything other than NOT_LISTED at lock time must be automatically removed from the parlay and replaced or the parlay must be reduced to a shorter card.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 2/2 sweep — 'Monday Volume Sweep' hit 3/3 legs with each player exceeding threshold by significant margin, and 'Soft Slate Scorers' hit 2/2 gradeable legs despite Jalen Johnson's pre-game DNP rendering his leg null.
parlay_lessons: The 'Soft Slate Scorers' parlay included Jalen Johnson (ATL PTS) as a leg despite Johnson having a recent injury history — his OUT designation was confirmed pre-game and voided the individual pick, but the parlay was not correspondingly adjusted before lock. The Parlay Agent must implement a hard rule: any leg voided due to pre-game OUT status must trigger an automatic parlay revision (removal of that leg and re-evaluation of the card as a shorter parlay) rather than allowing a null leg to persist in the parlay structure. A 3-leg parlay that silently becomes a 2-leg parlay mid-day is a structural integrity failure even when the remaining legs hit.

Both parlays relied on a common game — OKC vs. PHI — appearing in separate cards (Holmgren PTS in 'Monday Volume Sweep', SGA AST in 'Soft Slate Scorers'). The same-game blowout risk was already flagged in both individual picks; if OKC had lost or the game been close, both legs could have missed simultaneously across two separate parlays, concentrating single-game exposure. The Parlay Agent should track cross-parlay game concentration and flag when the same game appears in multiple parlays on the same card.
parlay_reinforcements: The 'Monday Volume Sweep' three-leg structure (Luka PTS + Holmgren PTS + Barnes REB) worked because it combined a high-floor elite scorer (Luka T25, 8/10 hit rate), a low-absolute-threshold iron-floor pick (Barnes T4 REB, 9/10), and a secondary scorer in a depleted matchup (Holmgren T10 vs. PHI missing Embiid/Broome) — three independent games, three different prop types across scorer and rebounder roles. This heterogeneous multi-game structure with one ultra-low iron-floor anchor is the optimal parlay template: the anchor (Barnes REB 4+) insulates the card against game-script variance while the higher-ceiling legs drive payout.

Stacking a primary scorer's PTS against a confirmed depleted defense (Banchero T15 vs. IND, Luka T25 vs. depleted DET) with a heterogeneous third leg (SGA AST or Barnes REB) produced two separate winning parlays. The 'soft defense + depleted roster + conservative low-tier threshold' formula for the anchor scorer leg, combined with a structurally independent AST or REB leg as the third component, is validated as a repeatable parlay construction pattern — the scorer leg provides the statistical floor while the non-PTS leg decouples from game-script correlation.

reinforcements: Conservative blowout-tier stepping on LaMelo Ball (PTS T10, AST T2) proved correct: Ball delivered 20 PTS and 8 AST despite only 23 minutes, demonstrating that iron-floor thresholds on primary playmakers survive blowout minute compression — the T10/T2 floors were never threatened.

Banchero PTS T15 at 70% confidence on B2B underdog context was structurally sound: the pick correctly relied on the 80% B2B rate as the primary signal, and Banchero's 36-point output validated that primary scorer PTS floors hold even in adverse game-script contexts. The conservative underdog penalty was appropriate but not determinative.

Desmond Bane AST T2 iron-floor held cleanly at 3 AST despite B2B road underdog context — the decision to use T2 rather than T4 as the safe tier given the quant's confirmed best-tier designation was correct, and the iron-floor tag provided structural confidence that survived the underdog penalty.

3PM picks went 5/5 across the session, including three SKIP calls that would have degraded overall rate — the discipline to not force 3PM picks through blowout caps and VOLATILE penalties (Ball T3, Miller T2, Mitchell) was validated. Every live 3PM pick cleared its 75% minimum floor and hit.

Brandon Miller AST T2 (iron_floor, 9/10) and Kon Knueppel AST T2 (90% overall, 9/10 trend-up) both hit cleanly with 6 AST and 3 AST respectively in the CHA blowout, confirming that ultra-low AST floor picks on CHA secondary players are resilient in blowout wins where the offense operates freely in garbage time.
lessons: James Harden REB T4 missed (actual: 3 REB vs pick_value: 4) in a CLOSE game (CLE won by 5) — game script was not a blowout, meaning the standard blowout penalty applied during selection understated real variance risk. The 88% overall hit rate sounds strong, but in a close, contested game Harden's rebounding volume compresses as guard usage is focused on shot creation rather than board pursuit. REB props on guard-role facilitators (Harden averages well below center-level rebounding) deserve extra scrutiny even at low T4 thresholds — the 78% confidence was already at the exact floor, flagging marginal soundness.

Donovan Mitchell AST T4 missed (actual: 3 AST vs pick_value: 4) against tough ORL defense in a CLOSE game — the tier_walk correctly applied tough-defense and blowout penalties but still landed at 74%. In a CLOSE game where ORL's defense remained engaged all four quarters, Mitchell's creation sequences were disrupted below threshold. The 90% overall rate at T4 overstates true confidence when combined with tough defense and a co-creator (Harden) handling primary facilitation. When Mitchell and Harden share the floor, Mitchell's AST rate is more volatile — the without-Harden AST avg of 5.9 is not the operative baseline in games where Harden plays full minutes.

Kon Knueppel PTS T15 missed (actual: 14 PTS vs pick_value: 15) — the tier_walk correctly flagged SKIP under blowout_secondary_scorer rules at spread_abs=16.5, and the system's own reasoning classified this as a SKIP. The pick still appeared on the slate at 74% confidence (classified as SKIP in reasoning but included in the active pick count). The miss was by 1 point, consistent with the model's own pre-game concern: blowout minute compression for secondary scorers on the favored team. The SKIP classification in the tier_walk should have been a hard exclusion from active picks, not a soft trim. This is a model_gap_rule issue — the SKIP tag did not propagate to a voided pick status.

REB is the weakest prop type this session at 66.7% (2/3 hits) and both the Harden miss and the parlay miss trace to the same leg — guard REB props at T4 carry more intrinsic variance than the 88% historical rate suggests, particularly when the player is a secondary rebounder by role. The system's REB confidence floor of 78% is appropriately conservative, but the Harden pick was placed exactly at the floor after blowout penalty, leaving zero margin for game-script surprise.
recommendations: Introduce a hard exclusion rule: if the tier_walk field explicitly labels a pick 'SKIP' in its reasoning, the pick must not appear as an active pick regardless of a post-processor confidence correction. Knueppel PTS T15 was internally classified as SKIP due to blowout_secondary_scorer but still entered the active pick pool — this is a pipeline integrity gap. Add a SKIP-propagation check so any pick whose tier_walk contains 'SKIP' as its final determination is automatically voided before publication.

For guard/wing REB props at T4 (the system's minimum REB tier), require the player's raw REB average to be ≥5.0 before qualifying at 78% confidence. Harden's REB average as a guard-role player is structurally below center-level production — a T4 pick at the exact 78% floor leaves no buffer when close game scripts keep him in creation mode rather than rebounding mode. Apply a minimum raw_avg_reb ≥ 5.0 gate or step to a lower confidence acknowledgment (70%) for guards at T4.

When Mitchell and Harden both play, use the standard (with-both) AST averages rather than the without-Harden averages as the baseline for Mitchell AST picks. The without-Harden data (5.9 APG) is not operative when Harden is active and handling primary facilitation — the co-creator dynamic compresses Mitchell's AST toward 3–4 in a full-minutes Harden game. Add a co-creator AST compression flag: when two players sharing the floor both have raw_avgs_AST ≥ 4.0, apply an additional -5% confidence to the secondary playmaker's AST props at T4+.

For CLOSE game scripts (margin < 10 as established pre-game by spread_abs < 8 or confirmed post-game), apply an additional -5% penalty to guard AST props at T4+ against tough defenses. Mitchell AST T4 vs ORL (tough) in a spread_abs=10.5 game that ended at 5-point margin exemplifies the risk: tough defenses remain fully engaged in close games and suppress creation sequences throughout, unlike blowouts where the defense may soften late. The current model accounts for blowout risk but does not specifically penalize tight-spread AST picks vs. tough defenses.

Validate the 76-80% confidence band calibration — this session's 76-80% band hit rate was 66.7% (2/3) against an expected 78.0%, with both misses (Harden REB at 78%, Mitchell AST at 74% rounded up) falling in this range. The band is underperforming expected rate. In future sessions, picks in the 76-80% band should be treated with extra scrutiny equivalent to one tier step down, or the analyst should require a higher base hit rate (9/10 rather than 8/10) before assigning confidence in this band to avoid systematic over-placement in a calibration-weak zone.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0: ORL @ CLE final score 131-136, CLE won by 5 — a CLOSE game. Game script was competitive throughout, meaning ORL's defense remained fully engaged and there was no garbage-time context. STEP 1: Harden recorded 3 REB in 37 minutes — active and playing full starter minutes, clear miss by 1 rebound. STEP 2: No injury event or workflow gap. STEP 3: The original reasoning cited '88% overall REB T4 with 100% vs soft' and applied a blowout penalty to land at exactly 78% — the REB minimum confidence floor. The confidence was marginal by design. STEP 4: Hit rate of 8/10 overall is solid, but the pick was placed at the exact floor (78%) after blowout penalties, leaving zero buffer. In a CLOSE game rather than a blowout, the blowout penalty slightly overstated downside risk during selection, but did not fundamentally change the pick logic. STEP 5: The tier_walk correctly identified the T4 exact-match exemption and arrived at T4 as the sole viable tier — the walk was sound. A miss by 1 rebound on an 8/10 hit rate prop in a 5-point game is within normal variance. The 2-in-10 miss probability was always present, and a close competitive game where Harden prioritized creation over rebounding is a plausible miss scenario. No selection error is identified — this is the 1-in-5 outcome on a well-constructed pick at the confidence floor. No lineup_update amendment was present.

STEP 0: ORL @ CLE final score 131-136, CLE won by 5 — a CLOSE game. ORL's tough defense remained active all four quarters with no garbage-time softening. STEP 1: Mitchell recorded 3 AST in 35 minutes — active with full minutes, clear miss by 1 assist. STEP 2: No injury event or workflow gap. STEP 3: The original reasoning correctly applied tough-defense (-5%) and blowout (-10%) penalties, arriving at 74% from a 90% base. The thesis was structurally sound. STEP 4: Hit rate was 9/10 overall, but the tough-defense penalty was applied as a flat discount without capturing the specific co-creator dynamic: with Harden active (37 minutes, 7 AST) as the primary facilitator, Mitchell's assist opportunities are structurally compressed. The without-Harden AST average (5.9 APG) was used as context but the operative baseline in a full-Harden game is lower. STEP 5: The tier_walk correctly evaluated T4 as the best available tier (quant-confirmed), applied appropriate penalties, and landed at 74% — the walk was honest and conservative. The miss signal that was unavailable to the model is the co-creator AST suppression: the system lacks a computed field for how Mitchell's individual AST rate distributes when Harden is healthy and handling primary ball-handler duties. This is a model_gap_signal — no quant field exists for co-creator AST compression between two active co-facilitators. In a close game against a tough defense, 3 AST for Mitchell in a Harden-active game is consistent with the ceiling being lower than the 4.0 threshold. No lineup_update amendment was present.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 0/1 — the Harden REB leg was the single failure point in an otherwise clean same-game REB stack; a 1-rebound miss eliminated a +111 payout.
parlay_lessons: Guard REB props are the weakest anchor for a same-game REB parlay: Harden's role as a guard-facilitator means his rebounding volume is subject to game-flow compression in close games, where creation takes priority over board pursuit. Future same-game REB parlays should require all legs to have a raw_avg_reb ≥ 5.0 — legs built on guards or small forwards at T4 carry asymmetric miss risk that can sink an otherwise high-probability stack.

A three-leg same-game REB parlay stacking both teams' rebounders assumes that volume game script (high pace, competitive game) inflates all three simultaneously — but a 5-point final margin means game script was close, not a high-pace blowout. The correlation assumption ('volume game script inflates both teams' rebounding') was not validated by the final score; close game scripts concentrate rebounding in rim-running bigs rather than distributing to all players equally. Prefer same-game REB stacks in confirmed high-pace or blowout contexts rather than near-coin-flip spread games.

When building same-game parlays, avoid placing a pick whose standalone confidence (78%) is exactly at the prop-type floor — a floor-confidence leg signals that the system itself has minimal margin on that pick, and a parlay multiplies that marginal risk across all legs. Require all parlay legs to clear their prop-type minimum confidence floor by at least +5pp (i.e., REB legs should be ≥83%) to ensure each leg contributes genuine confidence rather than minimum-qualifying exposure.
parlay_reinforcements: The Banchero REB + Mobley REB same-team/cross-team REB correlation logic was validated: both hit cleanly (6 and 9 REB respectively), confirming that pairing a primary forward rebounder (Banchero, ORL) with a high-usage big (Mobley, CLE) in the same game creates genuine positive correlation — when the game is competitive and both teams run their normal rotations, big-man rebounding floors at T4 are independently robust and their co-occurrence probability is higher than independence would imply.

Iron-floor tagged legs (Bane AST, Miller AST) consistently delivered in straight picks on this slate — in future parlays, iron-floor AST T2 legs on consistent-tagged players represent the most reliable parlay anchors: the structural floor guarantees that even in adverse game scripts (blowout, B2B), the threshold is so low that variance rarely produces a miss. Consider replacing the guard REB anchor in future same-game stacks with an iron-floor AST T2 leg from the same game to reduce the weakest-link risk.

reinforcements: AST props on elite playmakers at conservative tiers (T2–T4) went 8/8 — the system's AST selection discipline, including correct application of the MIN suppressor exception at T2-level thresholds, is functioning at a high level. Ultra-low thresholds for high-usage playmakers (Harden T4, LeBron T4 iron_floor, Sengun T2, SGA T4) were the engine of the day.

The 70-75% confidence band went 11/11, confirming that the system's conservative floor-tier picks — where VOLATILE penalties, B2B step-downs, and suppressor discounts compress confidence — are well-calibrated. These lower-confidence picks are not marginal; they reflect sound methodology producing reliable outcomes.

The blowout-exemption logic for elite scorers (Luka PTS T25, SGA PTS T20) held up correctly: both hit comfortably despite game-script risk on the LAL side and a tough BOS defense. The FG_MARGIN_THIN step from T25 to T20 on SGA was conservative and correct given the 9% margin — SGA scored 33, validating that T20 was a sound floor.

Applying the Without-Star baseline for Julius Randle (Edwards OUT, n=14) as the primary baseline rather than overall season averages correctly elevated the PTS pick to T15 at 93%. The n≥10 threshold for treating Without-Star as primary was properly observed.

The 3PM card went 4/4 including two VOLATILE-penalized picks (KD, SGA) and one B2B step-down (Harden). The T1/T2 discipline for VOLATILE 3PM props — accepting the -5% confidence penalty rather than skipping — produced clean results on high-volume shooters.
lessons: Kevin Durant REB T4 missed at actual_value=3 (miss margin: -1) in HOU @ MIN, a 2-point game (CLOSE). The miss was not explained by game script — this was a close, full-minutes contest with no blowout compression. The root cause is the MIN rebounding environment: despite the MIN AST suppressor being documented, the system has no parallel MIN rebounding suppressor signal, and KD's 9/10 REB hit rate at T4 did not account for the possibility that MIN's frontcourt rebounding competition (active switching scheme, strong defensive rebounding) could suppress KD's counting stat even in a full-minutes game. The 9/10 rate provided false confidence at T4 for a road game vs. a strong defensive team without a specific REB DvP signal.

Scottie Barnes PTS T10 missed at actual_value=9 (miss margin: -1) in TOR @ LAC, a 25-point LAC blowout win. STEP 0: LAC won by 25, labeled BLOWOUT. Barnes was on the losing side. The pick's tier_walk did not apply a losing-side blowout penalty — the BLOWOUT_RISK field was not flagged (spread_abs=4.5 at pick time, suggesting the pre-game line did not anticipate a blowout). However, the system's blowout game-script logic for losing-side secondary scorers should have acknowledged that a TOR road underdog at LAC (spread_abs=4.5) carries meaningful blowout risk, and on the losing side even a consistent scorer can see Q4 minutes compressed. The 10/10 hit rate and 'consistent' tag created overconfidence at 84% for a pick that sat at exactly the T10 floor — a single reduced-minutes fourth quarter produced the miss. The top_pick flag on a road underdog with narrow threshold margin was too aggressive.
recommendations: Institute a losing-side blowout buffer for road underdogs on PTS props near the threshold floor: when spread_abs is 4–7 (moderate underdog), apply a precautionary -5% confidence reduction to any PTS pick where pick_value is within 3 points of the T-floor (i.e., a near-threshold pick). A single short fourth quarter on the losing side produces exactly the -1 margin miss seen with Barnes today. This would have reduced confidence from 84% to ~79% or prompted a tier step-down on Barnes PTS.

Develop a MIN REB context flag analogous to the MIN AST suppressor. Today's KD REB miss (actual=3 vs. pick=4) in a competitive HOU @ MIN game is consistent with a pattern where MIN's frontcourt competition and scheme suppress opposing big-man rebounding. Track REB hit rates vs. MIN in the H15 backtest to evaluate whether a formal suppressor signal (similar to the AST suppressor at −29.5pp) is emerging for REB props as well.

For PTS top_pick designations on road underdogs, require at least one of: (a) spread_abs < 3 (near-even game), (b) iron_floor tag, or (c) pick_value at least 5 points below the player's L10 average. Barnes PTS at T10 was exactly at the tier floor with no iron_floor tag, on the road as a meaningful underdog — the top_pick flag elevated it inappropriately. Tighten this gate.

When a player's REB prop is targeted vs. a team with a documented defensive scheme signal (MIN switching, BOS switching, MIL switching), apply the same scrutiny logic used for AST props vs. those teams — prefer one tier lower or require a higher hit-rate threshold. The MIN AST suppressor framework is the right template; extend it to REB for known frontcourt-competition teams even before a formal backtest confirmation.

The 81-85% confidence band underperformed today (75.0% actual vs. 83.0% expected), driven by the Barnes PTS miss. Review all picks in this band for the pattern of 'consistent tag + 10/10 hit rate + near-threshold pick value on a road underdog' — this combination may be systematically overconfident. The 70-75% band's 11/11 performance suggests the system's conservatism at lower confidence levels is correctly calibrated; the 81-85% band may benefit from an additional gate requiring either iron_floor or pick_value ≥ 5 points below the tier threshold before emitting above 80%.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0: TOR @ LAC final score 94–119, LAC won by 25 — BLOWOUT. Barnes was on the losing side (TOR). STEP 1: Barnes played (31 min confirmed in post-game news), scoring 9 PTS — active, not an injury or workflow event. STEP 2: No injury_exit in post-game news. Classified as model_gap_rule: the blowout game-script suppression on the losing side is a signal that existed pre-game in the spread (spread_abs=4.5, TOR road underdog at LAC), but the rule threshold for applying a losing-side blowout penalty only fires at spread_abs ≥ 8 (or higher tiers of concern). A moderate underdog on the road (4.5-point spread) can blow out to a 25-point margin, and when that occurs, the losing side's secondary scorers face Q4 compression that a 10/10 hit rate cannot fully protect against when pick_value is exactly at the tier floor (T10 = exactly 10). The tier_walk correctly identified a 10/10 at T10 with consistent and FG_HOT tags, but the combination of road underdog + threshold-floor pick + no iron_floor + moderate spread created a structural vulnerability that the rule did not penalize. The top_pick flag was too aggressive here. STEP 3: The analyst's reasoning ('10/10 at T10 with consistent tag; FG_HOT, mid LAC defense, no blowout risk, road underdog') correctly noted road underdog status but then used it neutrally (no blowout risk) rather than as a risk factor — the pre-game spread was insufficient to trigger a blowout flag, but the probability of a blowout was non-trivial for a road underdog. STEP 4: 10/10 hit rate at T10 is the strongest possible signal; the miss confirms that even a 10/10 rate cannot eliminate game-script risk when the pick is at the exact floor threshold. STEP 5: Tier walk selected T10 as the best qualifying tier — no higher tier was available; this was not a tier_skip error. The pick was at the maximum supportable tier. STEP 6: No lineup_update amendment present.

STEP 0: HOU @ MIN final score 108–110, MIN won by 2 — CLOSE game. No blowout compression applies. KD played 43 minutes (confirmed in post-game news) — full-minutes competitive game, game script is not the cause. STEP 1: KD played full minutes and registered actual REB=3. Active, full production context. STEP 2: No injury_exit. Classified as model_gap_signal: the miss occurred in a fully competitive, full-minutes game where KD recorded only 3 rebounds despite averaging 6+ REB in recent sample (9/10 hit rate at T4). The system has a documented MIN AST suppressor signal, but no parallel REB suppressor for MIN has been evaluated or formalized. MIN's switching scheme and strong frontcourt rebounding competition (Rudy Gobert, Julius Randle, et al.) can suppress opposing bigs' and wings' rebounding counts below their historical floors even in competitive, full-minutes games — this is a signal NBAgent does not currently compute. The miss required a team-level REB DvP signal for MIN that does not exist in the current quant framework. STEP 3: The analyst's reasoning ('KD is frontcourt player averaging 6+ REB in recent sample; competitive game ensures full minutes') was logically sound given available data — full minutes were confirmed, the game was competitive, and the T4 exact-match exemption correctly removed the 25th-percentile floor gate concern. What the reasoning could not account for was opponent-specific rebounding suppression. STEP 4: 9/10 hit rate at T4 is a strong baseline; this is a genuine low-probability miss rather than a predictable failure, but the MIN context makes it directionally explainable as a missing signal rather than pure variance. STEP 5: Tier walk evaluated T4 at 9/10 and applied the exact-match exemption correctly. No tier_skip concern. STEP 6: No lineup_update amendment present.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 1/2 parlay card — the Iron Assist Sweep swept cleanly on elite playmaker AST and SGA PTS volume, while the Wednesday Floor Lock was undone solely by KD's 3-rebound game in a pick that survived the competitive game script but not the MIN rebounding environment.
parlay_lessons: REB legs are the highest-variance prop type in the system and represent a structural fragility point in any parlay, even at conservative tiers like T4 with 9/10 hit rates. The KD REB leg failed not due to game script (43 min played in a 2-point game) but due to an opponent-specific suppression signal (MIN frontcourt competition) that the system does not yet compute. Until a MIN REB suppressor is formally evaluated, the Parlay Agent should treat REB legs vs. MIN as elevated-risk anchors and either avoid them in parlays or require a 10/10 hit rate with iron_floor before inclusion.

When a parlay contains a REB leg for a wing or forward (non-center) vs. a team with a documented defensive identity (MIN switching, BOS switching), apply the same extra-scrutiny logic used for AST legs vs. confirmed suppressors — consider stepping to a lower tier than the system's best qualifying tier or replacing the REB leg with an AST or 3PM leg from the same player if available. The Wednesday Floor Lock would have survived at 3/3 if KD's leg had been AST T2 (which hit at actual=8) instead of REB T4.
parlay_reinforcements: The Iron Assist Sweep's structure — two ultra-conservative AST floor legs (LeBron T4 iron_floor, Sengun T2) anchoring a legitimate high-confidence scorer leg (SGA PTS T20) — is the ideal parlay template: anchor with iron-floor or near-certain statistical floors, then add one quality scorer leg rather than stacking multiple volatile props. All three legs hit comfortably (LeBron 9 AST, Sengun 3 AST, SGA 33 PTS), validating that floor-lock AST combinations provide a reliable parlay foundation.

Multi-game parlay structures with fully independent legs across three separate games (no same-game correlation) provided clean diversification: when the KD REB miss occurred in the Wednesday Floor Lock, it was a single-game event that did not cascade to the other legs. Independent multi-game construction is the correct structural choice for 3-leg parlays at this confidence level (~49-50% implied joint probability), as it prevents a shared game-script event (blowout, pace variance) from collapsing multiple legs simultaneously.

reinforcements: Blowout-capped picks still delivered: all three Banchero props (PTS T10, REB T4, AST T4) were capped at 74% due to the -15.5 spread vs. depleted SAC, yet Banchero posted 30 PTS / 9 REB / 7 AST in 38 minutes — reinforcing that the blowout cap is a calibration tool, not a kill switch, and that elite primary scorers on favored teams against soft defenses will frequently clear even modest thresholds comfortably.

Iron-floor AST props at T2 were the most reliable structural play of the session: Desmond Bane AST T2 (90% hit rate, HIT 6), Brandon Miller AST T2 (100% hit rate, HIT 3), and LaMelo Ball AST T4 (95% hit rate, HIT 6) all cleared with room to spare. Low-threshold AST picks on primary ball-handlers and active secondary playmakers in competitive games remain a high-floor selection strategy.

The depleted-opponent stack logic proved correct: three CHA players (Ball PTS, Ball AST, Knueppel AST, Miller PTS, Miller AST, Miller 3PM) were selected against NYK missing key playmakers in a competitive H game (CHA won by 11). All six CHA picks hit, validating the approach of concentrating picks on the favored-by-usage side when opponent personnel is depleted.

Kon Knueppel AST T2 at 78% (9/10 hit rate, trend up) hit 8 assists — a 6-unit clearance over the T2 threshold. The upward trend tag on a secondary playmaker in a competitive home game with reduced opponent creation pressure is a reliable signal worth continuing to target.

Brandon Miller's 3PM iron-floor VOLATILE T2 pick cleared at 75% confidence (HIT 4), demonstrating that iron-floor status on a VOLATILE 3PM prop with a stable trend can still produce in a competitive game context — the iron floor provided the structural floor even with the VOLATILE penalty applied.
lessons: No misses this session — no analytical failures to log. However, three picks were human-flagged as trims: Banchero PTS/REB (blowout + minutes compression risk on a -15.5 spread), Banchero AST (VOLATILE tag compounding blowout script risk), LaMelo Ball PTS (minutes floor 21.8 below the 24-minute PTS safety threshold), and Miller 3PM (VOLATILE + exact minimum 7/10 threshold clearance). All four hit comfortably — Banchero played 38 minutes in what was ultimately a 4-point game, Ball scored 22 in 36 minutes, and Miller hit 4 threes. The SAC vs ORL final was 121-117 (CLOSE, not a blowout), which invalidated the -15.5 pre-game spread blowout concern retroactively. This is a calibration reminder: spread-based blowout penalties are pre-game risk adjustments, not deterministic outcomes — a tight final score on a large spread game is within normal variance and the underlying tier quality still drives results.

The proj_min=0 flag on Karl-Anthony Towns triggered a precautionary -5% confidence reduction in the tier walk, landing at 74%. Towns played 22 minutes and scored 13 points (HIT). The proj_min=0 field appears to be a data artifact or placeholder rather than a true projection — future runs should document this field's behavior more precisely and avoid applying ad-hoc confidence penalties when the player is NOT_LISTED on the injury report and full quant data is present. A formal rule clarifying when proj_min=0 should trigger a skip vs. a penalty vs. no action would prevent inconsistent handling.
recommendations: Formalize the proj_min=0 handling rule: when a player shows proj_min=0 in their quant header but is NOT_LISTED on the injury report and full hit-rate/tier data is populated, do NOT apply an ad-hoc confidence penalty. Instead, flag the field as a data artifact and proceed on injury report status alone. If proj_min=0 genuinely indicates non-participation, it should surface as an OUT in the injury report — that is the authoritative pipeline source. This session's KAT pick showed the penalty was unnecessary.

Maintain the AST iron-floor T2 strategy as a core selection pattern: Bane (9/10, T2), Miller (10/10, T2), and Ball (9/10, T4) all hit with substantial clearance. Low-threshold AST picks on primary and secondary playmakers in competitive games with soft-to-mid opponent AST defense are among the system's most reliable prop types. Continue prioritizing these when qualifying, especially in same-game stacks.

Revisit the Banchero blowout AST pick classification: the VOLATILE tag on a T4 AST prop in a -15.5 spread game correctly triggered a human skip recommendation, but the actual game finished as a 4-point CLOSE game (ORL 121, SAC 117). The pre-game spread did not materialize into a blowout. Going forward, when the VOLATILE tag and blowout cap combine to compress confidence below 72%, consider whether the pick should be skipped entirely at selection time rather than carried at reduced confidence — the risk-adjusted value of a 70% VOLATILE AST in a projected blowout is marginal, and the slot is better used on a cleaner prop.

Monitor the ORL vs. SAC game-script calibration: the pre-game spread was -15.5 favoring ORL, yet the final margin was only 4 points (SAC covered by a wide margin). This is a notable spread miss — the system applied blowout caps to three Banchero props based on a spread that did not reflect the actual game. Over time, tracking pre-game spread vs. actual margin calibration will help quantify how often large spreads vs. depleted opponents (SAC is 19-55, eliminated) produce genuine blowouts vs. competitive games, which should inform the blowout cap threshold for opponent-quality-adjusted spreads.

For the next CHA slate, continue targeting Brandon Miller AST T2 as a structural anchor: 10/10 iron-floor consistent tag, 3 assists again tonight (HIT), upward trend. This prop has the highest structural confidence on the CHA roster and should be a default consideration whenever Miller is active in a home game with mid-or-softer AST defense opposition.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 1/1 sweep — the Hornets Assist Anchor same-game stack hit all three legs with LaMelo (6 AST), Knueppel (8 AST), and Miller (21 PTS) clearing their thresholds comfortably in CHA's 11-point competitive win over NYK.
parlay_lessons: The structural risk on this parlay was concentration: all three legs came from the same team (CHA) in the same game, meaning a single game-script disruption — foul trouble for Ball, a blowout loss collapsing starters' minutes, or a NYK defensive adjustment suppressing creation — would have voided the correlation benefit and killed all three legs simultaneously. Same-game stacks amplify both upside and downside through shared game-script exposure; the Parlay Agent should always note the single-point-of-failure risk when all legs share one team, and consider whether a mixed-game hedge leg could reduce catastrophic miss risk on future 3-leg same-team stacks.
parlay_reinforcements: The feeder-target-scorer correlation structure worked precisely as designed: LaMelo Ball (feeder, 6 AST) distributed to Knueppel (target, 8 AST) and Miller (scorer, 21 PTS) within the same offensive system, and the depleted NYK defense (missing key playmakers) created the open creation lanes the stack required. Same-game stacks built on a primary ball-handler as feeder with secondary playmakers as targets are the system's most validated parlay structure — this result adds another data point confirming the logic.

Pairing an iron-floor AST leg (Miller AST T2, 100% hit rate) with a high-confidence feeder leg (Ball AST T4, 95% hit rate) as the parlay foundation and using the scorer leg (Miller PTS T15, 85% hit rate) as the ceiling-chasing third leg is a structurally sound 3-leg construction: the two AST legs provide a near-certain correlation floor while the PTS leg adds implied odds value. This leg-ordering approach — anchor on iron-floor props, chase with the scoring prop — should be the default same-game stack template for CHA games going forward.

reinforcements: IND amplifier effect validated again: Kawhi Leonard PTS T25 (stepped to T20) hit at 28 actual vs 25 threshold, and Kawhi AST T2 hit at 4 actual vs 2 threshold — both in a LAC-IND game that finished 114-113 (CLOSE), confirming the IND amplifier operates even in non-blowout scripts where game-script suppression is minimal. Picking conservative tiers against IND and letting the matchup do the work is the correct structural approach.

Low-tier AST floors (T2) proved extremely robust across all five AST picks: SGA hit 5 vs 4, Kawhi hit 4 vs 2, Harden hit 14 vs 4, Durant hit 10 vs 2, Sengun hit 7 vs 2. All five cleared comfortably. In particular, the T2 AST picks on Durant and Sengun hit despite -13.5 blowout spread caps reducing confidence — the 74% cap was appropriate discipline, but the underlying signal (10/10 and 10/10 hit rates respectively) was genuinely strong. Conservative T2 AST floors on high-usage players in soft matchups are validated as a reliable pick type.

The Harden multi-prop stack (PTS T15 + REB T4 + AST T4 + 3PM T2) swept 4/4 props in a 21-point CLE blowout win. Harden played 30 minutes and hit all four thresholds comfortably (17 PTS, 5 REB, 14 AST, 2 3PM). Stacking multiple props on a primary ball-handler in a favorable blowout script — where the player stays on the floor long enough to accumulate across all categories — produced a clean sweep. The vs_soft split anomaly on PTS (78% vs 90% overall) flagged by the Analyst was correctly moderated but did not materialize as a miss.

Scottie Barnes PTS T10 hit 23 actual in a TOR home win over NOP (TOR won by 13, COMPETITIVE), validating that the 100% blowout-game rate translates to non-blowout scripts as well. The human trim flag referenced a prior session miss in a different game context; today's HOME favorite game script produced a comfortable margin. The T10 threshold at 90% hit rate on a COMPETITIVE game is a structurally sound pick type for Barnes.

Nikola Jokic REB T10 (stepped down from T12 via VOLATILE tag and 25th-pct gate) hit 15 actual vs 10 threshold in a 6-point DEN win over UTAH (CLOSE). The step-down rule from T12 to T10 was conservative and correct — the VOLATILE tag and 25th-pct gate combination forced a disciplined tier reduction that still cleared comfortably. The VOLATILE step-down mechanism is functioning as intended: it reduces tier without eliminating the pick.
lessons: All 13 picks hit and 0 misses were recorded — no analytical failures to log. However, human auditors flagged 8 of 13 picks as 'trim' or 'manual_skip', meaning the pick card had meaningful structural concerns that happened not to manifest as misses today. The most concentrated risk cluster was the HOU@MEM picks (KD AST T2, Sengun PTS T10, Sengun AST T2) — all three were flagged for blowout + min_floor fragility. All three hit, but HOU won by only 10 (COMPETITIVE, not a full blowout), which likely preserved Sengun's minutes near his floor. Had HOU won by 25+, Sengun's 33 minutes and 14 PTS suggest the blowout risk was real but didn't trigger. The system should track whether min_floor < 24 picks survive competitive vs. actual blowout games at different rates.

The Sengun PTS T10 pick was flagged 'manual_skip' by the human auditor despite hitting 14 actual vs 10 threshold. The pick was structurally sound on the outcome but the manual_skip classification reflects legitimate pre-game concern: VOLATILE tag + min_floor 21.6 + spread_abs 13.5 is a three-factor fragility stack that the system should consider formalizing as a hard skip rule rather than leaving to human override. If the game had become a true blowout (margin ≥20), Sengun's floor would have been genuinely threatened. Consider adding: if VOLATILE=true AND min_floor < 22 AND spread_abs ≥ 13, skip PTS prop regardless of hit rate.

Five of eight 70-75% confidence picks hit (100% actual vs 72.5% expected) — a significant positive variance session. Over a small sample, this is expected noise. The system should not recalibrate confidence floors upward based on a single 100% session; the confidence band distribution (all picks clustered in 70-80% range, zero picks in 81%+) reflects appropriate spread_abs cap discipline. Maintaining the cap structure is validated — the caps are doing their job of preventing overconfidence, not suppressing good picks.
recommendations: Formalize the VOLATILE + min_floor + spread_abs triple-fragility skip rule: if a player carries VOLATILE tag AND min_floor < 22 AND spread_abs ≥ 13 on a PTS prop, treat as a hard skip rather than a human-override 'manual_skip'. The Sengun PTS T10 case today is the prototype — the pick hit because HOU won by only 10 (COMPETITIVE), but the structural conditions for a miss were all present. Codify this to reduce dependency on human auditor review.

Continue applying the IND amplifier aggressively across all prop types when the game context is CLOSE or COMPETITIVE — today's LAC@IND result (114-113, CLOSE) confirmed the amplifier fires even in tight games where blowout-side suppression does not apply. In future IND matchups, consider whether the system's blowout penalty (-10%) should be offset more strongly by the IND amplifier (+14.3pp historically) when spread_abs < 10, potentially netting a smaller penalty than the standard rule applies.

The Harden AST T4 pick (actual: 14) and KD AST T2 pick (actual: 10) both far exceeded their thresholds — these players are generating assists well above the conservative tier floors. For primary ball-handlers with 10/10 AST hit rates at T2 (Durant, Sengun), consider whether T4 would also qualify (≥70% hit rate) and whether the higher tier offers better value with comparable confidence. Walking the tier further up before capping could improve expected value without increasing miss risk meaningfully.

The Barnes PTS T10 human trim flag referenced a prior-session miss in a blowout game, but today's COMPETITIVE game script (TOR won by 13) produced 23 actual — nearly 2.3x the threshold. When Barnes is a HOME favorite and the game script is not a blowout (spread_abs < 15), the T10 PTS floor appears highly durable. Separate Barnes PTS threshold analysis by home/away and blowout/competitive — the prior miss was likely a blowout road game, which is a structurally different pick.

The Jokic REB VOLATILE step-down from T12 to T10 hit 15 actual — the step-down was correct process but the outcome suggests T12 would also have cleared. However, this does not mean the step-down rule was wrong; the VOLATILE tag exists precisely because Jokic's REB distribution is wide enough that even an 8/10 rate at T12 carries meaningful miss risk. Do not loosen the VOLATILE 25th-pct gate based on single-session outcomes. Instead, track VOLATILE step-down picks over 20+ games to measure whether the actual hit rate at the stepped-down tier exceeds the projected rate (indicating the gate is slightly too conservative) or matches it (indicating correct calibration).

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 1/1 parlay sweep — all three legs cleared comfortably with Harden posting 17 PTS, Barnes 23 PTS, and Kawhi recording 4 AST against IND's amplifier defense.
parlay_lessons: The parlay's structural risk was its reliance on two PTS props (Harden T15 and Barnes T10) in games with BLOWOUT_RISK flags applied at pick time. Both games ended COMPETITIVE or BLOWOUT (CLE won by 21, TOR won by 13), which preserved minutes for both players — but if CLE's blowout had triggered heavy Q4 rest for Harden earlier in the game, his 30-minute line could have compressed his PTS below 15. Multi-leg parlays with two blowout-risk PTS legs on the same card should carry an explicit joint-risk note: if both favored teams blow out their opponents, both PTS legs face simultaneous minute-compression pressure, doubling the correlated downside.

The Kawhi AST T2 leg was the lowest-threshold, highest-hit-rate leg (9/10, 74% after blowout cap) and served as a structural stabilizer — in a close LAC@IND game (114-113), Kawhi played 35 minutes with no blowout-side suppression, recording 4 AST. Using a low-tier, high-hit-rate AST leg from an independent game as the anchor of a parlay that also contains two moderate-tier PTS legs is a validated combination structure: the AST leg provides near-certain floor support while the PTS legs carry the expected value. Replicate this structure — two PTS legs from separate games with ≥8/10 hit rates, anchored by a low-tier AST prop from a third independent game.
parlay_reinforcements: The three-leg cross-game structure (Harden PTS, Barnes PTS, Kawhi AST from three separate games) eliminated correlated game-script risk: no single game outcome could have simultaneously sunk more than one leg. This multi-game independence is the most important structural feature for 3-leg parlays — avoid stacking two legs from the same game unless the correlation is strongly positive (e.g. same team's primary scorer + primary playmaker in a confirmed high-pace matchup).

Pairing a primary ball-handler's volume PTS prop (Harden T15, 9/10 hit rate) with his same-game secondary prop (REB T4 or AST T4) in a favorable blowout script is a validated same-player multi-prop approach — Harden swept all four of his individual props today. For future parlay construction, consider a Harden PTS + AST two-leg same-game mini-parlay as a compact high-confidence anchor: both props have ≥9/10 hit rates, and in a soft-defense blowout game, the minute-compression risk is distributed across both stats simultaneously rather than isolated to one.

reinforcements: Wembanyama REB at T8 (10/10 hit rate) hit comfortably with 15 actual vs. 8 threshold even in a SA blowout win (+32 margin) — validating the Wembanyama blowout exemption for REB props and confirming that his REB floor holds even when minutes are reduced (31 min logged). The 10/10 consistency at T8 with a soft opposing frontcourt after Kuzma's scratch proved to be sound construction.

Stephon Castle AST at T4 (9/10 hit rate) crushed at 10 actual vs. 4 threshold in a blowout context, reinforcing that Castle's primary playmaker role and AST volume are robust even when SA builds large leads early — his assist output is game-script-resilient because the offense runs through him regardless of margin. The 95% hit rate at T4 with a soft MIL defense was the correct anchor for this pick.

Julius Randle PTS + AST dual-prop structure in the MIN vs. DET matchup (spread 2.5, CLOSE game) was correctly identified as a low-blowout-risk opportunity; both legs hit (11 PTS, 4 AST vs. 10 and 2 thresholds) in a game MIN won by 22. The usage elevation rationale — Edwards and McDaniels both OUT — translated directly into production despite facing a tough DET defense (95% vs. tough confirmed valid).

The Nickeil Alexander-Walker PTS at T15 (8/10 hit rate, B2B rate 92%) delivered 27 actual vs. 15 threshold in a COMPETITIVE ATL win (+10), validating both the blowout-resilient B2B pick structure and the lineup amendment that flagged upside from Daniels and Okongwu being OUT. Usage redistribution to NAW in a soft SAC matchup was the correct read.

De'Aaron Fox PTS at T10 (9/10 hit rate) hit at 12 actual vs. 10 threshold in the SA blowout win, with only 21 minutes played — confirming the system's caution about Fox's workload management risk was warranted but the conservative T10 threshold provided sufficient buffer even with compressed minutes. The spread cap to 74% was appropriate given the minutes risk realized.
lessons: The SA vs. MIL blowout (+32 final margin) validated all three human verdicts flagging blowout risk on SA players: Wembanyama REB hit easily despite the spread, Fox PTS hit at 12 with only 21 minutes (T10 buffer was essential — T25/T20/T15 would have been vulnerable), and Castle PTS hit at 22 with 29 minutes but was correctly marked manual_skip given VOLATILE tag and fragility stack. The system's blowout-tier step-down logic (forcing T10 rather than T15+) was the key protective mechanism — reinforce this as confirmed effective.

Castle PTS manual_skip by human review proved to be the right call philosophically even though the pick hit (22 actual vs. 10 threshold) — the VOLATILE + spread_abs≥15 + min_floor<24 fragility stack should continue to be treated as a structural skip regardless of outcome, because a single missed night with Castle benched early in Q4 of a blowout would have produced a clean miss. One hit does not invalidate the skip rule.

Fox PTS trim (21 min played in a +32 blowout) is strong confirmation that the trim verdict was structurally sound — Fox as a trade acquisition on a blowout-prone team is the highest workload-management risk on the roster, and the system correctly identified this even though the pick ultimately hit on the conservative T10 threshold. The trim signal on Fox PTS in large-spread SA games should be formalized.
recommendations: Formalize the Fox PTS blowout-management rule for SA games: when SA spread_abs≥15, De'Aaron Fox PTS should be capped at T10 or below AND flagged as a trim candidate regardless of hit rate — 21 minutes in this game confirms he is the primary rest candidate when SA builds a large lead. Do not select Fox PTS at T15+ in any SA game with spread_abs≥13.

Reinforce the Wembanyama blowout exemption for REB specifically: 15 REB in 31 minutes in a +32 blowout is the clearest possible validation that his REB floor (T8, 10/10) is structurally insulated from game-script compression. The amendment flagging upside after Kuzma's scratch (revised to 83%) was directionally correct — future SA blowout games against depleted frontcourts should trigger an upward amendment on Wembanyama REB confidence toward the spread cap.

The Castle AST at T4 hitting 10 assists (2.5x threshold) in a blowout confirms that Castle's playmaking is so deeply embedded in SAS offensive structure that even garbage-time minutes produce assist volume. Consider whether the spread cap (74%) is too conservative for Castle AST in SA blowout games — the underlying 95% hit rate at T4 with 10/10 blowout_game coverage may justify a higher floor confidence when spread_abs is 15-20 specifically for AST (not PTS, where the VOLATILE tag remains a valid suppressor).

The Randle dual-prop structure (PTS + AST) in a CLOSE-spread game with key absences (Edwards, McDaniels OUT) hit cleanly at conservative thresholds (T10 PTS, T2 AST). This pattern — star player on short-handed team, low spread, tough opponent with confirmed vs_tough≥90% — is the system's cleanest construction type. Prioritize this structure in future slates when a team's primary scorer faces usage elevation from confirmed absences with spread_abs<5.

The NAW PTS pick hitting 27 actual vs. 15 threshold after the Daniels + Okongwu double-absence lineup amendment (revised to 79%) confirms that same-team dual-absence triggers are highly valuable upside signals for secondary scorers on soft-defense opponents. When two rotation players from the same team are scratched on the same day and the surviving scorer has a B2B rate≥90% at T15, treat the lineup amendment upward revision as sufficient justification to keep the pick even under blowout-spread conditions.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 3/3 sweep — the Spurs MIL Floor Lock hit all legs comfortably with actual values of 15 REB, 10 AST, and 11 PTS against thresholds of 8, 4, and 10 respectively.
parlay_lessons: The parlay's structural risk was concentration on two SA legs (Wembanyama REB + Castle AST) in a game with spread_abs=17.5 — both legs were individually capped to 74-83% confidence by the blowout rule, meaning the correlation assumption (same-game, same-team, large spread) concentrated blowout failure risk. Had SA's big lead triggered deep bench rest in Q3 rather than Q4, both SA legs could have missed simultaneously. Future same-game parlays with spread_abs≥15 should consider whether the correlated blowout risk between two legs on the favored team effectively creates a hidden negative correlation — one miss scenario (early garbage time) kills both legs at once, which is a structural fragility not captured by treating the legs as 'independent within the same game context.'

The cross-game independence leg (Randle PTS from MIN vs. DET) served its intended function as a true diversifier — the MIN game was CLOSE (DET won by 22, MIN lost) but Randle still hit 11 PTS in 31 minutes, confirming that the T10 conservative threshold on a usage-elevated primary scorer in a close-spread game is the ideal anchor leg for a parlay with correlated high-spread legs elsewhere.
parlay_reinforcements: The 'soft-matchup floor lock' parlay structure — anchoring on a dominant player's conservative counting-stat threshold (Wembanyama REB T8, 10/10) paired with their primary playmaker's equally conservative threshold (Castle AST T4, 9/10) in the same favorable matchup — proved structurally sound: both legs crushed their thresholds by wide margins (15 vs. 8, 10 vs. 4), confirming that same-game stacking on a dominant favorite with verified high hit rates at conservative tiers creates genuine parlay floor value even after blowout caps.

Adding a fully independent third leg from a different game (Randle PTS, different opponent, different game script) as the parlay's 'swing leg' successfully diversified away from the SA blowout correlation risk while contributing independent upside — this cross-game independence structure (two correlated same-game legs + one fully independent leg) is the correct parlay architecture when the anchor game has spread_abs≥15 and blowout risk is acknowledged.

reinforcements: Low-threshold PTS and REB anchors (T10 and T4) with 9/10 hit rates delivered clean floors across all games — Barnes PTS T10 (23 actual), Kawhi REB T4 (8 actual), SGA AST T4 (4 actual exact), and KD REB T4 (4 actual exact) all hit, confirming that conservative tier selection at high hit rates is the session's most reliable pattern.

The IND amplifier context delivered again: Adebayo PTS T10 hit for 15, Herro PTS T10 hit for 31, and Adebayo AST T2 hit for 3 in a game IND won by 17 — even in a competitive loss for MIA, the IND soft-defense amplifier continued to boost visiting scorers' counting stats, validating the amplifier rule for offensive props.

Multi-prop stacking on a single player worked well when backed by consistent tags and low threshts: KD's four-prop stack (PTS/REB/AST/3PM) went 3/4, with the sole miss being the 3PM VOLATILE pick — the non-VOLATILE legs (REB and AST) both hit exactly at threshold, confirming that consistent-tagged low-tier props on the same player are the reliable legs of any stack.
