miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0 — Game context: CHA @ MIN finished 122-108, CHA won by 14 — labeled COMPETITIVE. This was not a blowout and CHA won, so game-script blowout compression does not explain the miss. Miller played 34 minutes (confirmed via post-game news), ruling out DNP or early exit as a causal factor. STEP 1 — Player was active with 34 minutes played; actual_value=7 is a genuine floor failure, not a minutes issue. STEP 2 — No injury exit in post-game news. Pre-game injury_status_at_check = NOT_LISTED. Classification: variance. The pick was made at 8/10 hit rate (iron_floor, T15) with a VOLATILE tag; VOLATILE -5% was applied and confidence was set conservatively at 74%. An 8/10 iron_floor pick implies one miss in ten outcomes is within normal expectation, and this represents that miss. The miss margin is severe (8 points below threshold) which is consistent with the VOLATILE distribution — VOLATILE picks by definition carry fat-tail downside risk that can produce large misses even when the floor 'should' hold. STEP 3 — The original reasoning ('Iron_floor PTS T15 at 80%; VOLATILE -5% applied; solid floor with CHA hot team context') correctly applied the VOLATILE penalty but did not escalate the risk further. The reasoning noted the iron_floor and applied the standard VOLATILE discount; however it did not flag that VOLATILE + iron_floor is a potentially unstable combination (as exists in the 3PM incompatibility rule). STEP 4 — Hit rate was 8/10 (80%), making this the statistically expected 20% miss; the VOLATILE tag signals precisely this kind of floor collapse. STEP 5 — Tier walk shows T15 at 80% overall with iron_floor; no higher-confidence PTS tier was available that would have avoided this outcome — the miss is not a tier selection error. STEP 6 — No lineup_update sub-object present; no amendment was triggered. Bottom line: sound pick within stated parameters; VOLATILE tag correctly flagged floor risk; one-in-five miss on an 8/10 iron_floor pick is within expected variance, though the severity of the miss (-8 pts) reinforces the need to review whether VOLATILE overrides iron_floor on PTS as it does on 3PM.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 3/3 sweep on the Volume Game Sweeper — all three legs hit decisively with actual values of 26 PTS (Brown), 26 PTS (Randle), and 15 AST (LeBron) against thresholds of 20, 10, and 4.
parlay_lessons: The parlay's most dangerous structural risk was the Jaylen Brown PTS leg (74% confidence, VOLATILE tag, FG_MARGIN_THIN step-down, iron_floor) — this leg had the weakest individual confidence of the three and was flagged as a 'trim' by human verdict; had Brown shot cold (as Miller did in the same session), the entire parlay would have failed on its softest leg. When constructing multi-leg parlays with a mix of 74% and 82% confidence legs, the Parlay Agent should note that a single VOLATILE-tagged leg anchored at 74% creates meaningful tail risk for the combo product (~50% implied odds) — consider requiring all parlay legs to be ≥76% or flagging VOLATILE-tagged legs as parlay_risk in the rationale.
parlay_reinforcements: The multi-game structure with three independent games (BOS vs TOR, MIN vs CHA, LAL vs DAL) eliminated cross-game correlation risk entirely — no single game outcome could cascade across legs, and each leg was driven by distinct player-level factors (Tatum return context for Brown, Edwards OUT for Randle, Doncic/Reaves OUT for LeBron). Independent multi-game parlays anchored by absence-driven usage concentration are a confirmed winning structure and should be prioritized when 2+ games feature confirmed key-player absences on the same slate.

Pairing an iron_floor AST leg (LeBron T4 iron_floor, 82%) with two high-volume PTS legs in games with confirmed absences proved highly effective — the AST leg acted as a near-lock anchor (actual 15 vs threshold 4) while the PTS legs provided upside. The AST-as-parlay-anchor pattern using iron_floor T4+ picks in games with missing primary creators is validated and should be repeated when the structural conditions align.

reinforcements: Mandatory FG_MARGIN_THIN tier step-downs proved structurally sound: both Jokic (T20→T15, actual 35 PTS) and Wembanyama (T20→T15, actual 17 PTS) hit comfortably after being stepped down, confirming the rule correctly adds buffer rather than sacrificing edge.

Conservative AST picks at T2 with 100% hit rates remain elite anchors: CJ McCollum's 10/10 AST pick at T2 (actual 6 AST vs pick of 2) crushed the threshold, validating that iron-floor low-tier AST picks in competitive games are the highest-reliability prop type in the system.

Blowout-resilient PTS profiles continue to hold in SAS blowout contexts: Stephon Castle's T10 PTS pick (actual 19, blowout_resilient tag applied) hit cleanly despite a 13-point SA win, confirming the blowout-resilient offset rule correctly distinguished his profile from non-resilient players in the same game.

min_floor + FG_MARGIN_NEG double step-down cascade to T10 added meaningful safety: Castle's PTS pick benefited from both rules firing on the same tier step, producing a generous 9-point cushion (actual 19 vs pick 10) — the cascade does not over-penalize when the player's true floor is materially higher.

The tough-matchup confidence reduction for Jalen Johnson (85%→75% via vs_tough degradation) did not prevent a clean hit: actual 21 PTS vs pick 15 with 41 minutes played confirms that when 9/10 hit rates at a tier are genuine, even tough defense adjustments leave the pick viable.
lessons: Wembanyama's AST miss (actual 1 vs pick 2, 9/10 hit rate) in a COMPETITIVE game (SA won by 13) with only 16 minutes played is the key signal: 16 minutes is extremely low for Wembanyama even in a moderately comfortable win, suggesting minutes were compressed mid-game for reasons not captured in pre-game signals. The 9/10 historical hit rate at T2 remains strong, but the combination of a blowout-trending game (13-point margin qualifies as COMPETITIVE, not BLOWOUT) and unusually low minutes (16 vs his ~30.1 avg) produced a zero-assist outcome that the rule framework did not anticipate.

AST props for rim-anchored big men at T2 carry a specific structural risk: assists require sustained offensive possessions late in games. When a player like Wembanyama logs only 16 minutes — nearly half his season average — assist accumulation is mechanically capped regardless of per-minute rate. The system currently penalizes AST blowout risk by -10% confidence but does not apply a minimum-minutes floor check specific to AST props (unlike the PTS min_floor rule). A parallel AST floor check should be considered.

The SAS floor compression pattern (mean miss margin −5.0 on opponent picks vs SAS) may also manifest internally for SAS starters in games where the lead triggers early rest: Wembanyama at 16 minutes is consistent with a managed-exit scenario in a comfortable win, similar to how blowout logic reduces counting stats. The system's blowout threshold (≥20 margin) did not fire here (margin = 13), but a 13-point win with a 30-minute player logging 16 minutes suggests a managed-rest protocol that sits just below the formal blowout trigger.
recommendations: Add an AST minimum-minutes floor check parallel to the existing PTS min_floor rule: for any player whose season-average minutes are ≥28, require that their documented L10 minimum minutes exceed a threshold (e.g., 18 min) before selecting an AST prop. Wembanyama's 16-minute game causing a 1-AST output illustrates that AST accumulation is mechanically impossible in severely reduced minutes even at T2.

For Wembanyama specifically, flag any AST pick in games where SA is favored by 7+ points with a conditional warning: his average minutes drop materially in comfortable wins, and his AST output is highly sensitive to Q4 rest. Consider requiring either (a) a 9+/10 AST hit rate in blowout-context games specifically, or (b) stepping to T1 rather than T2 when spread_abs ≥7 and no iron_floor is present on the AST line.

Extend the BLOWOUT_RISK confidence penalty trigger to games where spread_abs ≥7 (from ≥8) for AST props: the current ≥8 threshold missed the SAS game (spread was 8.5, which triggered the penalty) — but the actual mechanism was minutes management in a moderately lopsided game, not a formal blowout. The net -10% applied was insufficient given the realized outcome. Consider a 12-15% penalty for AST props in any game where the player's team is favored by 7+ and the player is not iron_floor on AST.

When a player's post-game minutes are reported at less than 60% of their season average (Wembanyama: 16 min vs 30.1 avg = 53%), treat this as a data point for the managed-rest risk model. Pre-game, the system has no direct signal for this, but the SAS blowout-adjacent pattern (13-point wins triggering early rest) is now a documented second occurrence — consider adding a SAS-specific managed-rest flag for Wembanyama AST props when SA spread ≥6.

The CJ McCollum PTS pick (human verdict: trim, VOLATILE tag, FG_MARGIN_THIN step T15→T10) hit comfortably (actual 17 vs pick 10) in a CLOSE game (NY won by 3). Audit confirms the trim was procedurally correct given uncertainty, but the 7-point cushion at T10 validated the step-down logic — do not second-guess FG_MARGIN_THIN cascades when the resulting tier pick is this conservative. The trim flag on human review is appropriate; the model rule is working as intended.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0 — GAME CONTEXT: SA defeated PHI 115–102, a COMPETITIVE margin of 13. Not a formal blowout (≥20), so blowout game-script reasoning does not fully apply, but a 13-point win is a comfortable enough margin that managed rest is plausible. Post-game news confirms Wembanyama logged only 16 minutes — approximately 53% of his 30.1 season average — which is unusually low for a COMPETITIVE game. STEP 1 — ACTIVITY: actual_value = 1.0 AST; player clearly played (16 min confirmed). Non-zero activity confirms no DNP or injury exit. STEP 2 — CLASSIFICATION: No injury_exit in POST-GAME NEWS; injury_status_at_check = NOT_LISTED; voided = false. The pick threshold is T2 (≥2 AST), and actual output was 1 — a single-assist miss. Given the 9/10 historical hit rate (85% overall), a 1-AST outcome in 16 minutes is within the tail of the distribution — the rate implies roughly 1-in-10 games miss this threshold. The immediate cause is dramatically reduced minutes (16 vs ~30 avg) in a comfortable win, which capped assist accumulation mechanically. However, the spread_abs = 8.5 was already present at pick time, the blowout -10% penalty was applied, and the final confidence was set at 74% explicitly acknowledging this risk. The pick was made with full awareness of blowout-adjacent risk and was priced accordingly. Classification: variance — the 1-in-10 miss rate materialized, amplified by minutes compression in a comfortable win that sat just below the formal blowout threshold. STEP 3 — REASONING CRITIQUE: The original reasoning ('85% AST hit rate; soft PHI defense; T2 provides wide buffer; blowout -10% still leaves comfortable margin') correctly identified the favorable hit rate and soft defense but slightly underweighted the interaction between reduced minutes and AST accumulation — assists require sustained possessions, and at 16 minutes Wembanyama had limited opportunity regardless of per-minute rate. The tier_walk correctly noted T2 cannot step lower and applied the -10% penalty instead. STEP 4 — HIT RATE: 9/10 (85%) is a strong baseline; this is a legitimate tail outcome, not a systematic error. STEP 5 — TIER WALK: T2 is the minimum AST tier; no lower tier was available. The walk confirms the analyst exhausted downside options — the -10% confidence penalty was the only available mitigation. Tier selection was not in error. STEP 6 — AMENDMENT STATUS: No lineup_update sub-object present; pick was not amended by the afternoon agent. No amendment commentary required.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 3/3 sweep — the McCollum AST anchor delivered 6 assists vs a pick of 2, Jokic went supernova with 35 PTS vs 15, and Castle hit 19 PTS vs 10 in a comfortable SA win.
parlay_lessons: The structural risk on this parlay was the Jokic AST trim flag and BLOWOUT concern on the DEN leg: had the parlay included Jokic AST (T8, blowout risk, tough opp) instead of Jokic PTS (T15, stepped-down, large cushion), a 13-assist actual would have saved it — but the PTS leg was the safer choice. The lesson is that multi-game parlays anchored by step-down PTS legs (FG_MARGIN_THIN T20→T15) are more robust than parlays anchored by high-tier AST legs subject to blowout minutes compression, as confirmed by the standalone Wembanyama AST miss this same session.

The confidence product of 0.4922 (~49%) at +103 implied odds represents a near-breakeven expected value proposition — the parlay hit but the structural edge was thin. Future parlay construction should target confidence products above 0.55 when implied odds are at or below +120, or accept +150 or better odds when the product falls below 0.50, to ensure positive EV on the combination rather than relying on all legs outperforming their individual pick values as they did today.
parlay_reinforcements: The anchor + two-scorer structure (one 90% iron-floor AST anchor paired with two independent 74% stepped-down PTS legs from different games) produced a resilient combination: the McCollum 10/10 AST leg at T2 provided near-certainty on one leg while the two PTS legs each had generous step-down buffers (Jokic T15 actual 35, Castle T10 actual 19), confirming that pairing a highest-confidence iron-floor AST anchor with conservative-tier PTS legs in independent games is a structurally sound multi-game parlay template.

Cross-game independence was the key structural advantage: all three legs came from different games (ATL vs NY, DEN vs POR, SA vs PHI), eliminating same-game correlation risk. When combining players from games with different pace profiles and opponent defensive ratings, the independence assumption holds cleanly — this combination type should be prioritized over same-game stacks when building parlays with mixed prop types.

reinforcements: Iron-floor PTS picks proved structurally sound again: Jaylen Brown (35 PTS vs T15 pick) and Scottie Barnes (25 PTS vs T10 pick) both hit by wide margins, validating that iron-floor classification is a reliable protective tag even when facing tough defense or FG_COLD signals — the tier itself is the floor, not the average.

Multi-stat player stacks on the same team hit cleanly: all three RJ Barrett TOR picks (PTS, AST, 3PM) were graded at pick time, and PTS + AST both hit — confirming that stacking a player's lower-tier props across prop types on a soft-defense opponent remains a high-value pattern. The sole 3PM miss was variance, not a structural stacking error.

The IND amplifier context performed exactly as documented: Julius Randle hit 19 PTS vs T10 (pick_value 10) and SGA hit 8 AST vs T4 (pick_value 4) against Indiana, with both clearing threshold comfortably despite blowout game scripts — reinforcing that the confirmed IND amplifier boosts prop floors even when game script compresses minutes.

AST props for primary playmakers hit 100% across five picks (Tatum 4, Kawhi 3, Barrett 2, Barnes 5, SGA 8), validating that T2/T4 AST picks for established creation hubs on soft or mid defenses are the system's most reliable prop type this session — all five were at or below each player's raw average, confirming conservative tier selection is the right approach for AST.

Afternoon lineup amendments correctly identified upside on two picks that hit: Kawhi REB (Washington OUT → revised to 82%, actual 4 REB exact hit) and SGA AST (LeBron OUT → revised to 77%, actual 8 AST) — the amendment pipeline's ability to capture same-day scratches is adding legitimate directional signal, particularly for REB props where frontcourt matchup changes are most impactful.
lessons: 3PM props remain the system's weakest prop type (66.7% this session, sole miss) and the only miss of the entire session was a non-iron-floor 3PM: RJ Barrett went 0-for on 3PM despite a 9/10 hit rate and a soft MIA defense. The 80% cap on non-iron-floor 3PM picks reflects real variance that a single game can produce, and the blowout game script (TOR won by 26) likely compressed Barrett's late-game shot attempts — a blowout win can reduce a secondary scorer's volume in the fourth quarter even if minutes are modest (31 min logged). The system correctly applied the non-iron-floor 3PM cap but should note that blowout game scripts are an independent 3PM suppressor even on T1 picks, since catch-and-shoot opportunities decline when the offense goes into ball-control mode.

The human_verdict='manual_skip' on SGA AST (spread_abs=17.5) was validated by game script: SGA played only 28 minutes in a 36-point blowout and still hit 8 AST — the pick hit, but the skip reasoning was sound. This is a feature calibration note: at spread_abs≥17.5 with a primary playmaker, the blowout AST cap at 74% may still be too permissive as a selection gate, but the actual outcome (8 AST in 28 min) suggests primary playmakers' assist rates are robust even in heavily managed minutes. The skip was defensible given structural uncertainty, but the AST outcome confirms SGA's assist floor is genuinely durable — future audit should track whether manual_skip picks at 74% would have hit at a rate consistent with their confidence level.

Two human_verdict='trim' picks (Kawhi REB at iron_floor T4 and RJ Barrett PTS at T15) both hit, including Kawhi's exact-threshold REB hit (actual 4.0 = pick_value 4) — confirming that the trim reasoning around load-management and min_floor fragility, while structurally valid, may be over-penalizing iron-floor picks. The iron_floor tag is specifically designed to indicate blowout and rest resilience; future trim decisions on iron_floor picks with spread_abs<13 should require stronger evidence of actual minutes restriction risk rather than theoretical load management.
recommendations: Apply a dedicated blowout-script 3PM penalty: when a player's team is favored by ≥15 points AND the player is not a primary ball-handler (i.e., not the team's leading scorer or AST leader), apply an additional -5% confidence reduction to any 3PM pick beyond the existing non-iron-floor cap. The RJ Barrett 3PM miss illustrates this gap — a 26-point TOR blowout win naturally reduces catch-and-shoot opportunities for secondary scorers in the fourth quarter, a game-script effect the current T1 capping rule does not fully price in.

Recalibrate trim thresholds for iron_floor REB picks specifically: Kawhi Leonard's REB hit exactly at threshold (4.0) despite a trim recommendation citing min_floor fragility. Iron_floor picks on REB props have demonstrated consistent blowout resilience across multiple sessions — the trim gate for iron_floor REB should require min_floor < 22 (not < 25) before flagging fragility, and spread_abs should exceed 14 (not 11) before a trim is warranted on an iron_floor prop.

Continue tracking the SGA manual_skip outcome pattern: SGA AST hit 8 (pick_value 4) in only 28 minutes during a 36-point blowout — a primary playmaker's AST floor is structurally more durable than the blowout penalty rule implies. Flag for future rule refinement: for players whose raw_avgs AST ≥ 5.0 AND who are the team's primary ball-handler, the blowout AST cap at 74% for spread_abs≥15 may be set 4–5pp too conservatively. Consider a primary-playmaker AST blowout floor of 78% rather than 74% for top-tier playmakers (≥5 APG average).

The multi-stat TOR stack (Barrett PTS + AST + 3PM, Barnes PTS + REB + AST) produced 5/6 hits across six picks against soft MIA defense. For future same-team multi-prop stacks vs. confirmed soft defenses, prioritize PTS and AST over 3PM as the anchor legs — the 3PM pick was the sole miss and carries the highest per-game variance. When constructing 3+ prop stacks on a single player, cap at one 3PM leg and ensure the other legs are PTS or AST with iron_floor or ≥9/10 hit rates.

Validate the amendment pipeline's REB accuracy specifically: the Kawhi REB amendment (Washington OUT → revised up to 82%) correctly identified frontcourt competition removal as an upside signal, and the pick hit at threshold. This is a repeatable structural pattern — when a starting big man is scratched from the opposing team pre-game, REB props for the opposing team's primary frontcourt scorer should receive a systematic upward amendment trigger. Confirm this rule is already encoded in the afternoon lineup agent; if not, add an explicit rule: 'opposing PF/C scratch → REB picks for matching frontcourt player revised +3–5pp confidence.'

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0 — Game context: TOR def. MIA 121–95, a 26-point BLOWOUT win for TOR. Barrett played 31 minutes (post-game news confirmed), with minutes reduction attributed to game flow in a comfortable victory. STEP 1 — Barrett was active; he scored 16 PTS (AST hit 2.0) but recorded 0 three-pointers attempted or made. He was not injured or restricted — the 0 3PM reflects a shot-attempt distribution outcome, not a DNP. STEP 2 — No injury_exit in post-game news. injury_status_at_check = NOT_LISTED. Classification: variance. A 9/10 hit rate at T1 means one miss per ten games is statistically expected; this is that miss. The blowout game script (TOR +26) likely compressed fourth-quarter shot volume and reduced catch-and-shoot opportunities as TOR went into ball-control mode — a game-script suppressor that the current rule set does not apply a formal penalty for on 3PM picks. However, the system correctly priced the non-iron-floor 3PM cap at 80%, acknowledging the inherent variance. The 9/10 hit rate and soft MIA defense supported the pick logically; the outcome was within the expected miss distribution. STEP 3 — Analyst reasoning ('90% T1 hit rate with 6-game streak vs soft MIA defense; capped at 80% per non-iron-floor 3PM rule') was accurate and appropriately conservative. The reasoning did not flag blowout game-script as a 3PM suppressor, which is a model gap to note but not a selection error at the pick's confidence level. STEP 4 — 9/10 hit rate; a miss on this pick is expected ~10% of the time. STEP 5 — Tier walk shows T1 at 90% overall with no lower tier evaluated (T1 is the minimum). No tier_skip_error. STEP 6 — No lineup_update sub-object present; no amendment was triggered for this pick.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 3/3 sweep on the Tuesday Volume Sweeper — all three legs hit comfortably, anchored by Jaylen Brown's 35-point explosion and supported by Durant's exact-threshold REB hit and Barrett's 16 PTS clearing the T15 floor.
parlay_lessons: The Durant REB leg hit exactly at threshold (actual 4.0 = pick_value 4) — a razor-thin exact-match hit that would have been the parlay's vulnerability. In future parlay construction, when one leg is a VOLATILE-tagged player at minimum tier (T4 REB), the structural risk of an exact-threshold result is high enough that this leg type should be treated as the weakest link. Consider whether exact-threshold REB picks on VOLATILE players should be replaced with a lower-tier alternate line or swapped for a non-VOLATILE player's REB prop to add cushion to the parlay's reliability.
parlay_reinforcements: The multi-game independent correlation structure (three different teams, three different opponents, zero shared game exposure) proved ideal: no single game script could have collapsed more than one leg simultaneously. When building volume parlays, prioritizing three fully independent games over same-game or same-team stacks is the correct structural approach — the Tuesday Volume Sweeper format validated this architecture cleanly.

Anchoring the parlay with a PTS leg from an iron-floor scorer (Jaylen Brown, iron_floor=true, 9/10 hit rate, trend=up) provided the most structurally reliable anchor leg type in the system — Brown hit for 35 points against a tough CHA defense, more than doubling the pick threshold. Future parlays should always include at least one iron-floor PTS leg as the primary anchor when the pick qualifies.

reinforcements: AST props were 5/5 on the day — iron-floor and near-iron T2/T4 AST picks (Bane T2, Fox T4, SGA T4, Kawhi T2, Johnson T4) all converted cleanly, confirming that low-threshold assist floors remain the system's most reliable output; the MIN AST suppressor concern at T2 iron-floor level was correctly neutralized.

3PM picks were 3/3 (Harden T1, Bane T1, SGA T1) — the trend=down step-down rule for Harden and the non-iron-floor 80% cap both fired correctly, and the conservative T1 floors held across all three shooters including a B2B road game for SGA; the MIN×3PM amplifier also contributed to Bane's clean hit at exactly threshold (1 actual = 1 pick).

The FG_MARGIN_THIN mandatory step-down rule proved structurally sound on Bane PTS (T15→T10, actual 18) and Harden PTS (T15→T10, actual 21) — both picks scored well above the stepped threshold, validating that the conservative tier is the correct place to anchor rather than fighting the thin-margin signal.

The lineup update agent correctly flagged upside on Fox PTS (revised to 86%), Bane PTS (revised to 87%), and SGA 3PM (revised to 83%) when key opponents were scratched — all three hit comfortably, confirming that opponent-side absences (Harper, Edwards, Gobert, Garland) are meaningful input to confidence revision and the agent is calibrated well on direction.

The blowout cap rule at spread_abs≥22.5 for Jokic PTS (capped at 74%) correctly identified the highest-risk pick on the slate; even though it ultimately missed by 1 point, the system's 70-75% band underperforming (50% actual) confirms the cap is appropriately conservative and the 74% confidence accurately reflected structural uncertainty.
lessons: Jalen Johnson PTS T15 missed (actual 12, needed 15) in a CLOSE game (CLE won by 6) — the miss was not explained by blowout game script. Johnson played 34 minutes with no injury flags, logged a normal game, and still fell 3 points short of the T15 threshold despite a 9/10 hit rate and stable trend. This is a variance miss on a sound pick, but the pattern of PTS being the session's weakest prop type (66.7%) reinforces that T15 PTS for secondary scorers in road close games carries meaningful within-game volatility even at high hit rates.

Nikola Jokic PTS T15 missed (actual 14, needed 15) — confirmed minutes restriction (31 min, sat Q4) driven by Denver's blowout win margin (DEN won by 17, classified COMPETITIVE but the pre-game spread was 22.5 points). Post-game news confirms Jokic was pulled from the fourth quarter with the game decided, producing exactly the compression the blowout cap rule anticipated. The FG_MARGIN_THIN step-down (T20→T15) and 74% blowout cap both fired correctly, but the pick still ran and narrowly missed by 1 point; the iron_floor tag at T20 did not protect T15 from garbage-time minute reduction on a historically large spread.
recommendations: For Jokic (and any elite center) on spreads ≥20 points, consider whether a further step-down to T10 is warranted rather than stopping at T15 after FG_MARGIN_THIN fires — the T15 blowout-capped pick missed by 1 point after a confirmed Q4 sit; T10 would have been a clean hit. The iron_floor tag provides false comfort on blowout games when minutes are structurally at risk: iron_floor reflects historical floor under normal minute distributions, not blowout-truncated games.

PTS is the session's weakest prop type at 66.7% (2 misses both PTS) — when PTS picks involve either (a) a secondary scorer on a team with a spread ≥20 or (b) a road scorer in a CLOSE game with trend=stable and no iron_floor, require the hit rate at the selected tier to be 10/10 or carry an explicit iron_floor tag before inclusion in parlays; single-leg standalone PTS picks at 9/10 in these contexts are acceptable but should not be parlay anchors.

The 70-75% confidence band is performing at 50% actual vs. 72.5% expected — both picks in this band were Jokic PTS (74%) and SGA PTS (74%), both subject to blowout cap rules. Consider treating blowout-capped PTS picks (confidence capped at 74% due to spread_abs≥12) as FADE or NEUTRAL bet recommendations and avoiding their inclusion in any parlay leg regardless of individual pick soundness; the structural ceiling on these picks reflects genuine uncertainty that compounds in multi-leg combinations.

The SGA PTS pick (T20, 74%, actual exactly 20) was a threshold-exact HIT graded correctly per the HIT rule (actual_value ≥ pick_value). However, post-game news confirms SGA also sat Q4 with OKC leading by 25+ — he hit 20 points in approximately 30 minutes before being rested. This near-miss pattern on blowout games for elite scorers who are pulled early reinforces that the VOLATILE tag plus spread_abs>15 on a B2B road game should trigger an automatic skip of PTS props for that player regardless of iron_floor status; the iron_floor protected the hit here by 0 points.

The lineup update agent's upside revisions (Fox to 86%, Bane to 87%, SGA 3PM to 83%) all hit comfortably — when the afternoon agent triggers an upside revision of ≥5pp due to opponent-side multi-player absences (Gobert+Edwards+Randle all out, Garland out), the Analyst should consider whether the confidence revision warrants upgrading the pick to a top_pick designation or including it in a parlay anchor role, as these opponent-depleted games produced the session's largest margin hits.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0 — Game context: ATL @ CLE, CLE won 122-116, margin 6 points — CLOSE game. No blowout game-script factor applies. STEP 1 — Johnson played 34 minutes with no injury flags (post-game news: inferred, normal game context). Actual 12 PTS is a non-trivial miss of 3 points below threshold. STEP 2 — No injury exit, no workflow gap, no blowout. Classification: variance. STEP 3 — Original reasoning ('9/10 at T15 in competitive game with full minutes and 2d rest') was structurally sound — full minutes were indeed logged (34), game was competitive (CLOSE), rest was confirmed. The reasoning correctly identified no suppressors. The miss reflects Johnson's natural scoring variance in road close-game environments where shot distribution can tighten. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 9/10 (90% implied), meaning this is a 1-in-10 miss event; no systematic failure. Trend was stable. STEP 5 — Tier walk shows T20 had no data ≥70%, T15 was the only viable tier at 9/10 (85% base). No tier_skip_error — this was the appropriate and only qualifying tier. The walk was complete. STEP 6 — No lineup_update sub-object present; no afternoon amendment was triggered for this pick.

STEP 0 — Game context: MEM @ DEN, DEN won 136-119, margin 17 points — classified COMPETITIVE, but the pre-game spread was 22.5 points (one of the session's largest). The actual margin of 17 is below the ≥20 BLOWOUT label, yet the post-game news confirms Jokic was held out of the fourth quarter as a rest/maintenance decision after Denver built a comfortable lead — a blowout-adjacent game script that produced minutes restriction even at a 17-point final margin. STEP 1 — Jokic played 31 minutes (confirmed via post-game news). Actual 14 PTS is 1 point below the T15 threshold. He recorded non-trivial output (scored 14), so this is a genuine production miss, not an injury or DNP. STEP 2 — Post-game news: MINUTES_RESTRICTION confirmed — 'held out of the fourth quarter as the Nuggets cruised to a comfortable win, a common rest/maintenance decision.' No injury exit. Classification: model_gap_rule. The pre-game spread of 22.5 correctly triggered the T25 hard skip and the large-spread PTS cap at 74%, and FG_MARGIN_THIN fired the T20→T15 step-down — all rules fired correctly. However, the miss reveals a rule gap: the blowout_secondary_scorer skip and large-spread PTS cap rules fire on percentage confidence adjustments but do not hard-stop the pick when spread_abs≥20 AND FG_MARGIN_THIN is also present, even for non-secondary scorers. The compound signal (extreme spread + thin FG margin + confirmed Q4 rest pattern for DEN stars late in season) was present in the data but no rule mandates a skip or a further step to T10 in this combination. A tighter rule requiring T10 rather than T15 when spread_abs≥20 AND FG_MARGIN_THIN fires would have produced a clean hit (14 actual ≥ 10 threshold). STEP 3 — Original reasoning ('FG_MARGIN_THIN steps T20→T15; iron_floor holds; blowout cap at 74% for spread_abs=22.5') correctly identified all risk factors but did not conclude that the pick should be skipped or stepped further. The iron_floor at T20 was used as a confidence anchor for T15, but iron_floor reflects historical floors under normal minute distributions — not Q4 blowout management where Jokic routinely sits. STEP 4 — Hit rate 9/10 at T20 level (iron_floor); the miss at T15 by 1 point reflects the Q4-sit compression more than statistical variance. STEP 5 — Tier walk correctly shows T25 as hard skip (spread≥15), T20 as qualifying tier (iron_floor), step to T15 via FG_MARGIN_THIN. The walk was appropriately executed but the final selected tier (T15) was still too aggressive given the combined risk signals. No tier_skip_error — the issue is the rule not mandating a second step under these conditions. STEP 6 — No lineup_update sub-object present for Jokic; no afternoon amendment was triggered.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 0/1 — the Jalen Johnson PTS leg was the single failure point; both SGA 3PM and Kawhi AST hit cleanly, making the parlay a near-miss driven entirely by Johnson's variance miss in a CLOSE road game.
parlay_lessons: Do not use a non-iron-floor PTS leg as a parlay anchor when the player is a road secondary scorer in a projected CLOSE game — Johnson's PTS T15 was the highest-risk leg of the three and the one that sank the parlay; the 9/10 hit rate at T15 sounds robust but road close-game scoring variance for non-primary scorers is the system's most common PTS miss pattern, and a parlay requires all legs to hit simultaneously.

When constructing a multi-game parlay with an OKC leg and a LAC leg in the same OKC vs. LAC game, be aware that both legs (SGA 3PM and Kawhi AST) are from the same game even though labeled 'independent' — if OKC wins in a blowout (which they did, winning by 18), SGA benefits from looser coverage while Kawhi faces reduced fourth-quarter minutes; these game-script effects are partially correlated even across teams, and the parlay correctly identified both as hits, but this structural dependency should be documented explicitly rather than labeled 'independent.'

For parlays where one leg carries a confirmed 'trim' human verdict (Johnson AST was trimmed, Kawhi AST was trimmed, Bane PTS was trimmed), avoid combining any trimmed-verdict leg with legs from different games unless all remaining legs are iron-floor — the trim designation signals known downside risk that is amplified in a parlay context where a single miss voids the entire bet.
parlay_reinforcements: The SGA 3PM + Kawhi AST same-game pairing from the OKC-LAC matchup both hit cleanly (SGA 2 actual vs 1 needed; Kawhi 3 actual vs 2 needed) — stacking a trend=up 3PM leg for the winning team's star with a low-threshold iron-adjacent AST leg for the home team's primary wing is a viable same-game combination structure when the spread is in the 5-8 point range and both players have documented B2B resilience; this pairing should be considered a reusable template for competitive spread games featuring an elite road scorer versus a home wing with a deep assist floor.

The T1 3PM floor as a parlay leg continues to perform well — Harden T1, Bane T1, and SGA T1 all hit on the session (3/3 combined across all picks), confirming that non-iron-floor 3PM at T1 with ≥9/10 hit rates and trend=up or trend=stable is a reliable low-variance parlay building block when capped at 80% confidence and used as a complementary leg rather than an anchor.

reinforcements: Tatum AST T4 at 78% (revised to 84% post-amendment) was the session's standout structural call: the lineup agent correctly identified Jaylen Brown's absence as a meaningful upside trigger for Tatum's creation role, and he delivered 8 assists (double the threshold). When a clear primary creator is removed from a playoff-race team, the remaining star's assist volume is a high-confidence elevation target — this pattern held cleanly.

3PM picks at T2 with consistent tags and upward trends continued their perfect run: both Tatum (2 actual) and Anunoby (3 actual) cleared their thresholds. The 80%/75% confidence caps on non-iron-floor 3PM picks are appropriately conservative — both hit, and the cap prevented overconfidence on a volatile prop type.

Scottie Barnes iron-floor REB T4 at 78% delivered an exact-threshold result (4 actual = 4 pick_value) — confirming that the T4 minimum-tier exception and iron-floor tag together create a structurally sound floor even against trend=down pressure. The exact hit validates the grading rule: exact threshold = HIT.

The COMPETITIVE game script in TOR vs MIA (TOR won by 14) supported full-game starters staying on the floor for Barnes, validating the no-blowout-risk read on a 4.5-point spread. Both Barnes PTS (13) and REB (4) cleared their thresholds, consistent with the competitive game context assessment.
lessons: Scottie Barnes AST T4 missed badly (2 actual vs. 4 pick_value) despite a VOLATILE tag, soft opponent, and trend=up — the pick sat at exactly 74% confidence with a market-implied 91.67% against it. The massive edge_pct disparity (-17.67%) was a red flag that the market strongly disagreed with this line, and the FADE recommendation from the bet engine should have been treated as a systemic caution signal: when the market prices a prop at 91.67% implied and the system rates it 74%, the market is pricing in a near-certainty that should prompt extra scrutiny rather than a contrarian pick.

OG Anunoby PTS T10 at 70% missed (13 actual vs. 15 pick_value) in a CLOSE game (NY won by 6). The original tier_walk noted FG_MARGIN_THIN forcing a step down from T15 to T10 — the step-down was executed correctly — but the vs_tough=53% split at T15 was a documented warning that PTS vs BOS tough defense was structurally fragile. At 70% confidence with VOLATILE tag, a vs_tough rate that was only 53% at the tier above, and the human trim recommendation citing this exact fragility, this pick was at the system's minimum qualifying floor with limited margin. The trim recommendation was correct and should have been honored: human-trimmed VOLATILE picks at exactly 70% confidence with documented vs_tough suppression should not survive to the card.
recommendations: Implement a hard rule: when bet_recommendation.recommendation_tier = 'FADE' AND the market-implied probability exceeds the system's confidence_pct by 15+ percentage points (as with Barnes AST, -17.67% edge), the pick should be automatically excluded unless it carries an iron_floor tag. The market's near-certainty pricing in the opposite direction is a stronger signal than any single hit-rate data point, and the current system allows FADE-rated picks to survive to the card unchecked.

For VOLATILE-tagged AST picks at exactly 70-74% confidence, add a mandatory vs_tough gate: if vs_tough rate at the selected tier is below 60% AND the market-implied probability exceeds the system confidence by more than 10pp, do not emit the pick. Both Barnes AST (74%, vs_soft=79% but market at 91.67%) and Anunoby PTS (70%, vs_tough=53%) show that the 70-75% band is where structural misses concentrate — the 50% actual hit rate in this band this session is a clear pattern.

Codify the Jaylen Brown absence → Tatum AST uplift as a template rule: when a team's leading scorer (28+ PPG) is confirmed OUT pre-game for a playoff-race team and the remaining star has a consistent AST tag with trend=up, the lineup agent's upward amendment should elevate the pick to iron_floor consideration if the revised confidence reaches 84%+. The amendment correctly identified this and the pick hit at 2x the threshold — this is a repeatable signal.

The human trim recommendations for both Anunoby PTS (VOLATILE + vs_tough=53%) and Pritchard PTS (minutes fragility + tough defense) were directionally correct — Anunoby missed, and Pritchard's 23 points hit comfortably but with high single-game variance risk. Establish a protocol: when human_verdict = 'trim' AND confidence_pct ≤ 75 AND at least one of (VOLATILE tag, vs_tough < 60%, road underdog), the pick should be excluded from the active card rather than retained at a trimmed status. Trim-but-keep at marginal confidence concentrates exactly the kind of picks that underperform.

For Barnes-type multi-prop stacks on the same player, add a correlation risk note: when a player has both an AST pick at VOLATILE/74% and a REB pick at iron_floor/78% in the same game, the AST pick's failure mode (low ball-handling game, off-script role) is uncorrelated with the REB pick's floor. This is structurally fine for the REB pick, but the AST pick's weakness is masked by the REB iron-floor confidence. Consider requiring a higher confidence floor (78%+) for any VOLATILE AST pick that appears alongside an iron-floor prop on the same player in the same game.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0: MIA @ TOR final 114-128, TOR won by 14 — COMPETITIVE game. No blowout; Barnes played 25 minutes (confirmed), consistent with a controlled home win. Minutes were somewhat limited at 25 (min_floor was 25.7), which is near the floor and may have modestly compressed his assist opportunities. STEP 1: Player active, 25 minutes played, 13 PTS, 4 REB recorded — AST was the sole miss at 2 actual vs. 4 pick_value. Full miss, not a near-miss. STEP 2: No injury exit in POST-GAME NEWS. injury_status_at_check = NOT_LISTED, voided = false. Classification: variance — the VOLATILE tag was applied and the 74% confidence already reflected the realistic miss probability. However, the magnitude of the miss (2 actual vs. 4 threshold) is notable. STEP 3: The analyst's reasoning cited soft MIA defense, VOLATILE -5% from 80% base, and trend=up. The VOLATILE tag was correctly applied. The analyst also noted that opponent absences (Rozier, Jovic OUT for MIA) could not boost confidence per VOLATILE rules — this discipline was correct. What the reasoning did not adequately weight: the market priced this at 91.67% implied probability, a 17.67 percentage-point gap against the system's 74%. This level of market disagreement is a structural signal that the line was set at a far lower threshold than the market expected Barnes to clear. STEP 4: Hit rate was 8/10 (80%) at the base T4 tier before VOLATILE penalty — a 2-in-10 miss probability materialized. STEP 5: The tier walk correctly identified T4 as the only qualifying tier; the AST T4+ hard gate did not fire for SF. No tier_skip or data conflict. STEP 6: No amendment direction change — pick remained at 74%. The bet engine rated this FADE (-6.77% edge) with market at 91.67%, and kelly_quarter = 0.0 — the system's own bet recommendation correctly identified this as a pick not to wager on. The core classification is variance at a documented VOLATILE tier, but the combination of FADE recommendation, massive market disagreement, and a 74% minimum-band confidence makes this a candidate for a future rule that excludes FADE-rated AST picks at ≤75% confidence regardless of hit rate.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 1/1 sweep — the 'Celtic Duo + Barnes' parlay hit on Tatum AST (8 assists, 2x threshold) and Barnes PTS (13, well above T10), with the Jaylen Brown leg voided pre-game, reducing the parlay to a 2-leg hit.
parlay_lessons: The 'Celtic Duo + Barnes' parlay had a structural fragility that was realized but didn't cost the result: Jaylen Brown (PTS leg, 80% confidence, labeled 'scorer') was confirmed OUT pre-game, reducing a 3-leg parlay to a 2-leg parlay. The Tatum AST leg was explicitly correlated with Brown's role as primary scorer — Brown's absence was the causal factor that boosted Tatum's AST. When a parlay contains a scorer/feeder correlation pair on the same team and the scorer DNPs, the feeder leg becomes an absence-driven elevation rather than a correlated structure. Future parlays should include a contingency note: if the anchor scorer DNPs, the feeder leg's confidence inflates artificially from the same event that voids the scorer leg, and the parlay's structural logic has changed. Consider voiding both legs of a same-team scorer/feeder pair when the scorer is confirmed OUT, rather than allowing the feeder leg to ride on an absence-driven boost.
parlay_reinforcements: The scorer/feeder correlation structure (Brown PTS + Tatum AST on the same BOS team) is a sound combination type when both legs are active: the scorer's volume creates assist opportunities for the feeder, and a competitive game against a tough opponent (NY, CLOSE game) keeps both players on the floor for full minutes. When the scorer leg is unavoidably voided, the feeder leg still hit because the underlying logic (Tatum as primary creator in Brown's absence) was even more valid — confirming that same-team feeder AST picks anchored to a star's creation role are structurally resilient even under personnel disruption.

Including an independent leg from a different game (Barnes PTS vs MIA) as the third parlay component successfully diversified game-script risk: the TOR-MIA and BOS-NY games were independent outcomes, and Barnes PTS hitting in a COMPETITIVE TOR win provided a clean non-correlated anchor. Future parlays should maintain at least one independent-game leg to protect against single-game script failures.

reinforcements: Low-tier PTS floors (T10) held across all game scripts: Wembanyama (40), Fox (10 AST anchored PTS floor), Durant (33), Sengun (22), Barnes (15), Ingram (16), KAT (22), LaMelo (27), Kawhi (24) — the system's conservative tier-walk cascade (FG_MARGIN_THIN and min_floor step-downs) correctly de-risked these props while still producing qualifying picks, confirming that walking down to T10 when signals fire is structurally sound.

Iron-floor AST T2 picks (Desmond Bane, VJ Edgecombe, Scottie Barnes REB, Kawhi REB) all hit despite blowout or B2B conditions, validating that iron-floor tags should anchor confidence even when game-script risk is present — the iron-floor exception correctly blocked the B2B road gate from firing on Edgecombe and correctly held Bane AST through a 24-point ORL blowout win.

Blowout cap at 74% (spread ≥12) was correctly applied to Fox AST, Wembanyama AST, Pritchard AST, and Bane AST — all hit comfortably, confirming the cap is conservative but not over-restrictive for these prop types; the cap prevents overconfidence without creating false skips.

The lineup amendment pipeline correctly identified upside on Barnes PTS/REB (RJ Barrett OUT), Sengun PTS, and Amen Thompson REB via Randle/Reid absences — all three revised-up picks hit decisively, validating the afternoon update agent's role for frontcourt scoring and rebounding props when key opponents are missing.

REB T4 iron-floor picks (Kawhi 8 REB vs T4, Barnes exact-hit at 4) were the most reliable prop type on the slate at 100% — the system's conservative REB tier selection and iron-floor gate continue to outperform all other prop types, supporting continued prioritization of T4 REB iron-floor picks in future slates.
lessons: Desmond Bane PTS T10 (ORL -14.5 blowout): The blowout_secondary_scorer hard skip threshold of ≥15 spread did not fire at spread_abs=14.5 — Bane played only 17 minutes and scored 2 points. The 0.5-point gap below the hard-skip threshold allowed a pick that produced a severe miss (actual=2, 8 points below threshold). The rule boundary at exactly 15.0 is too rigid; a blowout cap with secondary-scorer compression risk at spread_abs ≥14 for non-iron-floor secondary scorers should be considered, or the hard-skip threshold should be lowered to 14.

Brandon Miller AST T2 (0 actual assists, iron-floor 95% [consistent]): Even iron-floor picks with [consistent] tags can produce zero-assist games — Miller scored 22 points but recorded 0 assists in 31 minutes against a tough DET defense. The trend=down signal was present and the vs_tough=82% reflected some risk, but the iron-floor tag suppressed any further penalty. This is variance against a confirmed iron-floor, but the CLE switching-scheme-style DET tough defense may be suppressing AST creation specifically; the system should evaluate whether DET has an AST-specific suppression pattern analogous to MIN×AST before treating DET as a neutral tough defense for AST props.

Nickeil Alexander-Walker AST T2 (1 actual assist vs CLE): ATL won by 22 (blowout), and NAW played 32 minutes and scored 18 points — yet recorded only 1 assist. The blowout game script on the winning side did not compress NAW's minutes but did compress the game's assist-generation sequences (ATL comfortable lead reduced late-game playmaking urgency). The miss reflects a known weakness: AST props on winning blowout teams are exposed to creation-sequence collapse even with full minutes, especially for non-primary ball-handlers. The system correctly applied BLOWOUT cap at 74% but trend=down should have triggered additional scrutiny for a non-primary creator in a potential blowout. Classification: model_gap_rule — the winning-side blowout AST suppression signal existed (spread=7.5 was not a blowout pre-game, but ATL won by 22) and the post-game context confirms the mechanism.

Kon Knueppel AST T2 (1 actual assist vs DET): Knueppel played 29 minutes and scored 10 points but posted only 1 assist, missing the T2 threshold. DET won by 18 (COMPETITIVE per game label, margin 10-19). The tough DET defense (vs_tough=77% for Knueppel AST) materialized as suppression — DET's defensive structure limited secondary creators' assist opportunities even in a competitive game. The FG_COLD:-10% flag on his PTS prop was informational but indicated a cold-shooting context that typically compresses playmaking sequences for non-primary creators. The AST T2 pick was sound given 8/10 hit rate and 3-day rest, but the combination of tough defense + secondary creator + FG_COLD on PTS should have warranted a harder scrutiny check. Classification: variance — hit rate was 8/10 (80%), the pick was structurally sound, and this is within the 20% miss band.
recommendations: Lower the blowout_secondary_scorer hard-skip threshold from spread_abs ≥15 to spread_abs ≥13.5 for non-iron-floor secondary scorers on PTS props. The Bane miss (actual=2, spread=14.5) demonstrates that a 0.5-point buffer below a rigid threshold creates exactly the blowout minute-compression risk the rule was designed to prevent. At 74% confidence (blowout cap), the pick had marginal edge — a tighter hard-skip threshold eliminates the worst-case outcomes for secondary scorers in near-blowout spreads.

Begin tracking DET as a potential AST suppressor candidate. Both Knueppel AST T2 (1 assist) and Brandon Miller AST T2 (0 assists) missed on the same slate against DET (n=2, 0% AST hit rate this game). While this is a single-game sample, DET's defensive profile under Bickerstaff appears to compress secondary-creator assist sequences in the same way MIN's switching scheme does. Flag DET×AST for a watch item and apply extra scrutiny (prefer T2 iron-floor only, or require 9+/10 hit rate) until at least 8 AST picks vs DET are graded.

For AST props on winning-side blowout teams: when the pre-game spread is 5-10 points (competitive pre-game) but the team wins by 20+, non-primary creators' assist totals are compressed by creation-sequence collapse even with full minutes. NAW (1 assist in 32 min, ATL +22 blowout win) is the prototype case. The system should apply a post-hoc caution: if a player is a non-primary creator (best_tier AST ≤ T2, raw_avgs AST < 4.0) and is on a team that was not a heavy pre-game favorite, their AST pick is more vulnerable to winning-side blowout script than the pre-game spread suggests. Consider a winning-blowout-script secondary-creator penalty of -5% when spread_abs < 10 but game develops into a 20+ margin.

Validate the afternoon lineup amendment agent's upside-revision accuracy: on this slate, all three revised-up picks (Barnes PTS/REB after Barrett OUT, Sengun PTS and Thompson REB after Randle/Reid OUT) hit decisively. The agent correctly identified frontcourt scoring and rebounding upside when key opposing bigs were absent. Reinforce this pattern — future slates should explicitly prioritize interior scoring (PTS T10+) and REB picks vs. teams missing multiple frontcourt players, as the signal is confirmed reliable across this sample.

For iron-floor AST T2 picks with trend=down on non-primary creators against tough defenses: consider adding a rule that when (a) player is non-PG or non-primary ball-handler, (b) trend=down on AST, and (c) opp_defense=tough, the iron-floor tag should not fully suppress the trend=down penalty — apply -5% to the base before the iron-floor cap is set. Brandon Miller (iron_floor, consistent, 95% overall, trend=down, vs_tough=82%) produced 0 assists; the iron-floor blocked all downside signals. A modified rule: iron-floor protects the tier selection but allows a -5% confidence deduction for trend=down on non-primary creators vs tough defenses.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0 — Game: ORL @ CHI, ORL won 127-103 (blowout, +24). Bane's team won by 24 points — confirmed blowout winning side. Post-game news confirms Bane played only 17 minutes due to the blowout, producing just 2 points. STEP 1 — Player was active (17 min played, 2 PTS). STEP 2 — The blowout_secondary_scorer hard-skip rule requires spread_abs ≥15; Bane's pre-game spread was 14.5, exactly 0.5 below the threshold, so the hard skip did not fire. The actual game margin (24 points) vastly exceeded the pre-game spread, producing the exact minute-compression risk the rule was designed to prevent. The signal was present — blowout risk was visible in the pre-game spread — but the rule's hard threshold at 15.0 was too rigid. FG_MARGIN_THIN:4% had already forced a step-down from T15 to T10, signaling scoring fragility. STEP 3 — The original reasoning ('FG_MARGIN_THIN steps T15 to T10; soft CHI defense; blowout cap 74%; H2H 3/3 confirms floor') correctly identified the FG_MARGIN fragility and applied the blowout cap, but did not account for the 14.5-spread secondary scorer compression risk being functionally equivalent to the ≥15 hard-skip zone for a non-iron-floor player. STEP 4 — Hit rate was 8/10 (80% overall) but the blowout context is the mechanism that collapsed the floor. STEP 5 — Tier walk correctly stepped T15→T10 via FG_MARGIN. At T10 the pick was sound on history alone, but the game-script risk was not adequately priced. STEP 6 — Amendment flagged upside (revised to 78%) after CHI starters OUT, which increased confidence in a pick that ultimately missed badly — the amendment identified the wrong directional risk. Classification: model_gap_rule — the blowout secondary-scorer hard-skip threshold at ≥15 is too permissive; a 0.5-point gap below the threshold allowed a structurally risky pick to survive.

STEP 0 — Game: DET @ CHA, DET won 118-100 (COMPETITIVE, margin 18). The game was in the competitive range (margin 10-19), so full game-script blowout reasoning does not apply, though DET's late-game margin may have compressed CHA's starters slightly. Post-game news: Knueppel played 29 minutes with no external flags. STEP 1 — Player was active (29 min, 10 PTS confirmed, 1 AST). STEP 2 — No injury event. The pick was structurally sound: AST T2 at 85% overall, 8/10 hit rate, 3-day rest. The miss is within the 20% miss band for an 80% confidence pick. DET's tough defense (vs_tough=77%) materialized as AST suppression for a non-primary creator. STEP 3 — Original reasoning ('AST T2 at 85%; vs_tough=77%; well-rested; 3-day rest; competitive play-in context') correctly identified the tough defense caveat but still proceeded at 80% confidence. The FG_COLD:-10% flag on the PTS prop was informational and not applied to the AST prop — in retrospect, a cold-shooting context for a secondary creator tends to compress assist sequences as well. STEP 4 — Hit rate 8/10 (80%) — this is a 1-in-5 miss scenario; outcome is within expected variance. STEP 5 — Tier walk correctly identified T4 as a hard skip (raw_avgs AST < 4.0, opp=tough), walking down to T2 as the only qualifying tier — confirming this was the best available pick and not a selection error. STEP 6 — No amendment on this pick. Classification: variance — 8/10 hit rate with sound tier selection; DET tough defense compressed AST production for a non-primary creator, but the pick was defensible and within normal variance range.

STEP 0 — Game: DET @ CHA, DET won 118-100 (COMPETITIVE, margin 18). Post-game news: Miller played 31 minutes and scored 22 points — he was fully active and healthy. STEP 1 — Player was active (31 min, 22 PTS, 0 AST confirmed). STEP 2 — No injury event. The iron-floor tag at 95% [consistent] with a 9/10 hit rate represents an exceptionally strong qualifier, and a zero-assist game against this profile is a genuine outlier. STEP 3 — Original reasoning ('Iron-floor AST T2 at 95% [consistent]; 3-day rest; vs_tough=82%') correctly applied all available signals. The trend=down flag was noted but iron-floor rules correctly suppressed a mandatory tier step — however, the combined signal of trend=down + non-primary creator + tough DET defense + competitive game script was not penalized beyond the stated conservatism. Miller scored 22 points (well above his PTS tier) but channeled all production into scoring rather than playmaking, a pattern that can occur when secondary scorers are operating in isolation-heavy shot-creation mode against aggressive defenses. STEP 4 — Hit rate 9/10 (90%) overall; this 0-assist outcome is a severe miss (2 units below threshold) but within the 10% miss band for a 90%-calibrated prop. The consistent tag historically means near-zero variance, making this a notable single-game deviation. STEP 5 — Tier walk correctly hard-skipped T4 (raw_avgs AST < 4.0, opp=tough) and selected T2 as the only qualifying tier. STEP 6 — No amendment on this pick. Classification: variance — iron-floor 9/10 hit rate with [consistent] tag; zero-assist outcome is a legitimate outlier within the 10% miss band; DET tough defense and scoring-first game mode for Miller concentrated production in PTS rather than AST. Recommend monitoring whether DET has a recurring AST suppression effect on secondary creators (now 0/2 AST hits on this slate vs DET).

STEP 0 — Game: CLE @ ATL, ATL won 124-102 (BLOWOUT, +22). ATL was the winning side of a 22-point blowout. Post-game news: NAW played 32 minutes and scored 18 points — full minutes, no injury, but only 1 assist. STEP 1 — Player was active (32 min, 18 PTS, 1 AST). STEP 2 — No injury. The blowout mechanism here is winning-side creation-sequence collapse: with a large lead secured, ATL's offensive possessions in Q4 shifted toward isolation scoring and clock management rather than ball-movement and creation, collapsing assist generation for non-primary creators even with full minutes. NAW is a secondary creator (raw_avgs AST < 4.0, best_tier=T2). STEP 3 — Original reasoning ('Iron-floor AST T2 at 85%; vs_tough=88%; CLE depleted; trend=down noted but iron_floor holds tier') correctly noted trend=down but over-relied on the iron-floor tag to hold the pick. The pre-game spread was CLE @ ATL with spread_abs=7.5 — a competitive spread, not a blowout pre-game. The blowout cap at 74% did not apply (spread_abs=7.5 < 12), so no game-script penalty was applied. The actual 22-point blowout was not predictable from the pre-game spread, meaning the winning-side AST suppression signal was not available at pick time — but the underlying mechanism (non-primary creator AST collapses on winning blowout teams even with full minutes) is a systemic gap in the model's blowout handling. STEP 4 — Hit rate 8/10 (80%) with iron_floor tag; the miss is within the 20% band statistically, but the mechanism (winning-side blowout AST compression for non-primary creators) is recurring. STEP 5 — Tier walk correctly hard-skipped T4 (raw_avgs AST < 4.0, opp=mid) and selected T2 as the only qualifying tier. STEP 6 — No amendment on this pick. Classification: model_gap_rule — the pre-game spread (7.5) did not trigger any blowout cap, but the 22-point actual margin produced winning-side AST suppression that the current blowout rules (keyed on pre-game spread ≥12) cannot capture. A rule improvement: for non-primary-creator AST T2 picks, apply a post-game or intra-game adjustment mechanism, or more conservatively require iron-floor + 9+/10 hit rate for non-primary AST creators when the opposing team is being rested (CLE depleted) and the home team has strong motivation and talent advantage — the 'depleted opponent' signal that boosted this pick's confidence was actually the same signal that created blowout risk.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 0/1 — the Friday Edge Trio collapsed on two CHA AST legs: Miller (0 assists) and Knueppel (1 assist) both failed against DET's tough defense, each missing by the minimum margin, while the anchor Ingram AST leg hit comfortably (6 assists).
parlay_lessons: Stacking two AST legs from the same team (CHA) against the same opponent (DET) created hidden correlation: both Miller and Knueppel were non-primary creators on the same side of the same game, meaning a single defensive scheme (DET tough defense suppressing secondary-creator playmaking) could — and did — knock out both legs simultaneously. Multi-leg parlays should not include two non-primary-creator AST props from the same team-game, as a single game-script or defensive-scheme event produces correlated failure rather than independent variance.

AST T2 picks on non-primary creators (raw_avgs AST < 4.0) should not anchor parlay legs without iron-floor status on at least one of the two picks; Knueppel had no iron-floor tag and Miller's iron-floor did not protect against a zero-assist outcome in this context — in future parlays, require that any same-team AST leg pair includes at least one iron-floor pick with a 9+/10 hit rate to provide structural support.
parlay_reinforcements: The Ingram AST T2 leg (6 actual assists, hit by +4) validated the trend=up + B2B rate 92% combination as a reliable parlay anchor for secondary-creator AST when the player's role is clearly defined (Ingram as TOR's #2 creator with Barrett OUT) — trend=up on AST with a high B2B rate and confirmed starter role is a structurally strong parlay leg type regardless of opponent.

Cross-game multi-leg parlays (multi_game type, independent correlation roles) remain the correct structural approach — the failure here was not the parlay type but the concentration of two non-primary AST legs in a single game; future multi-game parlays should ensure no more than one AST leg per game, distributing game-script risk across independent matchups.

reinforcements: AST and REB props went a combined 10/10 — the system's non-PTS prop selection was flawless today. AST floors for primary ball-handlers (Fox, LaMelo, LeBron) and REB floors for rebounding anchors (Barnes, Adebayo) held even in blowout games, validating the iron-floor tier logic for these prop types.

The T10 Floor Exception for PTS worked as designed: Barnes, Ingram, Quickley, Knueppel, Miller, Banchero, and Maxey all cleared their T10 thresholds cleanly. The exception correctly prevented secondary scorer hard-skips from blocking picks that proved sound, with the single PTS miss (Quickley) attributable to minutes management rather than a scoring failure in normal run.

Lineup intelligence added real value: the ATL mass-absence update (Okongwu, Johnson, McCollum, Daniels, Alexander-Walker all OUT) correctly amplified confidence on Adebayo's REB and AST T2, both of which hit. Tracking opponent absence clusters as positive matchup signals is validated.

Blowout-resilient tags with iron_floor (Barnes PTS/REB, Adebayo REB) consistently held through end-of-season garbage-time conditions — all three hit despite massive spreads (23.5 and 26-point wins). The resilient offset rule (-5% net vs -10% standard) correctly kept these picks viable.

Trend=up picks on AST (LaMelo, LeBron, Bam) went 3/3 in blowout and near-blowout conditions, reinforcing that primary creator AST floors are the most durable prop type on the slate, especially when opp_defense is soft or opponent absences are confirmed.
lessons: PTS props in regular-season finales carry structural minutes-compression risk that the system does not fully price. Four of the five misses today (Quickley, Bridges, Cunningham, Bane PTS) involved players whose minutes were actively managed down — Quickley to 17 min, Bridges to 24 min, Cunningham to 22 min, Bane to 18 min — all in games where the outcome was settled or where playoff health was the priority. The system applied blowout caps but the MINUTES MANAGEMENT dimension of end-of-season games is distinct from in-season blowout garbage time and is not currently modeled as a separate suppressor.

Secondary scorer PTS T10 passes the floor exception but does not escape the underlying risk: Quickley (4 actual vs T10), Bane PTS (8 actual vs T10), and Bridges (7 actual vs T10) all missed as secondary scorers in games with large spreads or minute-management contexts. The T10 floor exception prevents the hard skip from firing but does not raise the underlying probability — when multiple structural risks compound (secondary scorer + season finale + blowout spread), the system's 74% cap may still be too permissive.

The VOLATILE tag combined with post-season load management produced the Cunningham miss: played only 22 minutes in an 81-63 halftime blowout. The VOLATILE deduction and blowout-resilient offset landed at 72%, which was sound analytically, but the season-finale playoff-rest dynamic (DET leading by 18+ at half, explicitly managing Cade for playoff health) was a structural miss factor. When a team has clinched seeding and faces a weaker opponent with a large pre-game spread on the final day of the regular season, an additional minutes-management risk flag should fire for star starters.

Desmond Bane's PTS T10 miss repeated the exact pattern documented in prior audit history — secondary scorer in an ORL blowout-risk game, pulled early with limited minutes (18 minutes in a game ORL lost 113-108, which was actually closer than expected). The human verdict was manual_skip, confirming the Analyst already identified this risk. This is the second consecutive documented Bane blowout PTS miss. The system should now hard-skip Bane PTS at any spread ≥ 10 when ORL is favored, not just at the T25+ volatile_weak_combo threshold — the Bane secondary scorer blowout pattern is confirmed across multiple instances.
recommendations: Add a SEASON_FINALE_MINUTES_RISK flag: on the final day of the regular season (April 12, 2026 and equivalent dates in future seasons), apply an additional -5% confidence deduction to ALL PTS picks for players on teams with seeding locked AND a pre-game spread ≥ 10. This is distinct from standard blowout risk — it reflects deliberate organizational health management for the postseason. Today's misses (Bridges, Cunningham, Bane) fit this profile. Note: Quickley's miss was reclassified to injury_event (hamstring exit Q2) and is not a season-finale minutes-management case.

Implement a BANE_BLOWOUT_HARD_SKIP rule: skip Desmond Bane PTS at any tier when ORL spread_abs ≥ 10 and ORL is the favorite, regardless of whether the T25+ volatile_weak_combo fires. The pattern has now missed twice in consecutive documented instances. His 3PM T1 (iron-floor protected) remains valid — only PTS should be skipped under this condition.

For Cade Cunningham PTS on DET games where DET spread_abs ≥ 12 AND DET has already clinched their playoff seed: apply a hard cap of 70% regardless of VOLATILE/blowout-resilient calculations. Cunningham's 22-minute game confirmed that the Pistons will aggressively rest him in comfortable wins when playoffs are ahead — this is a roster-management pattern that overrides his individual statistical floor.

Immanuel Quickley PTS T10 miss on 4/12 was reclassified to injury_event — he exited with right hamstring tightness in Q2 and did not return. This was not a blowout/minutes-management miss. His blowout resilience rate (100%) remains valid for non-injury contexts. Monitor hamstring status for TOR vs CLE R1 availability.

When a player has trend=down AND the game is on the final day of the regular season AND their team's spread_abs ≥ 10: do not select PTS props at any tier. The trend=down signal combined with playoff health management creates a compounding risk that the system's individual penalty rules (-5% VOLATILE, blowout cap) do not fully capture as a combined suppressor.

miss_details.root_cause: STEP 0: TOR @ BKN final 136-101, TOR won by 35 — BLOWOUT. STEP 1: Quickley scored 4 actual points in 17 minutes. STEP 2: Classified injury_event. Quickley exited the game with right hamstring tightness with 17 seconds left in the second quarter and did not return. This was a mid-game injury departure, not season-finale minutes management. The post-game reporter failed to surface this injury (ESPN fetch failed, Brave Search not yet implemented), causing the auditor to incorrectly attribute the miss to blowout/load-management dynamics. The injury follows an 8-game absence earlier in April for plantar fasciitis on the same right leg. MRI scheduled as of April 13 — availability for TOR vs CLE R1 unknown. STEP 3: Original pick reasoning was sound on its own terms (9/10 T10 hit rate, blowout resilience); the miss was caused by an unforeseeable mid-game injury, not a rule gap. STEP 4: Reclassified from model_gap_rule to injury_event after external confirmation of the hamstring exit (Brave Search, April 13).

STEP 0: CHA @ NYK final 110-96, CHA won by 14 — COMPETITIVE (margin 10-19). However, POST-GAME NEWS confirms Bridges played only 24 minutes — below typical starter load — in a regular-season finale where CHA was managing players for the play-in tournament. STEP 1: Bridges scored 7 actual against a T10 threshold — a miss by 3 points in reduced minutes. STEP 2: Classified model_gap_rule. The game margin was technically COMPETITIVE (14 points), but the season-finale minutes-management context suppressed his output. The system's vs_tough=76% (14pp below overall) was cited correctly in the pick, but the additional season-finale load-management risk was not captured. STEP 3: The original reasoning correctly noted 'T10 exception clears secondary scorer skip' and the FG_HOT:+18% as informational, but did not address the end-of-season load management risk. The human verdict was 'trim' with specific concern about post_miss_hr=57% and vs_tough suppression — those concerns were directionally correct but attributed to the wrong mechanism (NYK defense vs. minutes management). STEP 4: Hit rate 9/10 is strong, confirming this is a rule gap not a weak-pick miss. STEP 5: Tier walk appropriately landed at T10 after secondary scorer blowout assessment. STEP 6: Amendment unchanged (Shamet absence irrelevant to Bridges PTS) — no directional flag was available from the amendment system for this miss.

STEP 0: ORL @ BOS final 108-113, BOS won by 5 — CLOSE game (not a blowout). STEP 1: Bane scored 8 actual points in only 18 minutes played. STEP 2: Classified model_gap_rule. The 18-minute restriction in a close game is consistent with season-finale organizational load management — Bane also rested the entire second half of the previous game vs CHI, confirming this was a deliberate multi-game minutes taper ahead of the play-in tournament, not a tactical mid-game decision. The system's blowout-risk framework was oriented toward ORL winning big (spread_abs=13.5, ORL favored), but the actual risk was organizational rest regardless of game flow. STEP 3: The original reasoning correctly stepped T15→T10 via FG_MARGIN_THIN and applied the T10 exception. The human verdict was manual_skip, confirming the risk identification — the Analyst spotted the structural Bane PTS pattern but the automated pipeline did not enforce it. STEP 4: This is the same season-finale minutes-compression pattern seen in Bridges (24 min) and Cunningham (22 min) on this slate. Hit rate 8/10 (80%) — the pick was analytically reasonable but the minutes-management dimension is a rule gap the system does not currently model.

STEP 0: PHI @ MIL final 126-106, PHI won by 20 — BLOWOUT. STEP 1: Edgecombe scored 9 actual points — a miss by exactly 1 point (pick_value=10, actual=9) in 31 minutes played. This is a near-threshold miss in a game where he played a full rotation. Note per grading rules: exact threshold matches are HITs — but 9 < 10 so this is a genuine miss by 1 unit. STEP 2: Classified variance. Edgecombe scored 9 in 31 minutes — he was active, played substantial time, and was simply 1 point short of the threshold. The PHI suppressor note (confirmed system-wide suppressor) is relevant here as PHI was the HOME team — this pick was for Edgecombe ON PHI vs MIL, not an opponent of PHI. The PHI suppressor applies to players FACING PHI's defense, not to PHI's own players. No suppressor applies to Edgecombe himself in this direction. STEP 3: The original reasoning correctly applied the T10 floor exception for secondary scorers and noted blowout_games=76% (not strongly blowout resilient). The reasoning was sound — 85% T10 with blowout penalty to 75% capped at 74% is appropriate. A 1-point miss after a full 31-minute game is within normal statistical variance. STEP 4: Hit rate 8/10 (80%) is solid. The miss is a single-unit shortfall, not a structural collapse. STEP 5: Tier walk correctly selected T10 after secondary scorer assessment. STEP 6: No amendment directional note needed (direction not present as a down flag).

STEP 0: DET @ IND final 133-121, DET won by 12 — COMPETITIVE. However, POST-GAME NEWS confirms Cunningham played only 22 minutes — explicitly inferred as minutes restriction due to DET leading 81-63 at halftime, with playoffs ahead. The reported halftime score (81-63, +18) means the game was a practical blowout by halftime even though the final margin was 12. STEP 1: Cunningham scored 7 actual points in 22 minutes — a miss by 3 points against T10. STEP 2: Classified model_gap_rule. The pick object shows VOLATILE tag, trend=down, and the system correctly stepped T15→T10. The pick was at 72% confidence — the lowest of all picks on the slate — reflecting genuine uncertainty. However, the fundamental missed signal was DET's explicit decision to rest Cunningham for playoff health once the game was well in hand at halftime. The system's blowout_risk framework operates on pre-game spread (14.5), not on in-game halftime score dynamics. The 81-63 halftime score created an in-game blowout trigger that the pre-game spread of 14.5 only partially anticipated. STEP 3: The original reasoning correctly noted the rest risk ('DET at 59-22 may rest Cade') as a scout note but proceeded because Cade was NOT_LISTED in the injury report. The reasoning was appropriately cautious (72%) but the season-finale playoff-health management pattern was not a hard deterrent. STEP 4: Hit rate 7/10 (70%) is the minimum qualifying floor — this was already the system's most marginal PTS pick of the session. A miss on a 7/10 pick is expected 30% of the time. STEP 5: Tier walk appropriately cascaded T15→T10 after VOLATILE merit failure (T15 at 70% fell below 70% after VOLATILE -5% deduction). The T10 selection was the only viable option after the cascade. STEP 6: Amendment flagged upside (revised to 77% with Jarace Walker out) but pick missed. The amendment incorrectly assessed Walker's absence as a meaningful positive without accounting for the season-finale Cunningham rest risk that was explicitly noted in the pick reasoning itself.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 0/1 — the Sunday Edge Trio fell on the Quickley PTS leg, which collapsed to 4 actual points in just 17 minutes of season-finale garbage-time management, despite two strong anchor legs hitting (Adebayo REB 10/8 ✓, Bane 3PM 1/1 ✓).
parlay_lessons: Never include a secondary-scorer PTS leg in a parlay on the final day of the regular season when the team has already clinched seeding and faces a large spread (≥ 20 points). Quickley's 100% blowout PTS rate was based on in-season data where starters play meaningful minutes — a season-finale rout with playoffs secured is categorically different and produces aggressive starter rest that the blowout-resilience tag does not protect against. The parlay rationale correctly cited Quickley's edge but did not account for the season-finale minutes-management risk that made this leg structurally fragile regardless of its standalone hit rate.

When two parlay legs are confirmed solid (iron-floor REB + near-iron-floor 3PM) but the third leg carries a known volatility risk (trend=down, secondary scorer, season-finale context), the combination is not saved by the anchor legs — the weakest structural leg determines the parlay outcome. Future parlay construction should require that PTS legs in season-finale games pass an additional season-finale minutes-safety check before inclusion, or default to AST/REB/3PM legs exclusively on the final day of the regular season.
parlay_reinforcements: The two non-PTS legs (Adebayo REB T8 and Bane 3PM T1) both hit cleanly and represent the correct anchor structure for a parlay — a rebounding-anchor leg with iron-floor protection paired with a near-iron-floor 3PM leg. This combination type (REB + 3PM across different games with independent correlation) is exactly the structural backbone that parlays should be built around, with PTS legs used only as the third leg when they carry top-tier confidence and no structural risk flags.

The independent multi-game correlation structure of this parlay (MIA vs ATL, ORL vs BOS, TOR vs BKN — three separate games with no shared players or defensive scheme overlap) was correctly designed. The failure was pick selection within the structure, not the correlation architecture. Continue building multi-game parlays with legs drawn from fully independent games to eliminate correlated collapse risk.

reinforcements: Play-in max-minutes context was the single most reliable amplifier on the slate: LaMelo Ball (40 min, 30 PTS), Miles Bridges (43 min, 28 PTS), Tyler Herro (34 min, 23 PTS), and Brandon Miller (37 min, 23 PTS) all cleared their thresholds comfortably — the play-in minutes elevation thesis was correct and should be applied confidently in future play-in games.

Iron-floor AST picks held even against a tough opponent: Brandon Miller AST T2 (iron_floor) hit 5 assists in 37 minutes, and Devin Booker AST T4 (iron_floor) hit exactly 4 assists at the threshold — confirming that iron_floor designation on AST props is a structurally reliable anchor, particularly when the player is a primary playmaker in a high-stakes game.

The FG_COLD flag on Knueppel PTS T10 was correctly identified as a meaningful structural risk by the human_verdict trim review, and the flag proved predictive: Knueppel went 2-for-12 FG, 0-for-6 3PT — a severe cold-shooting outcome. The system should treat FG_COLD as a stronger negative signal on secondary-scorer PTS props even at T10, not merely informational.
